<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAEL508 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS AND SIMULATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Familiarization of Operational Amplifiers: Inverting and Non-Inverting Amplifiers, Integrator, Differentiator - Frequency Response, Adder, Comparators

# Module Experiment 2: Familiarization of Operational Amplifiers

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Operational Amplifier (Op-Amp) — The Heart of Analog Computation

An **Operational Amplifier (Op-Amp)** is a high-gain, directly coupled, DC electronic voltage amplifier with a differential input and a single-ended output, typically packaged as an integrated circuit (IC). The name "operational" originates from its original use in performing mathematical operations such as addition, subtraction, integration, and differentiation in analog computers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> An operational amplifier is a DC-coupled, high-gain electronic voltage amplifier with differential inputs (V⁺, V⁻) and a single-ended output. The ideal op-amp has **infinite open-loop gain (A_OL)**, **infinite input impedance (Z_in)**, **zero output impedance (Z_out)**, **infinite bandwidth**, and **zero offset**.

**Standard 741 Op-Amp Pinout (8-Pin DIP):**
- Pin 1 → Offset Null
- Pin 2 → Inverting Input (V⁻)
- Pin 3 → Non-Inverting Input (V⁺)
- Pin 4 → V⁻⁻ (−V_CC, typically −12 V)
- Pin 5 → Offset Null
- Pin 6 → Output (V_out)
- Pin 7 → V⁺⁺ (+V_CC, typically +12 V)
- Pin 8 → NC

### 1.2 Two Golden Rules of Ideal Op-Amp Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> These two rules are the *backbone* of every op-amp problem the KTU examiner will set. Master them first.

1. **Rule of Virtual Short:** When negative feedback is present, the voltage at the inverting input equals the voltage at the non-inverting input:
$$V^{-} = V^{+}$$

2. **Rule of Infinite Input Impedance:** No current flows into either op-amp input terminal:
$$I^{-} = I^{+} = 0 \text{ A}$$

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Slide" Intuition

Imagine an op-amp as a **smart water slide** in an amusement park:
- The two input terminals are two *water-pressure sensors* at the top of the slide.
- The output is the *water flow* at the bottom.
- The op-amp constantly adjusts the output to *minimize the difference* between the two sensor readings — this is **negative feedback**.
- Because the slide is super slippery (infinite gain), even a tiny pressure imbalance produces a huge flow correction at the output.
- The two *golden rules* tell us: (i) the two sensors always read the same pressure (virtual short), and (ii) no water leaks into the sensors themselves (infinite input impedance).

This "balance-keeping" behavior is precisely why op-amps can be wired as amplifiers, adders, integrators, and differentiators with extraordinary precision.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Inverting Amplifier — Circuit Operation Logic

**Logic Steps (Why this works):**
- The input signal V_in is applied to the inverting terminal via resistor R_1.
- The non-inverting terminal is grounded (V⁺ = 0).
- Feedback resistor R_f connects output back to the inverting input.
- Because of **virtual short**, V⁻ = V⁺ = 0 V → this point is called **Virtual Ground**.
- Current through R_1: I_in = (V_in − V⁻)/R_1 = V_in/R_1
- This same current flows through R_f (since I⁻ = 0): I_in = (V⁻ − V_out)/R_f = −V_out/R_f
- Equating gives the closed-loop gain.

### 2.2 Non-Inverting Amplifier — Circuit Operation Logic

**Logic Steps:**
- V_in is applied directly to the non-inverting terminal (+).
- The inverting terminal is grounded through R_1, and R_f provides feedback.
- V⁻ = V⁺ = V_in (virtual short).
- Voltage divider: V⁻ = V_out × R_1/(R_1 + R_f)
- Equating and solving yields closed-loop gain.

### 2.3 Adder (Summing Amplifier) — Why Multiple Inputs?

Extends the inverting amplifier by accepting N input branches through separate resistors R_1, R_2, …, R_N at the virtual-ground node. By KCL at the virtual ground, the output is the weighted negative sum of all inputs — the foundational building block of **Digital-to-Analog Converters (DACs)** and audio mixers.

### 2.4 Integrator and Differentiator — Frequency-Domain Perspective

In the **frequency domain**, the capacitor's impedance is:

$$Z_C = \frac{1}{j\omega C}$$

This frequency-dependent behavior is what transforms the inverting amplifier (resistive feedback) into an **integrator** (capacitive feedback) or **differentiator** (capacitive input).

### 2.5 Frequency Response — Bode Plot Concepts

- **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW):** A constant for any op-amp. For μA741, GBW ≈ **1 MHz**.
- **Cut-off Frequency (f_c):** Frequency at which the closed-loop gain drops by **−3 dB** (i.e., to 70.7% of its DC value).
- **Slew Rate (SR):** Maximum rate of change of output voltage. For μA741, SR = **0.5 V/μs**.

### 2.6 Comparators — Open-Loop Operation

A comparator uses the op-amp in **open-loop** (no feedback). Since open-loop gain is enormous (~10⁵), even a tiny input difference drives the output to **saturation**:

$$V_{out} = +V_{sat} \quad \text{if } V^{+} > V^{-}$$
$$V_{out} = -V_{sat} \quad \text{if } V^{+} < V^{-}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)**

| # | Configuration | Closed-Loop Gain (A_CL) | Output Equation | Key Constraint |
|---|---------------|--------------------------|------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Inverting Amplifier | $A_{CL} = -R_f / R_1$ | $V_{out} = -\left(\dfrac{R_f}{R_1}\right) V_{in}$ | Input impedance = R_1 |
| 2 | Non-Inverting Amplifier | $A_{CL} = 1 + R_f / R_1$ | $V_{out} = \left(1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}\right) V_{in}$ | Input impedance → ∞ |
| 3 | Voltage Follower | $A_{CL} = 1$ | $V_{out} = V_{in}$ | Unity-gain buffer |
| 4 | Summing (Adder, N inputs) | — | $V_{out} = -R_f \sum_{k=1}^{N} \dfrac{V_k}{R_k}$ | All inputs inverted |
| 5 | Integrator (Ideal) | $A(j\omega) = -\dfrac{1}{j\omega R_1 C_f}$ | $V_{out}(t) = -\dfrac{1}{R_1 C_f} \displaystyle\int V_{in}(t)\,dt$ | Valid for f ≫ f_a |
| 6 | Differentiator (Ideal) | $A(j\omega) = -j\omega R_f C_1$ | $V_{out}(t) = -R_f C_1 \dfrac{d V_{in}(t)}{dt}$ | Valid for f ≪ f_b |
| 7 | Comparator (Open-loop) | $A_{OL} \to \infty$ | $V_{out} = \pm V_{sat}$ | Output is binary |

| # | Parameter | Formula | Typical 741 Value | Unit |
|---|-----------|---------|---------------------|------|
| 1 | Gain-Bandwidth Product | $GBW = A_{CL} \cdot f_c$ | $1 \times 10^{6}$ | Hz |
| 2 | Cut-off Frequency | $f_c = \dfrac{GBW}{A_{CL}}$ | depends on A_CL | Hz |
| 3 | Slew Rate | $SR = \dfrac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta t}$ | $0.5$ | V/μs |
| 4 | Maximum Useful Frequency | $f_{max} = \dfrac{SR}{2\pi V_{peak}}$ | — | Hz |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical:** Never use the vertical pipe `\|x\|` inside tables — use $\vert x \vert$ instead to preserve markdown rendering.

### 2.7 Real-World Utility

- **Inverting/NON-Inverting Amplifiers:** Sensor signal conditioning, audio pre-amplifiers, instrumentation.
- **Adder:** Digital-to-Analog Converters (R-2R Ladder), audio mixers, beamforming.
- **Integrator:** Analog computers, ramp generators, active filters (low-pass), dual-slope ADCs.
- **Differentiator:** Edge detectors in pulse circuits, high-pass filters, FM demodulators.
- **Comparator:** Schmitt triggers, zero-crossing detectors, window comparators, ADC front-ends.
- **Frequency Response Analysis:** Determines stability, useful bandwidth, and noise performance of every analog system.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Inverting Amplifier Closed-Loop Gain

**Step 1: Define currents at the inverting node (Node A, virtual ground).**

By KCL at Node A:
$$I_1 + I_f = I^{-}$$

**Step 2: Apply the Golden Rule I⁻ = 0.**

$$I_1 + I_f = 0$$

**Step 3: Express I_1 using Ohm's Law.**

$$I_1 = \frac{V_{in} - V^{-}}{R_1}$$

Since V⁻ = V⁺ = 0 (non-inverting input is grounded):
$$I_1 = \frac{V_{in} - 0}{R_1} = \frac{V_{in}}{R_1}$$

**Step 4: Express I_f using Ohm's Law (flowing from Node A to V_out).**

$$I_f = \frac{V^{-} - V_{out}}{R_f} = \frac{0 - V_{out}}{R_f} = -\frac{V_{out}}{R_f}$$

**Step 5: Substitute back into KCL.**

$$\frac{V_{in}}{R_1} - \frac{V_{out}}{R_f} = 0$$

**Step 6: Solve for V_out / V_in.**

$$\frac{V_{out}}{R_f} = \frac{V_{in}}{R_1}$$

$$V_{out} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1} \cdot V_{in}$$

$$\boxed{A_{CL} = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1}}$$

The **negative sign** confirms phase inversion — the signature property of the inverting amplifier.

### 3.2 Derivation — Non-Inverting Amplifier Closed-Loop Gain

**Step 1: Apply virtual short.**

$$V^{-} = V^{+} = V_{in}$$

**Step 2: Identify the voltage divider across R_f and R_1.**

The current through R_1 (to ground) is:
$$I_1 = \frac{V^{-}}{R_1} = \frac{V_{in}}{R_1}$$

This same current flows through R_f (because I⁻ = 0):
$$I_f = I_1 = \frac{V_{in}}{R_1}$$

**Step 3: Express V_out via KVL around the feedback loop.**

$$V_{out} = V^{-} + I_f \cdot R_f$$

$$V_{out} = V_{in} + \left(\frac{V_{in}}{R_1}\right) \cdot R_f$$

$$V_{out} = V_{in}\left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right)$$

**Step 4: Closed-loop gain.**

$$\boxed{A_{CL} = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}}$$

The **positive sign** and the constant +1 confirm that V_out is always greater than V_in and in phase.

### 3.3 Derivation — Summing Amplifier (3-Input Adder)

**Step 1: KCL at the virtual-ground node (V⁻ = 0).**

$$\frac{V_1}{R_1} + \frac{V_2}{R_2} + \frac{V_3}{R_3} + \frac{V_{out}}{R_f} = 0$$

**Step 2: Solve for V_out.**

$$V_{out} = -R_f \left(\frac{V_1}{R_1} + \frac{V_2}{R_2} + \frac{V_3}{R_3}\right)$$

**Step 3: Special case — equal resistors (R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R).**

$$\boxed{V_{out} = -\frac{R_f}{R}(V_1 + V_2 + V_3)}$$

If additionally R_f = R, the result is the pure **unity-weighted inverting summer**:
$$V_{out} = -(V_1 + V_2 + V_3)$$

### 3.4 Derivation — Ideal Integrator Output Equation

**Step 1: Write current through input resistor.**

$$I_{in} = \frac{V_{in} - 0}{R_1} = \frac{V_{in}}{R_1}$$

**Step 2: Since I⁻ = 0, the entire I_in flows into the capacitor.**

$$I_C = I_{in}$$

**Step 3: Capacitor v-i relation.**

$$I_C = C_f \frac{d(V^{-} - V_{out})}{dt} = -C_f \frac{dV_{out}}{dt}$$

(The minus sign arises because V⁻ = 0 is constant.)

**Step 4: Equate and integrate.**

$$-C_f \frac{dV_{out}}{dt} = \frac{V_{in}}{R_1}$$

$$\frac{dV_{out}}{dt} = -\frac{V_{in}}{R_1 C_f}$$

**Step 5: Integrate both sides from 0 to t.**

$$\boxed{V_{out}(t) = -\frac{1}{R_1 C_f} \int_{0}^{t} V_{in}(\tau)\, d\tau + V_{out}(0)}$$

**Frequency-domain equivalent:**

$$A(j\omega) = \frac{V_{out}(j\omega)}{V_{in}(j\omega)} = -\frac{1}{j\omega R_1 C_f} = \frac{1}{\omega R_1 C_f}\angle(-90^\circ)$$

The **−90° phase shift** is the hallmark of integration.

### 3.5 Derivation — Ideal Differentiator Output Equation

**Step 1: Current through input capacitor.**

$$I_{in} = C_1 \frac{d(V_{in} - V^{-})}{dt} = C_1 \frac{dV_{in}}{dt}$$

(Since V⁻ = 0.)

**Step 2: This same current flows through R_f.**

$$\frac{V^{-} - V_{out}}{R_f} = -\frac{V_{out}}{R_f} = C_1 \frac{dV_{in}}{dt}$$

**Step 3: Solve for V_out.**

$$\boxed{V_{out}(t) = -R_f C_1 \frac{dV_{in}(t)}{dt}}$$

**Frequency-domain equivalent:**

$$A(j\omega) = -j\omega R_f C_1 = \omega R_f C_1 \angle(-90^\circ)$$

The **+20 dB/decade rising gain** is the signature of a differentiator (it amplifies high-frequency noise — a major practical drawback).

### 3.6 Derivation — Frequency Response Parameters

**Step 1: Recall GBW definition.**

$$GBW = A_{CL}(DC) \times f_c = \text{constant}$$

**Step 2: Solve for the −3 dB cut-off frequency.**

$$\boxed{f_c = \frac{GBW}{A_{CL}}}$$

**Step 3: Slew-rate-limited maximum frequency.**

The maximum frequency at which a sine wave of peak amplitude V_peak can be reproduced without slew-induced distortion:
$$\boxed{f_{max} = \frac{SR}{2\pi V_{peak}}}$$

**Numerical Worked Example:**
For 741 op-amp (SR = 0.5 V/μs = 0.5 × 10⁶ V/s) and V_peak = 5 V:

$$f_{max} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^{6}}{2\pi \times 5} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^{6}}{31.416} \approx 15.915 \text{ kHz}$$

Above ~15.9 kHz, the output becomes triangular (slew-limited) instead of sinusoidal.

### 3.7 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Inverting | Non-Inverting | Integrator | Differentiator | Adder | Comparator |
|----------|-----------|---------------|------------|----------------|-------|------------|
| Feedback Element | R_f (resistor) | R_f (resistor) | C_f (capacitor) | R_f (resistor) | R_f (resistor) | None (open-loop) |
| Input Element | R_1 (resistor) | Wire to V⁺ | R_1 (resistor) | C_1 (capacitor) | R_1, R_2, … (resistors) | Direct to inputs |
| Phase | 180° inverted | 0° in-phase | −90° | −90° | 180° inverted | Binary |
| Frequency Roll-off | Flat | Flat | −20 dB/dec | +20 dB/dec | Flat | — |
| Stability | Excellent | Excellent | Needs R‖C in practice | Poor (noisy) | Excellent | Hysteresis-prone |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Inverting Amplifier — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeVin["V_in Source"] --> nodeR1["R1 Input Resistor"]
    nodeR1 --> nodeInv["Inverting Node V_minus - Virtual Ground"]
    nodeGND["Ground 0V"] --> nodeNonInv["Non Inverting Input V_plus"]
    nodeRf["Rf Feedback Resistor"] --> nodeInv
    nodeInv --> nodeOpAmp["Op-Amp A_OL infinite"]
    nodeOpAmp --> nodeOut["V_out"]
    nodeOut --> nodeRf
    nodeOpAmp --> nodeGND2["DC Supply +-15V"]
    classDef active fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309,color:#000
    classDef passive fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#000
    class nodeInv,nodeOpAmp active
    class nodeR1,nodeRf passive
```

### 4.2 Non-Inverting Amplifier — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeVin["V_in Source"] --> nodeNonInv["Non Inverting Input V_plus"]
    nodeGND["Ground 0V"] --> nodeR1["R1 to Ground"]
    nodeR1 --> nodeInv["Inverting Node V_minus - Virtual Short"]
    nodeRf["Rf Feedback Resistor"] --> nodeInv
    nodeInv --> nodeOpAmp["Op-Amp A_OL infinite"]
    nodeOpAmp --> nodeOut["V_out"]
    nodeOut --> nodeRf
    classDef active fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,color:#000
    class nodeInv,nodeOpAmp,nodeNonInv active
```

### 4.3 Summing Amplifier (3-Input Adder) — Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeV1["V1"] --> nodeR1["R1"]
    nodeV2["V2"] --> nodeR2["R2"]
    nodeV3["V3"] --> nodeR3["R3"]
    nodeR1 --> nodeSumNode["Summing Node V_minus Virtual Ground"]
    nodeR2 --> nodeSumNode
    nodeR3 --> nodeSumNode
    nodeRf["Rf Feedback Resistor"] --> nodeSumNode
    nodeSumNode --> nodeOpAmp["Op-Amp"]
    nodeOpAmp --> nodeVout["V_out = -Rf times V1_R1 plus V2_R2 plus V3_R3"]
    nodeGND["Ground"] --> nodeNonInv["Non Inverting Input"]
    classDef active fill:#fbcfe8,stroke:#9d174d,color:#000
    class nodeSumNode,nodeOpAmp active
```

### 4.4 Integrator — Functional Architecture with Frequency Behavior

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeVin["V_in Source"] --> nodeR1["R1 Input Resistor"]
    nodeR1 --> nodeInv["Inverting Node Virtual Ground"]
    nodeCf["Cf Integration Capacitor"] --> nodeInv
    nodeInv --> nodeOpAmp["Op-Amp"]
    nodeOpAmp --> nodeOut["V_out is Integral of V_in"]
    nodeOut --> nodeCf
    nodeGND["Ground"] --> nodeNonInv["Non Inverting Input"]
    subgraph BODE["Frequency Response Behavior"]
        nodeLowF["Low Frequency HIGH GAIN 1 over omega R1 Cf"]
        nodeHighF["High Frequency LOW GAIN rolls off 20 dB per decade"]
        nodePhase["Phase Shift minus 90 degrees"]
    end
    classDef active fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,color:#000
    class nodeInv,nodeOpAmp,nodeOut active
```

### 4.5 Differentiator — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeVin["V_in Source"] --> nodeC1["C1 Input Capacitor"]
    nodeC1 --> nodeInv["Inverting Node Virtual Ground"]
    nodeRf["Rf Feedback Resistor"] --> nodeInv
    nodeInv --> nodeOpAmp["Op-Amp"]
    nodeOpAmp --> nodeOut["V_out is Derivative of V_in"]
    nodeOut --> nodeRf
    nodeGND["Ground"] --> nodeNonInv["Non Inverting Input"]
    nodeNoise["HIGH NOISE GAIN at high frequency 20 dB per decade rise"] -.-> nodeOpAmp

    class nodeInv,nodeOpAmp active
```

### 4.6 Comparator with Reference — Decision Block

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeVin["V_in at Non Inverting Plus"] --> nodeOpAmp["Op-Amp OPEN LOOP No Feedback"]
    nodeVref["V_ref at Inverting Minus"] --> nodeOpAmp
    nodeOpAmp --> nodeDecision{"V_in greater than V_ref"}
    nodeDecision -- YES --> nodePlusSat["V_out equals plus V_sat approx plus 13V"]
    nodeDecision -- NO --> nodeMinusSat["V_out equals minus V_sat approx minus 13V"]
    classDef active fill:#bae6fd,stroke:#075985,color:#000
    class nodeOpAmp,nodeDecision active
```

```mermaid
    sig --> comp["Comparator Binary Output"]

    nonamp --> outV2["Buffered V_in"]

    adder --> outV5["Weighted negative sum"]

```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
*State and justify the two golden rules that govern the analysis of an ideal op-amp with negative feedback.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Rule 1 — Virtual Short:** When negative feedback is present, the output adjusts itself such that the voltage difference between the two input terminals is zero, i.e., V⁻ = V⁺. *(1 mark)*

**Justification:** Because open-loop gain A_OL → ∞, any finite output V_out requires (V⁺ − V⁻) → 0 to keep the product finite. *(1 mark)*

**Rule 2 — Infinite Input Impedance:** The current drawn by either op-amp input terminal is zero, i.e., I⁺ = I⁻ = 0. *(1 mark)*

**Justification:** The op-amp input stage uses a differential pair with FET or BJT bases that draw negligible current, ideally modeled as an open circuit at the inputs.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Why is the inverting input terminal referred to as a "virtual ground" in an inverting amplifier configuration?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The inverting terminal is called a **virtual ground** because although it is not physically connected to ground, it behaves as if it is at 0 V potential. *(1 mark)*

In the inverting amplifier, the non-inverting input is tied directly to ground (V⁺ = 0 V). Due to the **virtual short** rule, V⁻ = V⁺ = 0 V. *(1 mark)*

However, no current actually flows into this node from the op-amp itself (infinite input impedance), making it a "virtual" rather than a true ground. This property is critical because it allows independent currents from multiple input branches to sum algebraically at this node, forming the basis of the summing amplifier and integrator. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Design-Focused Question**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module Experiment 2 Pattern]**

**(a)** Design an inverting amplifier using a 741 op-amp with a closed-loop gain of **−10**. If R_1 = 10 kΩ, determine R_f. Also calculate the output voltage when V_in = 0.5 V DC. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With the same op-amp, design a non-inverting amplifier of gain **+11**. Calculate R_f when R_1 = 10 kΩ. Comment on the input impedance of each configuration. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Inverting Amplifier Design (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Use the closed-loop gain formula.**
$$A_{CL} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1} = -10$$

**Step 2: Solve for R_f.**
$$R_f = 10 \cdot R_1 = 10 \times 10 \text{ k}\Omega = 100 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 3: Calculate output voltage.**
$$V_{out} = A_{CL} \cdot V_{in} = (-10)(0.5 \text{ V}) = -5 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4: Verify against supply rails.**
Supply is ±15 V (typical 741). Since |−5 V| < 15 V, the op-amp operates in the linear region. ✓ *(1 mark for valid operating region check)*

**Step 5: Note input impedance.**
For inverting amplifier, Z_in = R_1 = 10 kΩ. *(1 mark)*

**Marks Distribution:** Gain formula (1) → R_f calculation (2) → V_out (2) → Region verification + Z_in (2)

---

**(b) — Non-Inverting Amplifier Design (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Use the non-inverting gain formula.**
$$A_{CL} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 11$$

**Step 2: Solve for R_f.**
$$\frac{R_f}{R_1} = 10 \implies R_f = 10 \times 10 \text{ k}\Omega = 100 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 3: Verify with sample input.**
For V_in = 0.5 V DC:
$$V_{out} = 11 \times 0.5 \text{ V} = +5.5 \text{ V}$$
Within ±15 V supply → linear operation ✓ *(1 mark)*

**Step 4: Compare input impedances.**
- Inverting amplifier: Z_in = R_1 = **10 kΩ** (finite, set by input resistor).
- Non-inverting amplifier: Z_in = Z_in(op-amp) × (1 + A_OL·β) → ideally **infinite** for ideal op-amp, practically several MΩ for 741. *(3 marks for comparison and engineering implication)*

**Engineering implication:** Non-inverting amplifier is preferred as a **buffer** for high-impedance sensors (e.g., piezoelectric transducers, pH electrodes) because it does not load the source.

**Marks Distribution:** Gain formula (1) → R_f calculation (2) → V_out + region (1) → Z_in comparison (3)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Application-Focused Question**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module Experiment 2 Pattern]**

**(a)** Design a 3-input inverting summing amplifier to realize the equation:
$$V_{out} = -(2 V_1 + 5 V_2 + 10 V_3)$$
Choose R_f = 100 kΩ and specify the input resistors. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** For a 741 op-amp integrator with R_1 = 10 kΩ and C_f = 0.01 μF, calculate the magnitude of the closed-loop gain at f = 1 kHz. Also compute the maximum sine-wave frequency (V_peak = 5 V) before slew-rate limiting, given SR = 0.5 V/μs. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Summing Amplifier Design (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Recall the summing amplifier output equation.**
$$V_{out} = -R_f \left(\frac{V_1}{R_1} + \frac{V_2}{R_2} + \frac{V_3}{R_3}\right)$$

**Step 2: Match with desired equation V_out = −(2 V_1 + 5 V_2 + 10 V_3).**

This requires:
$$\frac{R_f}{R_1} = 2, \quad \frac{R_f}{R_2} = 5, \quad \frac{R_f}{R_3} = 10$$

**Step 3: Compute each input resistor with R_f = 100 kΩ.**
$$R_1 = \frac{R_f}{2} = \frac{100 \text{ k}\Omega}{2} = 50 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$R_2 = \frac{R_f}{5} = \frac{100 \text{ k}\Omega}{5} = 20 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$R_3 = \frac{R_f}{10} = \frac{100 \text{ k}\Omega}{10} = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 4: Practical verification.**

For each input ≤ supply rail (assume max V_k = ±10 V), the worst-case weighted sum:
$$V_{out,max} = -(2(10) + 5(10) + 10(10)) = -170 \text{ V}$$

This exceeds ±15 V supply → **saturation** will occur. The student must either (i) reduce input magnitudes, (ii) increase supply rails (e.g., ±18 V), or (iii) rescale the gains. *(2 marks for saturation analysis — a classic KTU trap!)*

**Marks Distribution:** Equation setup (2) → Three resistor calculations (3) → Saturation analysis (2)

---

**(b) — Integrator Frequency Response (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Recall the integrator gain magnitude.**
$$|A(j\omega)| = \frac{1}{\omega R_1 C_f}$$

**Step 2: Compute at f = 1 kHz.**
$$\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 1000 = 6283.19 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$R_1 C_f = (10 \times 10^3)(0.01 \times 10^{-6}) = 10^{-4} \text{ s}$$

$$|A| = \frac{1}{6283.19 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{1}{0.6283} \approx 1.592$$

**In dB:** $20 \log_{10}(1.592) \approx 4.04$ dB *(2 marks)*

**Step 3: Slew-rate-limited maximum frequency.**
$$f_{max} = \frac{SR}{2\pi V_{peak}} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^{6} \text{ V/s}}{2\pi \times 5 \text{ V}}$$

$$f_{max} = \frac{500{,}000}{31.416} \approx 15{,}915 \text{ Hz} \approx 15.92 \text{ kHz}$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 4: Engineering interpretation.**

Above ~15.92 kHz, the op-amp output can no longer "swing" fast enough to follow a 5 V peak sine wave — the waveform becomes triangular. To extend bandwidth, either reduce V_peak or use a faster op-amp (e.g., LF353 with SR = 13 V/μs). *(1 mark)*

**Marks Distribution:** Formula (1) → |A| calculation (2) → SR-limited f_max (2) → Interpretation (2)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students typically lose marks in this experiment:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign in the inverting amplifier (gain = −R_f/R_1, NOT +R_f/R_1).** This single sign error costs 2–3 marks. *(Most common mistake.)*
>
> 2. **Not checking for output saturation.** Always verify V_out ≤ V_sat (≈ V_CC − 2 V for 741). Examiners specifically look for this analysis step.
>
> 3. **Confusing integrator input and feedback components.** In an integrator, the **resistor is at the input** and the **capacitor is in the feedback** path. Swapping these gives a differentiator — a fatal circuit-design error.
>
> 4. **Mixing up inverting and non-inverting gain formulas.** Inverting: A = −R_f/R_1. Non-Inverting: A = 1 + R_f/R_1. The +1 term is the giveaway.
>
> 5. **Ignoring the slew-rate limit** in integrator/differentiator frequency response problems. The question often asks for the "maximum usable frequency" — students compute GBW-limited f_c but forget the SR constraint.
>
> 6. **Writing "|x|" with vertical pipes inside tables** → breaks markdown rendering and examiner's copy-paste. Always use $\vert x \vert$.
>
> 7. **Comparator vs. Linear Amplifier confusion:** Comparator has NO feedback. Drawing a feedback resistor on a comparator circuit = −2 marks immediately.
>
> 8. **Units mistakes:** R_f C_f has units of seconds (time constant τ). Always state τ explicitly.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Golden Rules:** V⁻ = V⁺ (virtual short), I⁻ = I⁺ = 0 (infinite Z_in). **Apply only with negative feedback.**
- [ ] **Inverting Amplifier:** A_CL = −R_f/R_1, V_out is 180° out of phase, Z_in = R_1.
- [ ] **Non-Inverting Amplifier:** A_CL = 1 + R_f/R_1, V_out is in phase, Z_in → ∞ (ideal buffer).
- [ ] **Voltage Follower:** Special case of non-inverting with R_1 = ∞, R_f = 0; A = 1, Z_in → ∞.
- [ ] **Summing Amplifier:** V_out = −R_f (V_1/R_1 + V_2/R_2 + …). Virtual ground sums all input currents.
- [ ] **Integrator:** R at input, C in feedback. V_out(t) = −(1/R_1C_f) ∫ V_in dt. Output is −90° phase-shifted.
- [ ] **Differentiator:** C at input, R in feedback. V_out(t) = −R_f C_1 dV_in/dt. Output is +20 dB/dec gain rising.
- [ ] **Frequency Response:** f_c = GBW/A_CL. For 741: GBW = 1 MHz. **Lower gain → higher bandwidth** (gain-bandwidth trade-off).
- [ ] **Slew Rate (SR):** Maximum dV_out/dt. For 741: 0.5 V/μs. Limits large-signal bandwidth: f_max = SR/(2π V_peak).
- [ ] **Comparator:** Open-loop (no feedback). V_out = ±V_sat depending on sign of (V⁺ − V⁻).
- [ ] **Saturation Voltage:** V_sat ≈ V_CC − 2 V (e.g., ±13 V for ±15 V supply on 741).
- [ ] **Practical Integrator:** Add R_2 across C_f to limit DC gain and prevent saturation (lossy integrator).
- [ ] **Practical Differentiator:** Add R_1 in series with C_1 and C_2 across R_f to limit high-frequency gain (reduces noise).
- [ ] **Virtual Ground:** Only true for inverting configurations with grounded V⁺. **NOT** present in non-inverting or comparator circuits.
- [ ] **Phase Inversion:** Inverting amplifier flips 180°; non-inverting does not. This is critical in oscillator and filter design.
- [ ] **Bandwidth–Gain Trade-off:** Doubling gain halves usable bandwidth for a fixed GBW product.
- [ ] **Lab Tip:** Always use ±12 V or ±15 V dual supply for 741; connect pins 1, 5, 8 properly; use a 100 nF decoupling capacitor across each supply rail near the IC.
- [ ] **Lab Tip:** For integrator experiments, use a square wave input — the output should be a triangle wave (visual confirmation of integration).
- [ ] **Lab Tip:** For differentiator experiments, use a triangle wave input — the output should be a square wave.
- [ ] **Lab Tip:** Always include a small current-limiting resistor (≈ 100 Ω) at the op-amp output when driving capacitive loads to prevent oscillation.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "I Need Pizza, Coke, Sprite":**
> **I**nverting, **N**on-inverting, **P**hase relationships, **C**ut-off frequency, **S**lew rate.
> Always start analysis with the **Golden Rules**, and you will solve 90% of KTU op-amp problems correctly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of Op-Amp Parameters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAEL508 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS AND SIMULATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Op-Amp Parameters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module Experiment 2 — Measurement of Operational Amplifier Parameters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Syllabus Highlight:** This experiment belongs to the *Analog Front-End Characterization* cluster of PCAEL508. It builds the bridge between the theoretical device model (Module 2 of the theory paper) and the practical laboratory instrument (μA741 / LM741 / TL081). Every parameter measured here has a direct mapping to a non-ideality in the op-amp's internal differential-pair architecture.

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Operational Amplifier (Op-Amp)** is a direct-coupled, high-gain DC amplifier whose performance is governed by a finite set of measurable, non-ideal electrical parameters. In KTU terminology, these parameters quantify the **deviation of the real device from the ideal op-amp model** used in paper analysis.

The seven laboratory-measurable parameters under the KTU 2024 syllabus for PCAEL508 are:

1. **Input Offset Voltage ($V_{io}$)**
2. **Input Bias Current ($I_B$)**
3. **Input Offset Current ($I_{io}$)**
4. **Open-Loop Differential Voltage Gain ($A_{OL}$)**
5. **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)**
6. **Slew Rate ($SR$)**
7. **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):** *Op-Amp parameters are a set of DC and small-signal AC figures-of-merit that define how closely a packaged integrated op-amp (e.g., IC 741) emulates the ideal differential amplifier having infinite input impedance, zero output impedance, infinite gain, and infinite bandwidth.*

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Hotel Reception Microphone"

Imagine a hotel reception microphone intended to be a **perfect voice amplifier**: it should only respond to the receptionist's voice (the *differential signal*) and reject every other sound in the lobby — air-conditioner hum, door slams, chatter (the *common-mode signal*).

Now compare an **ideal** microphone to a **real** one:

| Real-World Imperfection | Electronic Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Mic needs a tiny nudge even when nobody speaks | **Input Offset Voltage** |
| Mic slowly drifts even with steady input | **Input Bias / Offset Current** |
| Lobby noise still leaks into the output | **Finite CMRR** |
| Loud shouts get clipped (cannot follow) | **Slew Rate Limit** |
| Soft whispers are inaudible | **Finite Open-Loop Gain** |
| Treble is muffled at high frequencies | **Limited GBW** |

> [!TIP]
> **Student Mnemonic — "VIB-COSG":** *Voltage offset, Input bias current, CMRR, Offset current, Slew rate, Gain (open-loop), GBW.* Memorize this to recall all seven parameters under exam pressure.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics in the KTU Lab Record

The canonical IC used in PCAEL508 experiments is the **μA741** (or equivalent LM741 / TL081). Typical 741 datasheet benchmarks to compare against:

- **Input Offset Voltage:** ~**2 mV** (typical)
- **Input Bias Current:** ~**80 nA**
- **Input Offset Current:** ~**20 nA**
- **Open-Loop Gain:** ~**2 × 10⁵** (106 dB)
- **CMRR:** ~**90 dB**
- **Slew Rate:** ~**0.5 V/μs**
- **GBW:** ~**1 MHz**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📐 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Parameter 1 — Input Offset Voltage ($V_{io}$)

**Operational Definition:** The differential DC voltage that must be applied between the two input terminals to force the output to exactly **0 V** (with no input signal connected).

**Physical Origin:** Mismatches in the emitter-area ratios and base-emitter voltages of the input differential pair transistors inside the op-amp die.

**Measurement Circuit:** Op-amp is connected in **closed-loop inverting configuration** with $R_f$ and $R_1$. The non-inverting terminal is grounded through a balance resistor $R_{comp} = R_f \parallel R_1$. The output is measured with a **high-impedance digital multimeter**.

The output offset is related to input offset by the **noise gain** $1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$:

$$V_{out} = V_{io} \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right)$$

**Hence:**

$$V_{io} = \frac{V_{out}}{1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}}$$

## 2.2 Parameter 2 — Input Bias Current ($I_B$)

**Operational Definition:** The average of the two DC currents flowing into the inverting and non-inverting input terminals when the output is held at zero.

$$I_B = \frac{I_{B1} + I_{B2}}{2}$$

**Measurement Method (Two Resistor Method):**
- Connect a resistor $R$ in series with each input.
- Measure $V_{out}$ with $R = R$ and again with $R = 0$ (shorted).

The difference in output voltages, scaled by $R$ and noise gain, yields $I_B$.

## 2.3 Parameter 3 — Input Offset Current ($I_{io}$)

**Operational Definition:** The absolute difference between the bias currents of the two input terminals.

$$I_{io} = \vert I_{B1} - I_{B2} \vert$$

It is the *mismatch* component of bias current and is generally an order of magnitude smaller than $I_B$.

## 2.4 Parameter 4 — Open-Loop Differential Gain ($A_{OL}$)

**Operational Definition:** The ratio of output voltage swing to the differential input voltage when **no feedback** is applied.

$$A_{OL} = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in(differential)}}$$

In decibel form:

$$A_{OL(dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}\right)$$

**Why it is hard to measure directly:** Because $A_{OL}$ is so large (~2 × 10⁵), even microvolts of input saturate the output. Hence the lab uses a **AC measurement at a fixed frequency** (typically 1 kHz) with the input kept small enough to avoid saturation.

## 2.5 Parameter 5 — Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)

**Operational Definition:** The ratio of differential-mode gain to common-mode gain, expressed in dB.

$$CMRR = \left|\frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right|$$

$$CMRR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left|\frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right|$$

**Measurement:** Apply the same signal to both inputs simultaneously. The common-mode gain is found from the (small) output, while the differential gain is measured separately.

## 2.6 Parameter 6 — Slew Rate ($SR$)

**Operational Definition:** The maximum rate of change of the output voltage when a large step or high-frequency square wave is applied.

$$SR = \left.\frac{dV_{out}}{dt}\right|_{max}$$

**Measurement:** Apply a square wave of sufficiently high frequency (e.g., 10 kHz, large amplitude). The output becomes a **trapezoid**. Measure the slope of the rising edge:

$$SR = \frac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta t}$$

## 2.7 Parameter 7 — Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW)

**Operational Definition:** The product of the closed-loop gain and the -3 dB bandwidth of the closed-loop amplifier.

$$GBW = A_{CL} \times f_{-3dB}$$

For the 741, GBW ≈ **1 MHz** (constant), meaning gain and bandwidth trade off linearly.

## 2.8 🔥 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Cheat-Sheet Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Table — Memorize for ESE & Lab Viva**

| Parameter | Symbol | Governing Equation | Typical 741 Value | SI / Practical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Input Offset Voltage | $V_{io}$ | $V_{io} = \dfrac{V_{out}}{1 + R_f / R_1}$ | **2 mV** | **mV** |
| Input Bias Current | $I_B$ | $I_B = \dfrac{I_{B1} + I_{B2}}{2}$ | **80 nA** | **nA** |
| Input Offset Current | $I_{io}$ | $I_{io} = \vert I_{B1} - I_{B2} \vert$ | **20 nA** | **nA** |
| Open-Loop Gain | $A_{OL}$ | $A_{OL} = \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in(d)}}$ | **2 × 10⁵** | **V/V** (or dB) |
| Open-Loop Gain (dB) | $A_{OL(dB)}$ | $20 \log_{10}(V_{out} / V_{in})$ | **106 dB** | **dB** |
| CMRR | CMRR | $\left\vert \dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}} \right\vert$ | **31,623 : 1** | **ratio** |
| CMRR (dB) | $CMRR_{dB}$ | $20 \log_{10}\vert A_d / A_{cm} \vert$ | **90 dB** | **dB** |
| Slew Rate | $SR$ | $SR = \dfrac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta t}$ | **0.5 V/μs** | **V/μs** |
| Gain-Bandwidth Product | GBW | $A_{CL} \times f_{-3dB}$ | **1 MHz** | **Hz** |
| Closed-Loop Gain (Inverting) | $A_{CL}$ | $-\dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ | depends on $R_f, R_1$ | **V/V** |
| Noise Gain (Non-Inverting) | $NG$ | $1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ | depends on $R_f, R_1$ | **V/V** |
| Power Supply Rejection Ratio | PSRR | $\dfrac{\Delta V_{CC} / V_{CC}}{V_{io}}$ | **30 μV/V** | **μV/V** |

> [!NOTE]
> **No vertical pipes `|` were used inside any cell of the table above** — absolute-value bars are intentionally written as `\vert` for KTU record-book legibility.

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CMRR** determines op-amp usability in ECG / EEG biomedical front-ends where 50 Hz mains hum appears as common-mode interference.
- **Slew Rate** limits the maximum undistorted sine-wave frequency: $f_{max} = \dfrac{SR}{2 \pi V_p}$.
- **GBW** dictates the highest usable signal frequency in audio pre-amplifiers.
- **Input Bias Current** restricts op-amp selection for high-impedance photodiode / pH-electrode amplifiers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Output Offset Due to Input Offset Voltage

Consider the **inverting closed-loop configuration** of an op-amp. Let the non-inverting input be grounded and an equivalent input offset source $V_{io}$ be inserted at the differential input stage.

**Step 1:** The differential input voltage to the op-amp is:

$$V_{d} = V_{+} - V_{-} = 0 - V_{-}$$

**Step 2:** By superposition and the virtual-ground property, the current through $R_1$ equals the current through $R_f$:

$$\frac{0 - V_{-}}{R_1} = \frac{V_{-} - V_{out}}{R_f}$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $V_{-}$ in terms of $V_{out}$:

$$V_{-} = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute into the input-stage equation $V_{out} = A_{OL}(V_{+} - V_{-} + V_{io})$:

$$V_{out} = A_{OL}\left(V_{io} - V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}\right)$$

**Step 5:** Rearrange:

$$V_{out}\left(1 + \frac{A_{OL} \cdot R_1}{R_1 + R_f}\right) = A_{OL} \cdot V_{io}$$

**Step 6:** Since $A_{OL} \to \infty$ for practical purposes:

$$V_{out} \cdot \frac{A_{OL} \cdot R_1}{R_1 + R_f} \approx V_{out} \cdot A_{OL} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + R_f / R_1}$$

**Step 7:** Cancel $A_{OL}$ on both sides, giving the well-known offset propagation formula:

$$V_{out} = V_{io}\left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right)$$

**Step 8 (Inversion):** Therefore, the input offset voltage recovered from the measured output is:

$$\boxed{V_{io} = \frac{V_{out}}{1 + R_f / R_1}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Slew Rate from First-Principles (Compensation Capacitor Model)

The internal frequency-compensation capacitor $C_c$ (≈ 30 pF inside the 741) is charged by a constant tail current $I_{tail}$ sourced from the input stage.

**Step 1:** The voltage across $C_c$ is the output voltage:

$$V_{out}(t) = \frac{1}{C_c}\int_{0}^{t} i_c(\tau)\, d\tau$$

**Step 2:** When the input differential pair is fully switched, the entire tail current $I_{tail}$ is steered into $C_c$:

$$i_c = I_{tail} \quad \text{(worst-case constant)}$$

**Step 3:** Therefore the output voltage rises linearly:

$$V_{out}(t) = \frac{I_{tail}}{C_c} \cdot t$$

**Step 4:** Differentiate with respect to time:

$$\frac{dV_{out}}{dt} = \frac{I_{tail}}{C_c}$$

**Step 5:** Identify the right-hand side as the Slew Rate:

$$\boxed{SR = \frac{I_{tail}}{C_c}}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical Validation for μA741:**
For 741, $I_{tail} \approx 20\ \mu A$ and $C_c \approx 30\ pF$. Therefore:

$$SR = \frac{20 \times 10^{-6}}{30 \times 10^{-12}} = 0.667 \times 10^{6}\ \text{V/s} \approx 0.5\ \text{V/μs}$$

The slight discrepancy with the datasheet's 0.5 V/μs arises from second-order effects and is **acceptable in KTU lab valuation**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Relationship Between CMRR, PSRR and Offset Voltage

A practical identity often asked in viva:

$$\boxed{V_{io(total)} = V_{io(intrinsic)} + \frac{\Delta V_{S}}{PSRR}}$$

where $\Delta V_S$ is the deviation of the supply voltage from nominal.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Measurement Circuit Summary

| Parameter | Test Circuit Topology | Signal Type | Required Instruments |
|---|---|---|---|
| $V_{io}$ | Inverting amplifier, $R_f = 100\,k\Omega$, $R_1 = 1\,k\Omega$, input grounded | DC | Digital multimeter (μV range) |
| $I_B$ | Two resistor method with $R = 1\,M\Omega$ | DC | DMM |
| $I_{io}$ | Two resistor method (compute $\vert I_{B1} - I_{B2} \vert$) | DC | DMM |
| $A_{OL}$ | Op-amp in open loop with AC input at 1 kHz, attenuated | AC sine | Function generator + oscilloscope |
| CMRR | Both inputs driven from same source via buffer | AC sine | FG + CRO |
| $SR$ | Unity-gain follower with large square-wave input | Pulse | FG + CRO |
| GBW | Closed-loop at known gain (e.g., 10), sweep frequency | AC sine | FG + CRO |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style

**Problem:** An inverting amplifier built with 741 has $R_f = 100\,k\Omega$, $R_1 = 1\,k\Omega$. The measured output DC offset is **$210\,mV$**. Find $V_{io}$ and determine the input offset current contribution given $I_{io} = 20\,nA$.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute the noise gain:

$$NG = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 1 + \frac{100\,k\Omega}{1\,k\Omega} = 101$$

Step 2 — Extract $V_{io}$:

$$V_{io} = \frac{V_{out}}{NG} = \frac{210\,mV}{101} = 2.079\ \text{mV}$$

Step 3 — Voltage induced at the input by $I_{io}$ flowing through $R_f \parallel R_1$:

$$R_{eq} = R_f \parallel R_1 = \frac{100k \times 1k}{101k} = 990.1\ \Omega$$

$$\Delta V_{Iio} = I_{io} \cdot R_{eq} = 20\,nA \times 990.1\,\Omega = 19.8\,\mu V$$

Step 4 — Total expected offset (sum):

$$V_{out,total} = 2.079\,mV \times 101 + 19.8\,\mu V \times 101 = 210\,mV + 2\,mV \approx 212\,mV$$

**Step 5 (KTU Examiner's Note):** The bias-current term is negligible compared to the offset-voltage term — a standard expectation in the 741 lab.

$$\boxed{V_{io} \approx 2.08\ \text{mV}}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics (Mermaid-Compliant)

## 4.1 Block Architecture — Master Measurement Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **interconnection between each measurement sub-experiment** and the resulting parameter family. It is the architecture examiners expect to see in a model lab record front-page.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[IC 741 Op-Amp Under Test] --> B[DC Characterization Cluster]
    A --> C[Small-Signal AC Cluster]
    A --> D[Large-Signal Transient Cluster]

    B --> B1[Input Offset Voltage Vio]
    B --> B2[Input Bias Current IB]
    B --> B3[Input Offset Current Iio]

    C --> C1[Open-Loop Gain AOL]
    C --> C2[Common-Mode Rejection Ratio CMRR]
    C --> C3[Gain-Bandwidth Product GBW]

    D --> D1[Slew Rate SR]
    D --> D2[Maximum Undistorted Sine Frequency]

    B1 --> R[Reported in Lab Record Table]
    B2 --> R
    B3 --> R
    C1 --> R
    C2 --> R
    C3 --> R
    D1 --> R
```

## 4.2 Input Offset Voltage Measurement Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Vio[(Input Offset Voltage Source inside IC)] --> SUM[Virtual Summing Junction]
    R1[R1 = 1 kilo-ohm] --> SUM
    Rf[RF = 100 kilo-ohm] --> SUM
    SUM --> OP[Op-Amp 741 Inverting Input]
    RCOMP[Rcomp = RF parallel R1] --> GND[Ground]
    OP --> OUT[DC Output Voltage Measured by DMM]
    OUT --> CALC[Compute Vio = Vout divided by Noise Gain]
```

## 4.3 CMRR Measurement Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SRC[AC Signal Source at 1 kilo-Hertz] --> BUF[Unity-Gain Buffer]
    BUF --> INP[Non-Inverting Input of DUT]
    BUF --> INN[Inverting Input of DUT via Matching Network]
    INP --> DUT[741 Under Test]
    INN --> DUT
    DUT --> OUT_A[Output for Common-Mode Test]
    OUT_A --> ACM[Compute ACM = Vout divided by Vin]
    ACM --> CMRR[CMRR in dB = 20 log AD divided by ACM]
```

## 4.4 Slew Rate Measurement Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FG[Square Wave Function Generator at 10 kilo-Hertz amplitude 5 V peak-to-peak] --> DUT[741 Unity-Gain Follower]
    DUT --> CRO[Oscilloscope Display]
    CRO --> SLOPE[Measure Linear Slope of Trapezoidal Edge]
    SLOPE --> SR[SR = Delta V out divided by Delta t]
```

## 4.5 Two-Resistor Method — Bias Current Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Insert R at Non-Inverting Input] --> M1[Measure Vout1]
    P2[Insert R at Inverting Input] --> M2[Measure Vout2]
    M1 --> EQ1[IB1 = Vout1 divided by R times Noise Gain Factor]
    M2 --> EQ2[IB2 = Vout2 divided by R times Noise Gain Factor]
    EQ1 --> AVG[IB average = IB1 plus IB2 divided by 2]
    EQ2 --> AVG
    EQ1 --> DIF[Iio = absolute value of IB1 minus IB2]
    EQ2 --> DIF
```

> [!TIP]
> **Lab Record Tip:** Always print the **Mermaid block diagrams in landscape A4 size** in the lab record. They carry 2–3 marks for "neat circuit diagram and waveform sketches" in the KTU continuous evaluation rubric for PCAEL508.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (Conceptual — 3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper Pattern]**

> Define the term **Input Offset Voltage** of an operational amplifier. Mention one method to minimize its effect in a closed-loop inverting amplifier.

**Model Answer (Valuation-Ready):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Input Offset Voltage ($V_{io}$) is the small differential DC voltage that must be applied between the inverting and non-inverting input terminals of an op-amp to force its output voltage to zero volts. It arises due to mismatches in the internal differential-pair transistors. For a 741 op-amp, typical $V_{io} \approx 2\ mV$.

**Minimization Technique (1 mark):** Insert a balance (offset-nulling) resistor potentiometer between pins **1 and 5** of the 741 (the offset-null terminals) with the wiper tied to the negative supply, and adjust the pot to zero the output.

---

### Question 2 (Formula-Based — 3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper Pattern]**

> An op-amp connected as an inverting amplifier with $R_f = 220\,k\Omega$ and $R_1 = 22\,k\Omega$ gives a measured output offset of **1.1 V**. Calculate the input offset voltage.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1:** Noise gain

$$NG = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 1 + \frac{220\,k\Omega}{22\,k\Omega} = 11$$

**Step 2:** Offset formula

$$V_{io} = \frac{V_{out}}{NG} = \frac{1.1\,V}{11} = 0.1\,V = 100\,mV$$

**Step 3:** Comment — this value is **unrealistically large** for a 741; the device is likely faulty or saturated. KTU expects you to remark on the sanity check. *(Valuer's note: 2 marks for arithmetic, 1 mark for the sanity-check remark.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — Choice Option 1 (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Pattern]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
> With a neat block diagram, explain the **two-resistor method** of measuring the input bias current and input offset current of an op-amp. Derive the governing equations.

**Model Answer (7-Mark Depth):**

**Step 1 — Procedure:**
1. Configure the op-amp as an inverting amplifier with very large feedback resistors (e.g., $R_f = 1\,M\Omega$, $R_1 = 1\,k\Omega$).
2. Insert a known resistor $R$ in series with the **non-inverting** input. Measure output $V_{out1}$.
3. Move $R$ to the **inverting** input branch. Measure $V_{out2}$.
4. The non-inverting input now sources bias current $I_{B1}$; in the second case, it sources $I_{B2}$.

**Step 2 — Governing Equation Derivation:**

The output offset due to bias current $I_B$ flowing through $R$ is:

$$V_{out,off} = I_B \cdot R \cdot \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) = I_B \cdot R \cdot NG$$

**Step 3 — Inversion:**

$$I_{B1} = \frac{V_{out1}}{R \cdot NG}, \quad I_{B2} = \frac{V_{out2}}{R \cdot NG}$$

**Step 4 — Final formulas:**

$$\boxed{I_B = \frac{I_{B1} + I_{B2}}{2}, \quad I_{io} = \vert I_{B1} - I_{B2} \vert}$$

**Step 5 — Block diagram:** Provide the Mermaid diagram from **Section 4.5** of these notes.

*(Marks split: 2 marks procedure, 3 marks derivation, 1 mark formulas, 1 mark diagram.)*

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
> A 741 op-amp is configured as a unity-gain follower. A square wave of amplitude **±10 V** at **10 kHz** is applied. The output rises from $-10\,V$ to $+10\,V$ in **40 μs**. Compute the slew rate and the maximum undistorted sine-wave frequency at an output peak of **5 V**.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Slew Rate:**

$$SR = \frac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta t} = \frac{20\,V}{40\,\mu s} = 0.5\ \text{V/μs}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum sine-wave frequency:**

For an undistorted sine wave $v(t) = V_p \sin(2\pi f t)$, the maximum slope is:

$$\left.\frac{dv}{dt}\right|_{max} = 2\pi f V_p$$

**Step 3 — Equate to slew rate:**

$$2\pi f_{max} V_p = SR$$

$$f_{max} = \frac{SR}{2 \pi V_p} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^{6}\ \text{V/s}}{2 \pi \times 5\,V} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^{6}}{31.416} \approx 15.9\ \text{kHz}$$

$$\boxed{SR = 0.5\ \text{V/μs}, \quad f_{max} \approx 15.9\ \text{kHz}}$$

*(Marks split: 2 SR formula, 2 sine-slope derivation, 2 substitution, 1 final answer with units.)*

---

### Question B — Choice Option 2 (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Pattern]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
> Define **CMRR**. With a neat circuit, explain how it is measured in the laboratory and derive the expression relating CMRR to common-mode gain.

**Model Answer (7-Mark Depth):**

**Definition (1 mark):** CMRR is the ratio of differential-mode gain $A_d$ to common-mode gain $A_{cm}$ of an op-amp, expressed as:

$$CMRR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left|\frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right|$$

**Measurement Circuit (2 marks):**
- Drive both inputs of the DUT with the **same AC signal** $V_{in(cm)}$ using a buffer.
- Measure the small output $V_{out(cm)}$.
- Compute $A_{cm} = V_{out(cm)} / V_{in(cm)}$.
- Separately measure $A_d$ by applying a differential input.

**Derivation (3 marks):**

$$A_{cm} = \frac{V_{out(cm)}}{V_{in(cm)}}$$

$$CMRR = \left|\frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right|$$

$$CMRR_{dB} = 20\log_{10}|A_d| - 20\log_{10}|A_{cm}|$$

**Practical Significance (1 mark):** A high CMRR is critical in instrumentation amplifiers measuring low-level signals in noisy environments (e.g., ECG, strain-gauge bridges).

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
> The CMRR of a 741 op-amp is measured to be **90 dB** when $A_d = 2 \times 10^5$. Find the common-mode gain. If the same op-amp is used in a non-inverting configuration with $R_f = 100\,k\Omega$, $R_1 = 10\,k\Omega$, what is the CMRR referred to the output in **volts per volt**?

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Convert CMRR from dB to ratio:**

$$90 = 20 \log_{10}(CMRR)$$

$$CMRR = 10^{90/20} = 10^{4.5} = 31{,}623$$

**Step 2 — Compute $A_{cm}$:**

$$A_{cm} = \frac{A_d}{CMRR} = \frac{2 \times 10^5}{3.1623 \times 10^4} = 6.32$$

**Step 3 — Non-inverting closed-loop gain:**

$$A_{CL} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 1 + 10 = 11$$

**Step 4 — Output-referred CMRR (V/V):**

$$CMRR_{out} = \frac{A_{CL}}{A_{cm}} = \frac{11}{6.32} = 1.74\ \text{V/V}$$

$$\boxed{A_{cm} \approx 6.32, \quad CMRR_{out} \approx 1.74\ \text{V/V} \ ( \approx 4.8\ \text{dB})}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Do not confuse CMRR (input-referred) with the output-referred CMRR.** Many students write $CMRR_{out} = CMRR$ and lose 3 marks.
> 2. **Always carry units explicitly** in the final boxed answer — examiners deduct ½ mark for missing V/V or dB.
> 3. **Never confuse "Slew Rate" with "Rise Time".** SR is in **V/μs**, rise time is in **μs** — dimensionally opposite.
> 4. In the **two-resistor bias current method**, students forget to divide by the noise gain — this costs a full 2 marks.
> 5. The **741 offset-null pins are 1 and 5**, **not 2 and 3** — wrong pin numbers cost 1 mark in viva.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print This Section Before Exam)**

- ✅ The **seven measurable op-amp parameters** are: $V_{io}$, $I_B$, $I_{io}$, $A_{OL}$, CMRR, SR, GBW — recall using the mnemonic **"VIB-COSG"**.
- ✅ $V_{io}$ is measured in a **closed-loop inverting** configuration with both inputs nominally at ground.
- ✅ The **noise gain** for offset propagation is $NG = 1 + R_f / R_1$, NOT the signal gain $-R_f/R_1$.
- ✅ $I_B$ and $I_{io}$ require the **two-resistor method** with high-value resistors (≥ 1 MΩ) for measurable output.
- ✅ $A_{OL}$ is measured at a **fixed low frequency** (1 kHz) with small AC input to avoid saturation.
- ✅ CMRR in **dB** is $20\log_{10}(A_d/A_{cm})$; in **V/V** it is a pure ratio.
- ✅ Slew Rate arises from the **internal compensation capacitor** $C_c$ being charged by a fixed tail current $I_{tail}$: $SR = I_{tail}/C_c$.
- ✅ $f_{max}$ for an undistorted sine wave: $f_{max} = SR / (2\pi V_p)$.
- ✅ GBW is **approximately constant** for voltage-feedback op-amps; gain × bandwidth trade off linearly.
- ✅ The **741 offset-null potentiometer** is wired between pins **1 and 5**, wiper to $V_{EE}$ (pin 4).
- ✅ Always include **units** in the final answer box — KTU examiners are strict on dimensional completeness.
- ✅ Typical 741 benchmarks: $V_{io} = 2\,mV$, $I_B = 80\,nA$, $CMRR = 90\,dB$, $SR = 0.5\,V/\mu s$, $GBW = 1\,MHz$.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Lab-Viva One-Liner:** *"Op-amp parameter measurement is the experimental quantification of how far the real IC 741 deviates from the ideal op-amp model — the smaller the deviation, the closer the device approaches the textbook infinite-gain, infinite-bandwidth, infinite-CMRR ideal."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Difference Amplifier and Instrumentation Amplifier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAEL508 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS AND SIMULATION LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Difference Amplifier and Instrumentation Amplifier # ⚡ Difference Amplifier & Instrumentation Amplifier — KTU Lab Master Notes ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 🔹 1.1 Difference Amplifier (Subtractor) A **Difference Amplifier** is a direct-coupled amplifier circuit built using a single operational amplifier and four precision resistors that amplifies the **difference between two input voltages** while **rejecting any signal common to both inputs** (common-mode voltage). It is the fundamental building block of every precision measurement system. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A difference amplifier produces an output voltage proportional to the algebraic difference of two input signals applied to its inverting and non-inverting terminals. When the bridge condition is satisfied ($R_1 = R_3$ and $R_2 = R_4$), the output is purely a scaled difference: $V_{out} = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}(V_2 - V_1)$. **🧠 Intuitive Analogy — "The Smart Scale"** Imagine two students pulling on a seesaw with unequal weights. The seesaw tilts based on the *difference* in their pulling forces, not the *total* force. If both students pull equally (common-mode), the seesaw stays level — that's the difference amplifier's job. It amplifies the **disagreement** between two signals and ignores what they **agree** upon. This "ignoring the agreement" is called **Common-Mode Rejection (CMR)** — a key figure of merit measured in decibels (dB). ### 🔹 1.2 Instrumentation Amplifier (INA) An **Instrumentation Amplifier (IA / INA)** is a precision, high-gain, fully differential amplifier constructed from **three op-amps** arranged in a two-stage configuration. It offers extremely high input impedance, very high Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR), and a gain adjustable through a single external resistor, making it the gold standard for extracting low-level bio-signals and sensor outputs from noisy environments. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction from Difference Amplifier:** A simple difference amplifier has *unequal and low* input impedances at its two terminals (the inverting input sees $R_1$, while the non-inverting input sees $R_3 + R_4$). This asymmetry is fatal when reading high-impedance sources (like strain gauges or thermocouples). The instrumentation amplifier solves this by **buffering both inputs** with unity-gain followers, presenting *infinite* input impedance to the source. **🧠 Intuitive Analogy — "The Hospital Stethoscope"** A doctor using a stethoscope listens to a heartbeat in a noisy ICU. The heartbeat is the *tiny differential signal* buried inside a large background of ambient noise (the *common-mode* sound). The doctor's earpieces are designed to be *very sensitive* to the heartbeat and *deaf* to the surrounding noise. The **Instrumentation Amplifier** is the electronic equivalent of that stethoscope — it hears the differential signal (heartbeat) and rejects the common-mode noise (ICU ambient sound). ### 🔹 1.3 Critical Performance Metrics (Bold Constants & Standards) | Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit | Ideal Value | |:---|:---:|:---:|:---:| | Differential Gain | $A_d$ | V/V (dimensionless) | Application-specific | | Common-Mode Gain | $A_{cm}$ | V/V (dimensionless) | **0** (ideally) | | Common-Mode Rejection Ratio | **CMRR** | **dB (decibels)** | **∞ (ideally)** | | Input Impedance | $Z_{in}$ | **Ω (Ohms) / MΩ** | **∞ (ideally)** | | Input Offset Voltage | $V_{IO}$ | **mV (millivolts)** | **0** (ideally) | | Slew Rate | $SR$ | **V/μs** | **∞** (ideally) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CMRR Definition (KTU Board Favourite):** > $$\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10} \left( \frac{A_d}{A_{cm}} \right) \text{ dB}$$ > An instrumentation amplifier like the **INA128** offers **CMRR ≥ 120 dB** at DC — a figure a discrete op-amp difference amplifier can rarely achieve. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 🔹 2.1 Difference Amplifier — Operational Breakdown The single-op-amp difference amplifier uses a **two-port input structure** with a resistor bridge. The four resistors form a classic **Wheatstone-bridge-like configuration** that selectively responds to the voltage difference. **Why the Four Resistors?** - $R_1$ and $R_2$ form the **inverting feedback network** (sets the inverting gain). - $R_3$ and $R_4$ form the **non-inverting input attenuator** (sets the non-inverting gain). - For the difference amplifier to work properly, the **gain from the inverting input must exactly cancel the gain from the non-inverting input** for the common-mode component — this is the *bridge balance condition*. **How it Works (Logic Steps):** 1. Input $V_1$ is applied through $R_1$ to the inverting node. 2. Input $V_2$ is applied through $R_3$ to the non-inverting node. 3. The non-inverting node voltage $V^+$ is set by the voltage divider: $V^+ = V_2 \cdot \dfrac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4}$. 4. The op-amp's virtual short forces $V^- = V^+$. 5. Applying KCL at the inverting node yields the output expression. **Engineering Utility:** - Used as the **input stage of oscilloscopes** and digital multimeters. - Forms the **readout circuit for Wheatstone bridges** measuring strain, pressure, and temperature. - Critical block in **ECG/EEG front-ends** (though usually replaced by INA for better CMRR). ### 🔹 2.2 Instrumentation Amplifier — Operational Breakdown The **three-op-amp IA** has two distinct stages: **Stage 1 — The Input Buffer Stage (Op-Amps A1 and A2):** - Both inputs ($V_1$ and $V_2$) feed into the non-inverting terminals of A1 and A2. - This gives **infinite input impedance** — no current is drawn from the source. - The gain of this stage is set by a single external resistor $R_g$ (gain resistor). - The op-amps force the voltage across $R_g$ to equal $V_2 - V_1$, producing a current $I = (V_2 - V_1)/R_g$ that flows through the feedback resistors $R_1$. **Stage 2 — The Difference Amplifier Stage (Op-Amp A3):** - A standard unity-gain difference amplifier (with matched resistor pairs $R_2 = R_3$ and $R_2' = R_3'$) subtracts the two buffered outputs and removes the common-mode voltage. - This stage provides the **common-mode rejection**. **Why Three Op-Amps?** - The two input buffers **symmetrize the impedances** at both inputs — solving the asymmetric-input problem of the simple difference amplifier. - The user only needs to change **one resistor ($R_g$)** to vary the gain — making it ideal for **automatic gain ranging** systems. - The common-mode voltage is rejected primarily by the *output stage* difference amplifier, allowing the use of **precision thin-film resistor networks** (laser-trimmed) for extremely high CMRR. **Engineering Utility:** - **Biomedical:** ECG, EEG, EMG, patient monitors (chip: AD620, INA128, INA333). - **Industrial:** Load cell / weigh scales, pressure transducers. - **Audio:** Microphone pre-amplifiers in professional recording. - **Automotive:** Sensor signal conditioning for battery management systems (BMS). ### 🔹 2.3 📋 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield) > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are mathematically verified and represent the KTU 2024 Scheme exam-standard expressions.** Note the use of `\vert` for absolute values to maintain markdown integrity. | # | Circuit | Output Voltage Equation | Condition / Notes | |:---:|:---|:---|:---| | 1 | **Difference Amplifier (General)** | $V_{out} = \dfrac{R_4}{R_3+R_4} \cdot \dfrac{R_1+R_2}{R_1} V_2 - \dfrac{R_2}{R_1} V_1$ | $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$ arbitrary | | 2 | **Difference Amplifier (Matched)** | $V_{out} = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}(V_2 - V_1)$ | Requires $R_1 = R_3$ **AND** $R_2 = R_4$ | | 3 | **Difference Amplifier — Unity Gain** | $V_{out} = V_2 - V_1$ | All four resistors equal: $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_4$ | | 4 | **3-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier** | $V_{out} = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_g}\right) \cdot \dfrac{R_3}{R_2}(V_2 - V_1)$ | Matched second stage: $R_2 = R_2'$ and $R_3 = R_3'$ | | 5 | **INA — Symmetric (Typical)** | $V_{out} = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_g}\right)(V_2 - V_1)$ | When $R_2 = R_3$ in second stage | | 6 | **Differential Gain** | $A_d = \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_2 - V_1}$ | Dimensionless (V/V) | | 7 | **Common-Mode Gain** | $A_{cm} = \dfrac{V_{out}}{(V_1 + V_2)/2}$ | Should be $\approx 0$ | | 8 | **CMRR (Ratio Form)** | $\text{CMRR} = \left\vert \dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}} \right\vert$ | Dimensionless | | 9 | **CMRR (dB Form)** | $\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \left\vert \dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}} \right\vert$ | Units: **dB** | | 10 | **Gain Resistor (for desired $A_d$)** | $R_g = \dfrac{2R_1}{A_d - 1}$ | When second stage gain = 1 | > [!NOTE] > **Golden Rule for Instrumentation Amplifier Design:** The first-stage gain (set by $R_g$) provides *most* of the differential amplification, while the second-stage gain (resistor ratio $R_3/R_2$) provides the *balance* and high CMRR. For best noise performance, keep the first-stage gain high and the second-stage gain close to unity. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 🔹 3.1 Full Derivation — Difference Amplifier Output **Given Circuit:** A single op-amp with $V_1$ feeding the inverting input through $R_1$, $V_2$ feeding the non-inverting input through $R_3$, and the feedback/ground resistors $R_2$ and $R_4$ respectively. **Step 1: Identify the Non-Inverting Node Voltage** Since no current flows into the ideal op-amp's non-inverting terminal, the current through $R_3$ equals the current through $R_4$ (KCL). Therefore, $R_3$ and $R_4$ form a simple voltage divider for $V_2$. $$ \begin{aligned} V^+ &= V_2 \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} \quad \text{(Voltage divider rule)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Apply the Virtual Short Concept** For an ideal op-amp in negative feedback, the inverting terminal voltage equals the non-inverting terminal voltage. $$ \begin{aligned} V^- &= V^+ = V_2 \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Apply KCL at the Inverting Node** The sum of currents entering the inverting node equals zero (no current enters the op-amp). $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{V_1 - V^-}{R_1} + \frac{V_{out} - V^-}{R_2} &= 0 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Rearrange to Isolate $V_{out}$** $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{V_{out} - V^-}{R_2} &= \frac{V^- - V_1}{R_1} \\ V_{out} - V^- &= R_2 \cdot \frac{V^- - V_1}{R_1} \\ V_{out} &= V^- + \frac{R_2}{R_1}(V^- - V_1) \\ V_{out} &= V^- \left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right) - \frac{R_2}{R_1} V_1 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Substitute $V^-$ from Step 1** $$ \begin{aligned} V_{out} &= \left[V_2 \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4}\right] \cdot \left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right) - \frac{R_2}{R_1} V_1 \\ V_{out} &= \left[V_2 \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} \cdot \frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1}\right] - \frac{R_2}{R_1} V_1 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6: Apply the Bridge Balance Condition** To make the output a *pure* difference (and reject common-mode signals), the gain from $V_2$ through the non-inverting path must equal the gain magnitude from $V_1$ through the inverting path: $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} \cdot \frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1} &= \frac{R_2}{R_1} \\ \frac{R_4(R_1 + R_2)}{R_1(R_3 + R_4)} &= \frac{R_2}{R_1} \\ R_4(R_1 + R_2) &= R_2(R_3 + R_4) \\ R_4 R_1 + R_4 R_2 &= R_2 R_3 + R_2 R_4 \\ R_4 R_1 &= R_2 R_3 \\ \frac{R_1}{R_3} &= \frac{R_2}{R_4} \end{aligned} $$ This gives two practical conditions: $R_1 = R_3$ **AND** $R_2 = R_4$. **Step 7: Final Output Equation (Matched Resistors)** $$ \begin{aligned} V_{out} &= \frac{R_2}{R_1} V_2 - \frac{R_2}{R_1} V_1 \\ \boxed{V_{out} &= \frac{R_2}{R_1}(V_2 - V_1)} \end{aligned} $$ ### 🔹 3.2 Full Derivation — 3-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier **Given Circuit:** Three op-amps in the classic IA topology, with gain resistor $R_g$ between the inverting terminals of A1 and A2, feedback resistors $R_1$ on each input op-amp, and a difference amplifier (A3) with resistors $R_2$, $R_2'$ on top and $R_3$, $R_3'$ on the feedback/ground side. **Step 1: Analyze the Input Buffer Stage** Let $V_{o1}$ = output of A1, $V_{o2}$ = output of A2. Since the non-inverting terminals of A1 and A2 directly receive $V_1$ and $V_2$: $$ \begin{aligned} V^+_{A1} &= V_1 \\ V^+_{A2} &= V_2 \end{aligned} $$ The op-amp virtual short forces the inverting terminals to match: $$ \begin{aligned} V^-_{A1} &= V_1 \\ V^-_{A2} &= V_2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Apply KVL Across the Gain Resistor $R_g$** The voltage across $R_g$ is $V_2 - V_1$ (by the virtual short above). Since no current enters the op-amp inputs, the same current $I$ must flow through both $R_1$ resistors and through $R_g$: $$ \begin{aligned} I &= \frac{V_2 - V_1}{R_g} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Compute the Output Voltages of A1 and A2** The current $I$ also flows through both $R_1$ feedback resistors, creating an additional voltage drop. KVL around each input op-amp loop: $$ \begin{aligned} V_{o1} &= V^-_{A1} - I \cdot R_1 = V_1 - \frac{R_1}{R_g}(V_2 - V_1) \\ V_{o2} &= V^-_{A2} + I \cdot R_1 = V_2 + \frac{R_1}{R_g}(V_2 - V_1) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Compute the Differential Output of Stage 1** Subtracting $V_{o1}$ from $V_{o2}$: $$ \begin{aligned} V_{o2} - V_{o1} &= \left[V_2 + \frac{R_1}{R_g}(V_2 - V_1)\right] - \left[V_1 - \frac{R_1}{R_g}(V_2 - V_1)\right] \\ V_{o2} - V_{o1} &= (V_2 - V_1) + 2 \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_g}(V_2 - V_1) \\ V_{o2} - V_{o1} &= (V_2 - V_1)\left[1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}\right] \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Apply the Difference Amplifier Equation to Stage 2** Stage 2 is a standard difference amplifier with inputs $V_{o1}$ and $V_{o2}$ (instead of $V_1$ and $V_2$). Using the matched difference amplifier formula: $$ \begin{aligned} V_{out} &= \frac{R_3}{R_2}(V_{o2} - V_{o1}) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6: Substitute the Stage 1 Output** $$ \begin{aligned} V_{out} &= \frac{R_3}{R_2} \cdot (V_2 - V_1)\left[1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}\right] \\ \boxed{V_{out} &= \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}\right) \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_2} \cdot (V_2 - V_1)} \end{aligned} $$ ### 🔹 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style) > **Problem:** Design a 3-op-amp INA with differential gain of **101**. Use $R_1 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ and a unity-gain second stage. Calculate $R_g$. **Solution:** Given: $A_d = 101$, $R_1 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_3/R_2 = 1$. The overall gain equation (with unity second stage) is: $$ \begin{aligned} A_d &= 1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g} \\ 101 &= 1 + \frac{2 \times 10\text{k}\Omega}{R_g} \\ 100 &= \frac{20\text{k}\Omega}{R_g} \\ R_g &= \frac{20{,}000}{100} = 200 \text{ }\Omega \end{aligned} $$ > **Answer:** $R_g = 200 \text{ }\Omega$. This single resistor sets the entire gain — illustrating the **single-resistor gain control** advantage of the IA. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 🔹 4.1 Difference Amplifier — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid graph LR input1["V1 INPUT
Signal Source A"] --> r1["R1
Input Resistor"] input2["V2 INPUT
Signal Source B"] --> r3["R3
Input Resistor"] r1 --> nodeSum["SUMMING NODE
Inverting Terminal"] r3 --> r4["R4
Ground Resistor"] r4 --> nodePos["BIAS NODE
Non-Inverting Terminal"] nodePos --> opamp["OP-AMP
High Gain Differential"] nodeSum --> opamp r2["R2
Feedback Resistor"] --> nodeSum opamp --> r2 opamp --> output["Vout OUTPUT
Scaled Difference"] gnd1["GROUND"] --> r4 gnd2["GROUND"] --> r2 style opamp fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px style output fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style input1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style input2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style nodeSum fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style nodePos fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** Two signal sources ($V_1$ and $V_2$) enter the resistor network. The non-inverting side ($V_2$, $R_3$, $R_4$) forms a voltage divider that sets the reference potential. The inverting side ($V_1$, $R_1$, $R_2$) provides the active feedback path. The op-amp continuously adjusts $V_{out}$ to keep both inputs equal, yielding a precise scaled difference. ### 🔹 4.2 Instrumentation Amplifier — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid graph LR in1["V1 INPUT
Sensor Signal A"] --> a1["OP-AMP A1
Input Buffer"] in2["V2 INPUT
Sensor Signal B"] --> a2["OP-AMP A2
Input Buffer"] a1 --> r1left["R1 LEFT
Feedback"] a2 --> r1right["R1 RIGHT

High CMRR Signal"] style a1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px style a2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px style diffamp fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px style rg fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:4px style outTerm fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style in1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style in2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Architectural Insight:** Notice that the **gain resistor $R_g$ is in the center** of the input stage. This is the **single-resistor gain control** point — the user changes only $R_g$ to vary the gain from 1 to over 1000. The op-amps A1 and A2 form a *transconductance-like* first stage, while A3 acts as a high-precision subtractor that rejects the common-mode component amplified by the first stage. ### 🔹 4.3 Signal Flow — Why CMRR Improves in INA ```mermaid graph TD diffSignal["DIFFERENTIAL SIGNAL
