KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question 1: Define Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR) of a difference amplifier. State its ideal value.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** CMRR is defined as the ratio of the differential-mode gain ($A_d$) to the common-mode gain ($A_{cm}$) of an amplifier, expressed in decibels. It quantifies the amplifier's ability to **reject signals that appear simultaneously and in-phase on both inputs** (common-mode signals like noise or hum). $$ \begin{aligned} \text{CMRR}_{dB} &= 20 \log_{10} \left\vert \frac{A_d}{A_{cm}} \right\vert \text{ dB} \end{aligned} $$ **Ideal value: ∞ (infinite dB)**, meaning the amplifier responds only to the difference between the inputs and completely ignores any common component. --- #### **Question 2: List two advantages of a 3-op-amp instrumentation amplifier over a basic difference amplifier.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** 1. **Infinite (very high) input impedance:** The instrumentation amplifier uses op-amp buffers at both inputs, so it draws negligible current from the source — essential when reading high-impedance sensors like strain gauges and thermocouples. 2. **Single-resistor gain control:** The differential gain of the IA can be set by varying just one external resistor $R_g$, making it ideal for **programmable/automatic gain ranging** systems. In contrast, the basic difference amplifier requires changing *two* resistors to vary the gain while maintaining CMRR. *(Acceptable additional answers: Higher CMRR, better common-mode voltage range, balanced input impedances.)* --- ### 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** Derive the output voltage expression of a single-op-amp difference amplifier. State the **bridge balance condition** required for proper operation and explain what happens if the resistor matching is imperfect. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** For the difference amplifier shown, $R_1 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 100 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_3 = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_4 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $V_1 = 2.5 \text{ V}$, $V_2 = 3.0 \text{ V}$. Calculate the output voltage $V_{out}$. Comment on the validity of the difference amplifier behavior. **[7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Adapted]` **Model Solution (a):** The derivation proceeds as follows: 1. Using the voltage divider at the non-inverting node: $V^+ = V_2 \cdot \dfrac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4}$. 2. Virtual short gives: $V^- = V^+$. 3. KCL at inverting node: $\dfrac{V_1 - V^-}{R_1} + \dfrac{V_{out} - V^-}{R_2} = 0$. 4. Solving for $V_{out}$: $$ \begin{aligned} V_{out} &= V^- \left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right) - \frac{R_2}{R_1} V_1 \\ V_{out} &= \left(\frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4}\right)\left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right)V_2 - \frac{R_2}{R_1} V_1 \end{aligned} $$ 5. **Bridge Balance Condition** for pure difference output: $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{R_1}{R_3} = \frac{R_2}{R_4} \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_1 R_4 = R_2 R_3 \end{aligned} $$ Under this condition: $$ \begin{aligned} V_{out} = \frac{R_2}{R_1}(V_2 - V_1) \end{aligned} $$ **Effect of Imperfect Matching:** If the bridge condition is not satisfied, a fraction of the **common-mode voltage** $\left(\dfrac{V_1 + V_2}{2}\right)$ appears at the output along with the desired difference. This **degrades CMRR**, increases output error, and makes the circuit sensitive to the average DC level of the inputs — a serious problem in noisy industrial environments. **Model Solution (b):** Given: $R_1 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 100 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_3 = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_4 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $V_1 = 2.5 \text{ V}$, $V_2 = 3.0 \text{ V}$. **Check bridge balance:** $$ \begin{aligned} R_1 \cdot R_4 &= 10 \text{k} \times 10 \text{k} = 100 \text{ k}^2 \\ R_2 \cdot R_3 &= 100 \text{k} \times 1 \text{k} = 100 \text{ k}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Bridge condition is satisfied** (rare coincidence given arbitrary values), so the simple difference equation applies: $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{R_2}{R_1} &= \frac{100 \text{ k}\Omega}{10 \text{ k}\Omega} = 10 \\ V_{out} &= 10 \times (V_2 - V_1) \\ V_{out} &= 10 \times (3.0 - 2.5) \\ \boxed{V_{out} &= 10 \times 0.5 = 5.0 \text{ V}} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Verify with general formula:** $V^+ = 3.0 \times \dfrac{10\text{k}}{1\text{k}+10\text{k}} = 2.727 \text{ V}$. Then $V_{out} = 2.727(1+10) - 10 \times 2.5 = 30 - 25 = 5.0 \text{ V}$. ✓ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** **(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a **3-op-amp instrumentation amplifier**. Derive its output voltage expression in terms of the gain resistor $R_g$. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** An instrumentation amplifier uses $R_1 = 25 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_g = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$, and the second-stage difference amplifier has $R_2 = R_3 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$. If the input voltages are $V_1 = 2.485 \text{ V}$ and $V_2 = 2.515 \text{ V}$, calculate: (i) the differential gain $A_d$, (ii) the output voltage $V_{out}$. **[7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Adapted]` **Model Solution (a):** The 3-op-amp IA consists of: - **Stage 1:** Two non-inverting buffers (A1, A2) with a shared gain resistor $R_g$ between their inverting terminals. Both input op-amps have feedback resistors $R_1$ each. - **Stage 2:** A unity-gain (or scaled) difference amplifier (A3) that subtracts the two buffered outputs. **Working Principle:** - The inputs $V_1$ and $V_2$ are applied to the non-inverting terminals of A1 and A2, giving **infinite input impedance**. - By the virtual short principle, the voltage across $R_g$ equals $V_2 - V_1$, setting current $I = (V_2 - V_1)/R_g$. - This same current flows through both $R_1$ feedback resistors, creating voltages $IR_1$ at each output. - The first-stage differential output is $(V_2 - V_1)(1 + 2R_1/R_g)$. - A3 amplifies this differential signal by $R_3/R_2$ while rejecting common-mode voltages. **Final Derived Expression:** $$ \begin{aligned} \boxed{V_{out} = \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}\right) \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_2} \cdot (V_2 - V_1)} \end{aligned} $$ **Model Solution (b):** Given: $R_1 = 25 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_g = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = R_3 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $V_1 = 2.485 \text{ V}$, $V_2 = 2.515 \text{ V}$. **(i) Differential Gain $A_d$:** Since $R_2 = R_3$, the second stage gain is 1. The total gain is: $$ \begin{aligned} A_d &= \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}\right) \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_2} \\ A_d &= \left(1 + \frac{2 \times 25 \text{ k}\Omega}{1 \text{ k}\Omega}\right) \cdot \frac{10 \text{ k}\Omega}{10 \text{ k}\Omega} \\ A_d &= (1 + 50) \times 1 \\ \boxed{A_d &= 51 \text{ V/V}} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Output Voltage $V_{out}$:** $$ \begin{aligned} V_{out} &= A_d \cdot (V_2 - V_1) \\ V_{out} &= 51 \times (2.515 - 2.485) \\ V_{out} &= 51 \times 0.030 \\ \boxed{V_{out} &= 1.53 \text{ V}} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Design Insight:** A tiny 30 mV sensor differential signal has been amplified to 1.53 V — a 51× amplification that boosts the signal well above the noise floor of subsequent ADC stages. Note that the common-mode voltage here is 2.5 V (a typical bridge excitation), which is effectively **rejected** by the IA. --- ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Read Carefully!):** > 1. **Forgetting the second-stage gain term ($R_3/R_2$):** Many students write the IA output as $(1 + 2R_1/R_g)(V_2 - V_1)$ even when $R_2 \neq R_3$. If the second stage is *not* unity gain, you **must** include $R_3/R_2$. Loss: **2 marks**. > 2. **Confusing bridge balance:** Students often state $R_1 = R_2$ for the difference amplifier. The correct condition is $R_1 = R_3$ **AND** $R_2 = R_4$ (or equivalently $R_1/R_3 = R_2/R_4$). Loss: **1–2 marks**. > 3. **CMRR in dB vs. ratio:** The numerical value of CMRR depends on whether you express it as a ratio (dimensionless) or in decibels. Always state the unit explicitly. Loss: **1 mark**. > 4. **Sign error in derivation:** The final sign of $V_{out}$ depends on which input goes to the inverting terminal. Standard convention: $V_2$ at non-inverting, $V_1$ at inverting → positive $V_{out}$ when $V_2 > V_1$. Loss: **1 mark**. > 5. **Missing virtual short justification:** When using $V^- = V^+$, you must mention this holds only under **negative feedback** with an **ideal op-amp**. Loss: **1 mark** for KTU valuation. > 6. **Forgetting to verify the bridge condition in numerical problems:** KTU examiners often give resistor values that *look* matched but are subtly mismatched to test if you check. Always verify $R_1 R_4 = R_2 R_3$ before using the simple difference formula! --- ### ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] **Difference Amplifier** uses **1 op-amp + 4 resistors** and produces $V_{out} = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}(V_2 - V_1)$ only when $R_1 = R_3$ and $R_2 = R_4$. - [x] **Instrumentation Amplifier** uses **3 op-amps** for high input impedance, high CMRR, and single-resistor gain control via $R_g$. - [x] **Bridge Balance Condition** for a difference amplifier: $\dfrac{R_1}{R_3} = \dfrac{R_2}{R_4}$ (i.e., $R_1 R_4 = R_2 R_3$). Imbalance → poor CMRR. - [x] **IA Output Formula:** $V_{out} = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_g}\right) \cdot \dfrac{R_3}{R_2} \cdot (V_2 - V_1)$. - [x] **CMRR Definition:** $\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left\vert\dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right\vert$ — always in **dB** for KTU exams. - [x] **Single-Resistor Gain Setting in IA:** $R_g = \dfrac{2R_1}{A_d - 1}$ (for unity second stage). - [x] **Asymmetric Input Impedance** is the key weakness of the basic difference amplifier — the IA solves this with input buffers. - [x] **Real-World IA ICs:** AD620, INA128, INA333, INA128 (CMRR ≥ 120 dB). - [x] **Applications:** ECG/EEG amplifiers, weigh scales, strain gauge bridges, thermocouple amplifiers, battery monitoring. - [x] **For Lab Simulation (LTspice/Multisim):** Use ideal op-amp models (e.g., `opamp` in LTspice or `LM741` for non-ideal), apply differential inputs through function generators, and measure $V_{out}$ to compute experimental CMRR. - [x] **Lab Tip:** When measuring CMRR experimentally, set $V_1 = V_2 = V_{cm}$ (common-mode) and sweep frequency. The output should remain near zero — any deviation indicates finite CMRR. ---

topics.title: Schmitt Trigger Circuit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAEL508 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS AND SIMULATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Schmitt Trigger Circuit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Schmitt Trigger Circuit** is a regenerative comparator circuit with **positive feedback** that converts a slowly varying input signal into a sharply defined, square-wave output. It is characterized by **hysteresis**, meaning it possesses two distinct threshold voltages — an **Upper Threshold Voltage (VUT)** and a **Lower Threshold Voltage (VLT)** — rather than a single switching point. The output switches state only when the input crosses one of these thresholds, making the circuit highly immune to noise.

In the context of **PCAEL508 (Linear Integrated Circuits Lab)**, the Schmitt Trigger is typically realized using the **µA741 Operational Amplifier** configured with a feedback fraction (β) from the output back to the non-inverting terminal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Schmitt Trigger is a mandatory experiment demonstrating **hysteresis**, **positive feedback**, and **threshold voltage calculation** using op-amps like µA741. It tests the practical understanding of the difference between an ordinary comparator and a regenerative comparator.

## 1.2 Two Canonical Configurations

| Configuration | Input Applied To | Output Polarity | Common Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Inverting Schmitt Trigger** | Inverting Terminal (−) | Inverted (180° phase shift) | Waveform generation, noise filtering |
| **Non-Inverting Schmitt Trigger** | Non-Inverting Terminal (+) | Non-Inverted (in-phase) | Zero-crossing detectors with hysteresis |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Thermostat Model

Imagine a **room thermostat** controlling an air conditioner:

- The thermostat is set to maintain **24°C**.
- It turns the AC **ON** only when temperature rises above **26°C** (Upper Threshold).
- It turns the AC **OFF** only when temperature falls below **22°C** (Lower Threshold).

The gap of **4°C** between switching points is the **Hysteresis Band (VH)**. The thermostat ignores small fluctuations (like a brief gust of warm air) within the dead-zone. Similarly, a Schmitt Trigger ignores small noise ripples on the input, switching cleanly only at the two well-defined thresholds.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this important in KTU Labs?**
> A simple op-amp comparator without feedback produces **multiple transitions** when noise is present on a slowly-rising input. The Schmitt Trigger solves this with **positive (regenerative) feedback**, ensuring exactly one clean output transition — a classic "noise immunity" demonstration that is a guaranteed question in lab viva.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Saturation Voltage of µA741:** $V_{sat} = \pm V_{sat} \approx \pm 13\,\text{V}$ (with $\pm 15\,\text{V}$ dual supply). Some boards use $V_{sat} \approx \pm 12\,\text{V}$.
- **Feedback Fraction (β):** Dimensionless ratio $\beta = \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$, typically chosen between **0.1 and 0.9**.
- **Hysteresis Voltage (VH):** $V_H = V_{UT} - V_{LT}$, measured in **Volts (V)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — Why Hysteresis Works

The defining feature of the Schmitt Trigger is the **positive feedback loop** connecting the output back to the **non-inverting (+) input** through a resistive divider (R1, R2). This feedback ensures the switching action is **regenerative (snap-action)** rather than gradual.

### Logic of Operation (Inverting Configuration):

1. Initially, assume output $V_o = +V_{sat}$.
2. The voltage at the non-inverting terminal is: $V_+ = \beta \cdot V_o = \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot (+V_{sat})$.
3. This sets the **Upper Threshold Voltage**: $V_{UT} = +\beta V_{sat}$.
4. As long as $V_{in} < V_{UT}$, the op-amp differential input keeps output saturated at $+V_{sat}$.
5. The instant $V_{in}$ exceeds $V_{UT}$, the output **regeneratively snaps** to $-V_{sat}$.
6. Now the non-inverting terminal drops to: $V_+ = \beta \cdot (-V_{sat})$.
7. This sets the **Lower Threshold Voltage**: $V_{LT} = -\beta V_{sat}$.
8. Output stays at $-V_{sat}$ until $V_{in}$ falls below $V_{LT}$, triggering another snap.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Regeneration:** Positive feedback forces the output to saturate in either $+V_{sat}$ or $-V_{sat}$ **instantaneously**. Without it (open-loop comparator), the output transitions slowly through the linear region, creating susceptibility to noise-induced multiple switching.

## 2.2 Transfer Characteristics Explained

The **voltage transfer curve (VTC)** of a Schmitt Trigger is a **rectangular hysteresis loop** when plotting $V_{in}$ (x-axis) vs $V_o$ (y-axis):

- For $V_{in} < V_{LT}$: $V_o = +V_{sat}$
- For $V_{in} > V_{UT}$: $V_o = -V_{sat}$
- Within $V_{LT} < V_{in} < V_{UT}$: $V_o$ retains its previous state (memory effect)

The **width of the loop horizontally** = $V_H = V_{UT} - V_{LT} = 2\beta V_{sat}$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Feedback Fraction** | $\beta = \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Voltage divider ratio from output to $V_+$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | **Upper Threshold Voltage** | $V_{UT} = +\beta \cdot V_{sat}$ | Input level that triggers output from HIGH to LOW | Volts (V) |
| 3 | **Lower Threshold Voltage** | $V_{LT} = -\beta \cdot V_{sat}$ | Input level that triggers output from LOW to HIGH | Volts (V) |
| 4 | **Hysteresis Width** | $V_H = V_{UT} - V_{LT} = 2\beta V_{sat}$ | Dead-band region of input voltage | Volts (V) |
| 5 | **Threshold Magnitude** | $\vert V_{th} \vert = \beta \cdot V_{sat}$ | Symmetric trip point (for $V_{UT},V_{LT}$) | Volts (V) |
| 6 | **Output Saturation** | $V_o = \pm V_{sat}$ | Op-amp saturated output limits (typically $\pm 12$ to $\pm 13\,\text{V}$ for µA741) | Volts (V) |
| 7 | **Non-Inverting Trigger Thresholds** | $V_{th}^{\pm} = \pm \dfrac{V_{sat}}{1 - \beta}$ | Used when input is at $V_+$ (non-inverting config) | Volts (V) |
| 8 | **Slew Rate Constraint** | $SR = \dfrac{\Delta V}{\Delta t}$ | Op-amp slew rate limits max. frequency (µA741 SR = 0.5 V/µs) | V/µs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU Exam Tip:** In exam questions, if $V_{sat}$ is not specified, the examiner expects you to use $V_{sat} = 13\,\text{V}$ (standard for $\pm 15\,\text{V}$ supply on µA741). Always state this assumption explicitly in your answer. If $V_{sat} = 12\,\text{V}$ is given in the question, use that instead.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Signal Conditioning:** Converting noisy sinusoidal/sensor signals into clean digital pulses (e.g., tachometer signals, optical encoder outputs).
- **Zero-Crossing Detectors:** With asymmetric supply or biased reference, used in AC line voltage detection.
- **Relaxation Oscillators:** Combined with an RC timing network, forms a **square-wave generator** (astable multivibrator).
- **Debouncing Circuits:** Eliminates mechanical-switch bounce in digital input lines.
- **Over/Under-Voltage Protection:** Comparators in battery management systems use hysteresis to prevent rapid relay chattering.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Threshold Voltages (Inverting Schmitt Trigger)

### Given Circuit:
- Op-amp µA741 with supply $\pm V_{CC} = \pm 15\,\text{V}$.
- Input $V_{in}$ applied to the **inverting terminal (−)** through resistor $R_{in}$.
- Resistor $R_2$ from output to **non-inverting terminal (+)**.
- Resistor $R_1$ from non-inverting terminal (+) to **ground**.

### Step 1: Define the Voltage at the Non-Inverting Terminal

Using superposition (or voltage divider) at the non-inverting terminal, since no current flows into the ideal op-amp input:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_+ &= V_o \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} + 0 \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Simplify the Expression

Since the input signal is not connected to the non-inverting terminal (only the divider is), the second term vanishes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_+ &= V_o \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Define the Feedback Fraction (β)

Let us denote the constant multiplier as the **feedback fraction**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta &= \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_+ &= \beta \cdot V_o
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: State the Switching Condition

The op-amp output changes state when the differential input changes sign:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_d &= V_+ - V_- = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

At the instant of switching, $V_- = V_{in}$ and $V_+ = \beta \cdot V_o$. The transition occurs when $V_{in}$ reaches $\beta \cdot V_o$.

### Step 5: Case A — Output Previously at $+V_{sat}$

If the output is currently $+V_{sat}$, then $V_+ = +\beta V_{sat}$. The output will switch to $-V_{sat}$ when:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{in} &> V_+ = +\beta V_{sat}
\end{aligned}
$$

So the **Upper Threshold Voltage** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{UT} &= +\beta V_{sat}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6: Case B — Output Previously at $-V_{sat}$

If the output is currently $-V_{sat}$, then $V_+ = -\beta V_{sat}$. The output will switch to $+V_{sat}$ when:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{in} &< V_+ = -\beta V_{sat}
\end{aligned}
$$

So the **Lower Threshold Voltage** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{LT} &= -\beta V_{sat}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7: Hysteresis Voltage Calculation

The hysteresis width is the difference between the two thresholds:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_H &= V_{UT} - V_{LT}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_H &= (+\beta V_{sat}) - (-\beta V_{sat})
\end{aligned}
$$

Simplifying:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_H &= 2\beta V_{sat}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (Board Exam Style)

**Problem:** Design an inverting Schmitt Trigger with µA741 such that $V_{UT} = +4\,\text{V}$ and $V_{LT} = -4\,\text{V}$. Assume $V_{sat} = \pm 13\,\text{V}$. Find suitable values of $R_1$ and $R_2$.

### Solution:

**Step 1: Calculate the feedback fraction β.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta &= \frac{V_{UT}}{V_{sat}} = \frac{4}{13} \approx 0.3077
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Express β in terms of R1 and R2.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta &= \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Choose a standard value for R1.**

Let $R_1 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ (standard E12 series value).

**Step 4: Solve for R2.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.3077 &= \frac{10\,\text{k}\Omega}{10\,\text{k}\Omega + R_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.3077 \cdot (10\,\text{k}\Omega + R_2) &= 10\,\text{k}\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

Expanding:

$$
\begin{aligned}
3.077\,\text{k}\Omega + 0.3077 \cdot R_2 &= 10\,\text{k}\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

Isolating $R_2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.3077 \cdot R_2 &= 10\,\text{k}\Omega - 3.077\,\text{k}\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.3077 \cdot R_2 &= 6.923\,\text{k}\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_2 &= \frac{6.923}{0.3077} \approx 22.5\,\text{k}\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Select nearest standard value.**

Choose $R_2 = 22\,\text{k}\Omega$ (E12 standard). Recalculate β:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta_{new} &= \frac{10}{10 + 22} = \frac{10}{32} = 0.3125
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{UT,new} &= 0.3125 \times 13 = +4.06\,\text{V} \quad (\text{Acceptable})
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{LT,new} &= 0.3125 \times (-13) = -4.06\,\text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Design Parameter Summary Table

| Specification | Formula | Practical Range |
|---|---|---|
| Choose R1 | Free selection | $1\,\text{k}\Omega$ to $100\,\text{k}\Omega$ |
| Calculate R2 | $R_2 = R_1 \cdot \dfrac{(1-\beta)}{\beta}$ | Use E12 standard values |
| Verify $V_{sat}$ | Depends on op-amp | $\pm 13\,\text{V}$ for µA741 |
| Hysteresis | $V_H = 2\beta V_{sat}$ | Keep $> 100\,\text{mV}$ for noise immunity |
| Input Resistor | $R_{in}$ (limits bias current) | Typically $10\,\text{k}\Omega$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Circuit Architecture (Inverting Schmitt Trigger)

The following Mermaid block renders the **functional block architecture** of the inverting Schmitt trigger, illustrating the signal flow from input through the op-amp to the regenerative feedback loop.

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1["Vin - Input Signal"] --> node2["Rin - Input Resistor"]
    node2 --> node3["Node A - Inverting Terminal of Op-Amp"]
    node3 --> node4["uA741 Op-Amp Core"]
    node5["GND - Ground Reference"] --> node6["R1 - Lower Feedback Resistor"]
    node6 --> node7["Node B - Non-Inverting Terminal"]
    node4 --> node8["Vo - Output Node"]
    node8 --> node9["R2 - Upper Feedback Resistor"]
    node9 --> node7
    node7 --> node4
    node8 --> node10["RLoad and CRO Display"]
    node4 --> node11["Vplus plus 15V Supply"]
    node4 --> node12["Vminus minus 15V Supply"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The path `node8 → node9 → node7 → node4` represents the **positive (regenerative) feedback loop** — the defining feature of the Schmitt Trigger. Without this loop, the circuit behaves as a simple open-loop comparator without hysteresis.

## 4.2 Threshold Detection Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    stateA["Start - Output Initialized to plus Vsat"] --> stateB["Compute Vplus equals beta times plus Vsat equals VUT"]
    stateB --> stateC["Monitor Vin"]
    stateC --> cond1{"Vin greater than VUT ?"}
    cond1 -- YES --> stateD["Output Snaps to minus Vsat"]
    cond1 -- NO --> stateC
    stateD --> stateE["Compute Vplus equals beta times minus Vsat equals VLT"]
    stateE --> stateF["Monitor Vin"]
    stateF --> cond2{"Vin less than VLT ?"}
    cond2 -- YES --> stateG["Output Snaps to plus Vsat"]
    cond2 -- NO --> stateF
    stateG --> stateB
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram of the Experiment

```mermaid
graph LR
    block1["Function Generator - Sine Wave Input"] --> block2["Inverting Schmitt Trigger Circuit on Breadboard"]
    block2 --> block3["Channel 1 of CRO - Input Waveform"]
    block2 --> block4["Channel 2 of CRO - Output Square Wave"]
    block2 --> block5["DC Voltmeter - Threshold Verification"]
    block6["Dual Power Supply plus minus 15V DC"] --> block2
```

## 4.4 Transfer Characteristic (Hysteresis Loop)

The hysteresis loop is rectangular, with the input on the horizontal axis and the output on the vertical axis. The key transition points are:

- Point P1: $(V_{in} = V_{LT}, V_o = +V_{sat})$ — Output transitions from $+V_{sat}$ to $-V_{sat}$.
- Point P2: $(V_{in} = V_{UT}, V_o = -V_{sat})$ — Output transitions from $-V_{sat}$ to $+V_{sat}$.

The horizontal width of the loop is $V_H = V_{UT} - V_{LT} = 2\beta V_{sat}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Lab Observation Note:** When demonstrating this on the CRO in X-Y mode (Vin on X-axis, Vo on Y-axis), you will observe a clean rectangular hysteresis loop. If the loop appears **tilted** or **narrower than expected**, it indicates either (a) incorrect $V_{sat}$ assumption, (b) loading effect from the CRO probe, or (c) wrong feedback resistor values.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a Schmitt Trigger. What is hysteresis and why is it important in comparator circuits?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A Schmitt Trigger is a **regenerative comparator circuit** that uses **positive feedback** to convert a slowly varying input signal into a clean, square-wave output. It has **two distinct threshold voltages** — an Upper Threshold ($V_{UT}$) and a Lower Threshold ($V_{LT}$) — rather than a single switching point.

**Hysteresis** is the phenomenon where the output state depends not only on the present input value but also on the **previous input history**. The difference between the two thresholds is called the **Hysteresis Voltage**, $V_H = V_{UT} - V_{LT} = 2\beta V_{sat}$.

**Importance:** Hysteresis provides **noise immunity**. A simple op-amp comparator without feedback produces multiple unwanted transitions when the input has noise near the threshold. The Schmitt Trigger's two-threshold design ensures the input must cross a defined dead-band before the output changes state, eliminating spurious switching. (3 Marks)

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between an Inverting Schmitt Trigger and a Non-Inverting Schmitt Trigger.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Inverting Schmitt Trigger | Non-Inverting Schmitt Trigger |
|---|---|---|
| Input Applied To | Inverting (−) terminal | Non-Inverting (+) terminal |
| Output Polarity | Inverted w.r.t. input | Non-Inverted w.r.t. input |
| Threshold Formula | $V_{th} = \pm \beta V_{sat}$ | $V_{th} = \pm V_{sat}/(1-\beta)$ |
| Common Application | Waveform shaping, oscillators | Zero-crossing detectors |
| Transfer Curve Direction | Clockwise hysteresis loop | Counter-clockwise hysteresis loop |

In both configurations, **positive feedback** is present, and the output snaps between $+V_{sat}$ and $-V_{sat}$. (3 Marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of an **Inverting Schmitt Trigger** using an op-amp. Derive the expressions for the **upper and lower threshold voltages** and the **hysteresis width**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the inverting Schmitt Trigger circuit, $R_1 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 30\,\text{k}\Omega$, and $V_{sat} = \pm 13\,\text{V}$. Calculate the **threshold voltages** and **hysteresis width**. If the input is a sine wave of amplitude $8\,\text{V}$ peak, sketch the output waveform showing the switching instants. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question A(a):

**Circuit Diagram:** [Refer to Section 4.1 — Inverting Schmitt Trigger architecture]

**Working Principle:**

In the inverting Schmitt Trigger, the input signal $V_{in}$ is applied to the **inverting terminal** through resistor $R_{in}$. A resistive divider ($R_1$ from non-inverting terminal to ground, $R_2$ from non-inverting terminal to output) provides **positive feedback** to the non-inverting terminal.

The voltage at the non-inverting terminal is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_+ = \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_o = \beta \cdot V_o
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\beta = \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$ is the feedback fraction.

**State 1 — Output at $+V_{sat}$:**

When the output is at $+V_{sat}$, the voltage at the non-inverting terminal is $V_+ = +\beta V_{sat}$. The output remains at $+V_{sat}$ as long as the differential input $V_d = V_+ - V_{in} > 0$, i.e., $V_{in} < +\beta V_{sat}$. The instant $V_{in}$ exceeds $+\beta V_{sat}$, the output snaps to $-V_{sat}$.

Therefore, the **Upper Threshold Voltage** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{UT} = +\beta V_{sat} = +\frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_{sat}
\end{aligned}
$$

**State 2 — Output at $-V_{sat}$:**

When the output is at $-V_{sat}$, the voltage at the non-inverting terminal is $V_+ = -\beta V_{sat}$. The output remains at $-V_{sat}$ as long as $V_{in} > -\beta V_{sat}$. The instant $V_{in}$ falls below $-\beta V_{sat}$, the output snaps back to $+V_{sat}$.

Therefore, the **Lower Threshold Voltage** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{LT} = -\beta V_{sat} = -\frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_{sat}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Hysteresis Width:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_H = V_{UT} - V_{LT} = 2\beta V_{sat} = \frac{2R_1}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_{sat}
\end{aligned}
$$

This hysteresis prevents the output from oscillating when the input is near the threshold, providing excellent noise immunity. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question A(b):

**Step 1: Calculate the feedback fraction β.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta = \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{10}{10 + 30} = \frac{10}{40} = 0.25
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate the threshold voltages.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{UT} = +\beta V_{sat} = 0.25 \times 13 = +3.25\,\text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{LT} = -\beta V_{sat} = 0.25 \times (-13) = -3.25\,\text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate the hysteresis width.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_H = V_{UT} - V_{LT} = 3.25 - (-3.25) = 6.5\,\text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Analyze the sine wave input.**

Given: Sine wave amplitude $= 8\,\text{V}$ peak, so $V_{in}$ oscillates between $+8\,\text{V}$ and $-8\,\text{V}$.

Since the input amplitude ($8\,\text{V}$) **exceeds both threshold voltages** ($+3.25\,\text{V}$ and $-3.25\,\text{V}$), the output will switch states twice per cycle.

**Switching Instants:**

The sine wave $V_{in}(t) = 8\sin(\omega t)$ crosses $+3.25\,\text{V}$ at angle $\theta_1 = \sin^{-1}(3.25/8) = \sin^{-1}(0.40625) \approx 23.97°$.

The sine wave crosses $-3.25\,\text{V}$ at angle $\theta_2 = 180° - 23.97° = 156.03°$ (on the descending slope).

**Output Waveform Description:**

- From $\theta = 0°$ to $23.97°$: $V_{in}$ rises from $0$ to $3.25\,\text{V}$, output is at $+13\,\text{V}$.
- At $\theta = 23.97°$: $V_{in} = +3.25\,\text{V} = V_{UT}$, output **snaps to $-13\,\text{V}$**.
- From $\theta = 23.97°$ to $156.03°$: $V_{in}$ rises to peak ($+8\,\text{V}$) and falls to $3.25\,\text{V}$, output is at $-13\,\text{V}$.
- At $\theta = 156.03°$: $V_{in} = +3.25\,\text{V} = V_{UT}$ (on descending slope), output **snaps back to $+13\,\text{V}$**.
- From $\theta = 156.03°$ to $180° + 23.97° = 203.97°$: $V_{in}$ falls to $0$ then to $-3.25\,\text{V}$, output is at $+13\,\text{V}$.
- At $\theta = 203.97°$: $V_{in} = -3.25\,\text{V} = V_{LT}$, output **snaps to $-13\,\text{V}$**.

**The output is a square wave of amplitude $\pm 13\,\text{V}$ with a duty cycle slightly less than 50% (because the input sine wave is not symmetric about the threshold region in time).** **(7 Marks)**

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (Alternative Choice)
**(a)** Draw the circuit of a **Non-Inverting Schmitt Trigger** using an op-amp. Explain its operation and derive the threshold voltage expressions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A non-inverting Schmitt Trigger has $R_1 = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 9\,\text{k}\Omega$, and $V_{sat} = \pm 12\,\text{V}$. Find the **threshold voltages**, **hysteresis width**, and the **range of input voltage for which the output remains in the previous state**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question B(a):

**Circuit Diagram (Non-Inverting Schmitt Trigger):**

The input $V_{in}$ is applied to the **non-inverting terminal (+)**. The inverting terminal is connected to a voltage divider formed by $R_1$ (from inverting terminal to ground) and $R_2$ (from inverting terminal to output). This configuration provides positive feedback from the output to the inverting terminal.

**Operation:**

The voltage at the inverting terminal is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_- = \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_o = \beta \cdot V_o
\end{aligned}
$$

The op-amp output switches when $V_{in} = V_-$, i.e., $V_{in} = \beta V_o$.

**Case 1: Output at $+V_{sat}$**

The output remains at $+V_{sat}$ as long as $V_{in} > V_- = +\beta V_{sat}$. The instant $V_{in}$ falls below $+\beta V_{sat}$, the output snaps to $-V_{sat}$.

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{UT} = +\beta V_{sat} = +\frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_{sat}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Case 2: Output at $-V_{sat}$**

The output remains at $-V_{sat}$ as long as $V_{in} < V_- = -\beta V_{sat}$. The instant $V_{in}$ exceeds $-\beta V_{sat}$, the output snaps to $+V_{sat}$.

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{LT} = -\beta V_{sat} = -\frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_{sat}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Note:** The threshold expressions appear similar to the inverting configuration, but the **transfer characteristic direction is reversed** (counter-clockwise hysteresis loop). The output is non-inverted with respect to the input. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question B(b):

**Step 1: Calculate the feedback fraction β.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta = \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{1}{1 + 9} = \frac{1}{10} = 0.1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate the threshold voltages.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{UT} = +\beta V_{sat} = 0.1 \times 12 = +1.2\,\text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{LT} = -\beta V_{sat} = 0.1 \times (-12) = -1.2\,\text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate the hysteresis width.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_H = V_{UT} - V_{LT} = 1.2 - (-1.2) = 2.4\,\text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Determine the input range for no state change.**

The output retains its previous state when the input lies in the **hysteresis dead-band**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{LT} \leq V_{in} \leq V_{UT} \Rightarrow -1.2\,\text{V} \leq V_{in} \leq +1.2\,\text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

Within this $2.4\,\text{V}$ wide input range, the output **does not change state** — it remains at whatever value it last held ($+V_{sat}$ or $-V_{sat}$). This dead-band is precisely the noise-immunity zone. **(7 Marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in PCAEL508 Lab Exam:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the β factor:** Students often write $V_{UT} = V_{sat}$ directly, forgetting the voltage divider. Always remember $V_{UT} = \beta V_{sat}$, not $V_{sat}$.
> 2. **Wrong $V_{sat}$ value:** Using $V_{sat} = 15\,\text{V}$ (the supply voltage) instead of the actual saturation voltage ($\pm 13\,\text{V}$ for µA741). The op-amp output cannot reach the supply rail.
> 3. **Confusing inverting and non-inverting configurations:** The input is at different terminals, and the transfer curve direction differs. Mix-up = 2-3 marks lost.
> 4. **Not drawing the hysteresis loop:** In the circuit diagram question, failing to show the rectangular transfer characteristic costs 2 marks. Always sketch $V_{in}$ vs $V_o$ showing the two thresholds.
> 5. **Skipping units:** Writing "$V_H = 6.5$" without "V" (Volts). KTU examiners deduct half a mark for missing units in numerical answers.
> 6. **Wrong feedback direction in Mermaid diagram:** Drawing negative feedback (resistor from output to inverting terminal) instead of positive feedback (to non-inverting terminal). This fundamentally changes the circuit.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for Schmitt Trigger:**
>
> - **Schmitt Trigger** = Comparator + **Positive Feedback** (regenerative).
> - Defining feature: **Two threshold voltages** ($V_{UT}$ and $V_{LT}$) — not one.
> - **Hysteresis Voltage:** $V_H = V_{UT} - V_{LT} = 2\beta V_{sat}$.
> - **Feedback Fraction:** $\beta = \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$.
> - **Threshold formula (inverting/non-inverting):** $V_{th} = \pm\beta V_{sat}$.
> - Op-amp used in lab: **µA741** with $V_{sat} \approx \pm 13\,\text{V}$ (for $\pm 15\,\text{V}$ supply).
> - Input amplitude must **exceed** $\vert V_{UT} \vert$ for the output to switch.
> - **Output is always saturated** at $\pm V_{sat}$ — never in the linear region (in steady state).
> - **Slew rate** of µA741 (0.5 V/µs) limits the maximum frequency of input signals.
> - **Practical application:** Noise filtering, square-wave generation, debouncing, relaxation oscillators.
> - **Transfer curve:** Rectangular hysteresis loop. Inverting version = clockwise; non-inverting = counter-clockwise.
> - **Lab test instrument:** Function generator (input) + CRO channels 1 & 2 (input/output simultaneously).
> - **KVL/KCL at non-inverting terminal:** $V_+ = \beta V_o$ (since input current to op-amp is zero).
> - **Switching condition:** $V_{in} = \beta V_o$ (differential input = 0 at the switching instant).
> - **Non-inverting variant input threshold** (when input is at + terminal): $V_{th} = \pm V_{sat}/(1-\beta)$ — note the different denominator!
> - **Dead-band:** Input range $V_{LT} \leq V_{in} \leq V_{UT}$ where output state is **frozen** (memory effect).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Astable and Monostable Multivibrators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAEL508 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS AND SIMULATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Astable and Monostable Multivibrators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The 555 Timer — Foundation Stone

The **IC 555 Timer**, designed by Hans Camenzind in 1971 and commercialized by Signetics, is one of the most versatile and widely used linear integrated circuits in the history of electronics. It is an 8-pin device that can operate in three primary modes: **Monostable** (one-shot), **Astable** (free-running oscillator), and **Bistable** (flip-flop).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The 555 timer is treated as a "building block IC." For the Linear Integrated Circuits and Simulation Lab, students must master the design, simulation, and hardware realization of both **Astable** and **Monostable** modes using the 555.

### 1.1.1 Pin Configuration of IC 555

| Pin No. | Pin Name | Function |
|:-------:|:---------|:---------|
| **1** | **GND** | Ground reference (**0 V**) |
| **2** | **TRIG** | Trigger input (active LOW). Starts timing cycle when voltage falls below $V_{CC}/3$ |
| **3** | **OUT** | Output pin. Can source or sink up to **200 mA** |
| **4** | **RESET** | Active LOW reset. Discharges the timing capacitor when pulled LOW |
| **5** | **CONT** | Control Voltage. Modulates threshold/trigger levels internally |
| **6** | **THR** | Threshold input. Ends timing cycle when voltage rises above $2V_{CC}/3$ |
| **7** | **DIS** | Discharge transistor (open collector). Used to discharge timing capacitor |
| **8** | **VCC** | Supply voltage (**+5 V to +15 V** typical) |

### 1.1.2 Internal Block Architecture

The 555 timer is composed of:
- A **Resistive Voltage Divider** (three equal $5\,\text{k}\Omega$ resistors — the source of the name "555")
- **Two Voltage Comparators** (Threshold Comparator & Trigger Comparator)
- An **SR Flip-Flop** (Set-Reset Latch)
- A **Discharge Transistor** (open-collector NPN)
- An **Output Stage** (totem-pole driver capable of sourcing/sinking 200 mA)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The 555 as a "Tug-of-War" Engine:** Imagine two referees watching a seesaw. One referee blows the whistle when the weight on the right side crosses the upper mark (Threshold Comparator), and the other blows the whistle when the weight on the left side dips below the lower mark (Trigger Comparator). The seesaw itself is the **SR flip-flop**, and a heavy hand (the discharge transistor) pulls the weight down when needed. The output is simply the tilt direction of the seesaw. This mechanical analogy perfectly mirrors how the 555 makes binary decisions based on two voltage thresholds: $\frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$ and $\frac{V_{CC}}{3}$.

### 1.2 Astable Multivibrator — The Free-Running Oscillator

**Formal Definition:** An **Astable Multivibrator** is a regenerative electronic oscillator circuit that has **no stable states** — it continuously switches between HIGH and LOW output states without any external triggering, producing a continuous train of rectangular/square waves.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The term "Astable" means **"not stable in any state."** The output perpetually alternates between HIGH and LOW, making it ideal as a clock generator, LED flasher, tone generator, or PWM source.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition — The Self-Flipping Light Switch:** Think of an astable multivibrator as a bathroom light switch rigged to a swinging door: every time the door opens, the switch flips OFF; when the door closes, the switch flips ON, and the door's motion makes it swing the other way. The result is a light that blinks on its own, forever, with no human intervention. The "door" here is the charging/discharging capacitor, and the "switch" is the SR flip-flop inside the 555.

### 1.3 Monostable Multivibrator — The One-Shot Timer

**Formal Definition:** A **Monostable Multivibrator** (also called a **One-Shot Multivibrator**) is a circuit with **one stable state** and **one quasi-stable state**. In its stable state, the output rests at a fixed level (typically HIGH). Upon receiving a **trigger pulse**, the circuit flips to its quasi-stable state for a precisely timed duration $T$, after which it automatically returns to the stable state, awaiting the next trigger.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Monostable circuits produce a fixed-width output pulse in response to an arbitrary input trigger. They are used for **pulse stretching, debouncing mechanical switches, missing-pulse detection, time-delay generation, and digital timing applications.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition — The Automatic Door:** Imagine an automatic sliding door at a supermarket. The door stays open (stable state). When you press the button (trigger), the door closes for exactly 5 seconds (quasi-stable state), then automatically opens again. If you press the button again during the closing, the timer restarts. The door does not care how long you pressed the button — it always closes for exactly 5 seconds. This is precisely how a monostable multivibrator behaves with a trigger input.

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 555 Timer — Internal Comparator Logic

The internal architecture creates two critical reference voltages through a resistive divider:

$$V_{\text{ref,upper}} = \frac{2 V_{CC}}{3} \quad \text{(Threshold reference)}$$

$$V_{\text{ref,lower}} = \frac{V_{CC}}{3} \quad \text{(Trigger reference)}$$

### 2.1.1 Comparator Operation Logic

- **Threshold Comparator (CMP1):** Compares Pin 6 voltage ($V_{\text{THR}}$) with $\frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$.
  - If $V_{\text{THR}} > \frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$ → Output = HIGH → Resets the SR Flip-Flop → Output Pin 3 = LOW, Discharge transistor ON.
- **Trigger Comparator (CMP2):** Compares Pin 2 voltage ($V_{\text{TRIG}}$) with $\frac{V_{CC}}{3}$.
  - If $V_{\text{TRIG}} < \frac{V_{CC}}{3}$ → Output = HIGH → Sets the SR Flip-Flop → Output Pin 3 = HIGH, Discharge transistor OFF.

> [!NOTE]
> The **Control Voltage Pin (Pin 5)** allows external modulation of the upper threshold $\frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$, but is normally bypassed to ground with a **0.01 µF** capacitor to filter noise.

## 2.2 Astable Multivibrator — Operation Theory

### 2.2.1 Circuit Configuration

In astable mode, the 555 is wired such that:
- Threshold (Pin 6) and Trigger (Pin 2) pins are **tied together** and connected to the timing capacitor $C$.
- A resistor $R_A$ connects $V_{CC}$ to Pin 7 (Discharge).
- A resistor $R_B$ connects Pin 7 to the capacitor $C$ (junction of Pins 2 & 6).
- The discharge transistor alternately charges and discharges $C$ through $R_A$ and $R_B$.

### 2.2.2 Operational Cycle (Two Phases)

**Phase 1 — Charging (Output HIGH):**
- The SR flip-flop is SET → Output Pin 3 = HIGH → Discharge transistor (Pin 7) is OFF.
- Capacitor $C$ charges from $V_{CC}$ through resistors $R_A$ and $R_B$ in series.
- Voltage across $C$ rises exponentially from $\frac{V_{CC}}{3}$ toward $V_{CC}$.
- Charging continues until $V_C$ reaches $\frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$ (upper threshold).

**Phase 2 — Discharging (Output LOW):**
- When $V_C > \frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$, the Threshold Comparator trips → Resets the flip-flop → Output Pin 3 = LOW.
- Discharge transistor turns ON, providing a low-impedance path to ground.
- Capacitor $C$ now discharges through $R_B$ only (since Pin 7 is internally shorted to ground via the transistor).
- $V_C$ falls exponentially from $\frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$ toward 0 V.
- Discharging continues until $V_C$ drops below $\frac{V_{CC}}{3}$ (lower threshold).
- At this moment, the Trigger Comparator trips → Sets the flip-flop → Cycle restarts.

### 2.2.3 Critical Design Parameters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two resistors?** In the astable configuration, $R_A$ is in the charging path but **NOT** in the discharging path. The discharge transistor shorts out $R_A$ during discharge. This means the **charging time** is determined by $R_A + R_B$, but the **discharging time** is determined by $R_B$ alone. This asymmetry naturally produces a **non-50% duty cycle** unless $R_A \ll R_B$.

**Frequency of Oscillation:**

$$f = \frac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B) \cdot C}$$

**Time HIGH (Charging Duration $t_H$):**

$$t_H = 0.693 \cdot (R_A + R_B) \cdot C$$

**Time LOW (Discharging Duration $t_L$):**

$$t_L = 0.693 \cdot R_B \cdot C$$

**Total Period:**

$$T = t_H + t_L = 0.693 \cdot (R_A + 2R_B) \cdot C$$

**Duty Cycle (D):**

$$D = \frac{t_H}{T} = \frac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** A common mistake is to use $D = \frac{R_B}{R_A + 2R_B}$. This is the **fractional LOW time**, not the duty cycle. The duty cycle is the **fractional HIGH time**, given by $\frac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B}$. Always clarify what "duty cycle" means in your answer.

## 2.3 Monostable Multivibrator — Operation Theory

### 2.3.1 Circuit Configuration

In monostable mode, the 555 is wired such that:
- Threshold (Pin 6) is connected to the timing capacitor $C$ to ground.
- Trigger (Pin 2) receives external **negative-going trigger pulses**.
- A single resistor $R$ connects $V_{CC}$ to Pin 7 (Discharge).
- Pin 7 connects to the same capacitor $C$ (junction with Pin 6).
- No external resistor is connected between Pin 7 and $C$ — only $R$ from $V_{CC}$.

### 2.3.2 Operational States

**Stable State (Quiescent — Output LOW):**
- At power-on or after a timing cycle ends, the SR flip-flop is RESET → Output Pin 3 = LOW.
- Discharge transistor is ON, holding capacitor $C$ at 0 V.
- $V_C$ (Pin 6) is below $\frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$ — no threshold trip.
- The circuit sits in this state indefinitely until a trigger arrives.

**Quasi-Stable State (Output HIGH — Timing Phase):**
- A **negative-going trigger pulse** on Pin 2 with amplitude below $\frac{V_{CC}}{3}$ SETS the flip-flop.
- Output Pin 3 goes HIGH; discharge transistor turns OFF.
- Capacitor $C$ now charges through resistor $R$ from $V_{CC}$.
- $V_C$ rises exponentially: $V_C(t) = V_{CC} \left(1 - e^{-t/RC}\right)$.
- When $V_C$ reaches $\frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$, the Threshold Comparator RESETS the flip-flop.
- Output returns LOW; discharge transistor turns ON, rapidly discharging $C$.
- The circuit returns to the stable state and waits for the next trigger.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Property:** The output pulse width $T$ is **independent of the trigger pulse width** and depends only on the RC time constant. This makes the monostable a **pulse-stretcher** or **pulse-standardizer**.

**Pulse Width Formula:**

$$T = 1.1 \cdot R \cdot C$$

This is derived from solving $V_C(t) = \frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$ for $t$:

$$V_{CC} \left(1 - e^{-T/RC}\right) = \frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$$

$$1 - e^{-T/RC} = \frac{2}{3}$$

$$e^{-T/RC} = \frac{1}{3}$$

$$T = RC \cdot \ln(3) \approx 1.0986 \cdot RC \approx 1.1 \cdot RC$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Reference Table

| Parameter | Astable Mode | Monostable Mode |
|:----------|:-------------|:----------------|
| **Charging Path** | $V_{CC} \to R_A \to R_B \to C$ | $V_{CC} \to R \to C$ |
| **Discharging Path** | $R_B \to \text{Pin 7 (transistor)} \to GND$ | $\text{Pin 7 (transistor)} \to GND$ |
| **Charging Time $t_H$** | $0.693 \cdot (R_A + R_B) \cdot C$ | $1.1 \cdot R \cdot C$ (full pulse) |
| **Discharging Time $t_L$** | $0.693 \cdot R_B \cdot C$ | Very small (transistor saturated) |
| **Total Period $T$** | $0.693 \cdot (R_A + 2R_B) \cdot C$ | $1.1 \cdot R \cdot C$ |
| **Frequency $f$** | $\frac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B) C}$ | $\frac{1}{1.1 RC}$ (trigger rate) |
| **Duty Cycle $D$** | $\frac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B}$ | N/A (single pulse) |
| **Trigger Source** | None (self-triggered) | External negative pulse on Pin 2 |
| **Stable State** | None (oscillates) | Output LOW (after reset) |
| **Quasi-stable State** | Both HIGH and LOW | Output HIGH for duration $T$ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Astable:** LED flashers, tone generators, PWM controllers, clock generators for digital systems, servo motor control signals, alarm circuits, function generators.
- **Monostable:** Switch debouncing, missing pulse detectors, sequential timers, pulse-width measurement, frequency dividers (when cascaded), timer circuits, automatic reset circuits.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Lab Component Values:** For lab simulations, typical values used are $R_A = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_B = 6.8\,\text{k}\Omega$, $C = 0.1\,\mu\text{F}$ for astable, and $R = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, $C = 0.1\,\mu\text{F}$ for monostable.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Analysis

## 3.1 Derivation of the Astable Charging Time

The capacitor charges through $R_A + R_B$ in series from $V_{CC}$. The general RC charging equation with initial voltage $V_0$ and final voltage $V_{CC}$ is:

$$V_C(t) = V_{CC} - (V_{CC} - V_0) \cdot e^{-t/[(R_A + R_B)C]}$$

**Step 1:** In astable operation, charging begins when $V_C = \frac{V_{CC}}{3}$ (the moment the trigger comparator fires). So $V_0 = \frac{V_{CC}}{3}$.

$$V_C(t) = V_{CC} - \left(V_{CC} - \frac{V_{CC}}{3}\right) e^{-t/[(R_A + R_B)C]}$$

$$V_C(t) = V_{CC} - \frac{2V_{CC}}{3} \cdot e^{-t/[(R_A + R_B)C]}$$

**Step 2:** Charging ends when $V_C = \frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$ (the moment the threshold comparator fires).

$$\frac{2V_{CC}}{3} = V_{CC} - \frac{2V_{CC}}{3} \cdot e^{-t_H/[(R_A + R_B)C]}$$

**Step 3:** Subtract $V_{CC}$ from both sides:

$$-\frac{V_{CC}}{3} = -\frac{2V_{CC}}{3} \cdot e^{-t_H/[(R_A + R_B)C]}$$

**Step 4:** Multiply both sides by $-\frac{3}{2V_{CC}}$:

$$\frac{1}{2} = e^{-t_H/[(R_A + R_B)C]}$$

**Step 5:** Take natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = -\frac{t_H}{(R_A + R_B)C}$$

$$-\ln(2) = -\frac{t_H}{(R_A + R_B)C}$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $t_H$:

$$t_H = \ln(2) \cdot (R_A + R_B) \cdot C$$

$$\boxed{t_H = 0.693 \cdot (R_A + R_B) \cdot C}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Astable Discharging Time

During discharge, the capacitor discharges from $\frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$ toward 0 V through resistor $R_B$ only (the discharge transistor shorts $R_A$ to ground).

**Step 1:** The general RC discharge equation:

$$V_C(t) = V_{\text{initial}} \cdot e^{-t/(R_B C)}$$

**Step 2:** With $V_{\text{initial}} = \frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$:

$$V_C(t) = \frac{2V_{CC}}{3} \cdot e^{-t/(R_B C)}$$

**Step 3:** Discharging ends when $V_C = \frac{V_{CC}}{3}$:

$$\frac{V_{CC}}{3} = \frac{2V_{CC}}{3} \cdot e^{-t_L/(R_B C)}$$

**Step 4:** Simplify:

$$\frac{1}{2} = e^{-t_L/(R_B C)}$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $t_L$:

$$\boxed{t_L = \ln(2) \cdot R_B \cdot C = 0.693 \cdot R_B \cdot C}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Monostable Pulse Width

**Step 1:** Capacitor $C$ charges from 0 V (just discharged by transistor) toward $V_{CC}$ through $R$.

$$V_C(t) = V_{CC} \left(1 - e^{-t/RC}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Pulse ends when $V_C = \frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$:

$$\frac{2V_{CC}}{3} = V_{CC} \left(1 - e^{-T/RC}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Divide both sides by $V_{CC}$:

$$\frac{2}{3} = 1 - e^{-T/RC}$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange:

$$e^{-T/RC} = 1 - \frac{2}{3} = \frac{1}{3}$$

**Step 5:** Take natural logarithm:

$$-\frac{T}{RC} = \ln\left(\frac{1}{3}\right) = -\ln(3)$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $T$:

$$\boxed{T = \ln(3) \cdot R \cdot C \approx 1.1 \cdot RC}$$

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (Astable)

**Problem:** Design an astable multivibrator using 555 timer with frequency $f = 1\,\text{kHz}$ and duty cycle $D = 60\%$. Take $C = 0.1\,\mu\text{F}$.

**Step 1:** Use the duty cycle formula:

$$D = \frac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B} = 0.60$$

$$R_A + R_B = 0.60(R_A + 2R_B)$$

$$R_A + R_B = 0.60 R_A + 1.20 R_B$$

$$0.40 R_A = 0.20 R_B$$

$$R_B = 2 R_A$$

**Step 2:** Use the frequency formula:

$$f = \frac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B) C}$$

$$1000 = \frac{1.44}{(R_A + 4R_A)(0.1 \times 10^{-6})}$$

$$1000 = \frac{1.44}{5 R_A \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$5 R_A \times 10^{-7} = \frac{1.44}{1000} = 1.44 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$R_A = \frac{1.44 \times 10^{-3}}{5 \times 10^{-7}} = \frac{1.44}{5} \times 10^{4} = 2880\,\Omega \approx 2.88\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 3:** Find $R_B$:

$$R_B = 2 \times 2.88\,\text{k}\Omega = 5.76\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 4:** Verification:

$$t_H = 0.693 \times (2.88 + 5.76)\,\text{k}\Omega \times 0.1\,\mu\text{F} = 0.693 \times 8.64 \times 10^{3} \times 10^{-7}$$

$$t_H = 0.693 \times 8.64 \times 10^{-4} = 5.987 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{s} \approx 0.599\,\text{ms}$$

$$t_L = 0.693 \times 5.76 \times 10^{3} \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6} = 0.693 \times 5.76 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$t_L = 3.99 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{s} \approx 0.399\,\text{ms}$$

$$T = 0.599 + 0.399 = 0.998\,\text{ms} \approx 1\,\text{ms}$$

$$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{0.998 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 1002\,\text{Hz} \approx 1\,\text{kHz} \quad \checkmark$$

$$D = \frac{0.599}{0.998} = 0.6002 = 60.02\% \quad \checkmark$$

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (Monostable)

**Problem:** Design a monostable multivibrator using 555 timer to generate an output pulse of width $T = 10\,\text{ms}$. Choose a suitable $C$ and find $R$.

**Step 1:** Choose a standard capacitor value. Let $C = 1\,\mu\text{F}$.

**Step 2:** Apply the pulse width formula:

$$T = 1.1 \cdot R \cdot C$$

$$10 \times 10^{-3} = 1.1 \times R \times 1 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$R = \frac{10 \times 10^{-3}}{1.1 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{10^{-2}}{1.1 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{10^{4}}{1.1}$$

$$R = 9090.9\,\Omega \approx 9.1\,\text{k}\Omega \text{ (standard value)}$$

**Step 3:** Verification with $R = 9.1\,\text{k}\Omega$:

$$T = 1.1 \times 9100 \times 10^{-6} = 10.01 \times 10^{-3} = 10.01\,\text{ms} \approx 10\,\text{ms} \quad \checkmark$$

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    GND[GND Pin 1] --> DIV
    DIV --> REF1[Reference 2VCC by 3]
    DIV --> REF2[Reference VCC by 3]
    REF1 --> CMP1[Threshold Comparator]
    REF2 --> CMP2[Trigger Comparator]
    THR[THR Pin 6] --> CMP1
    TRIG[TRIG Pin 2] --> CMP2
    CMP1 --> RST[Reset SR Flip Flop]
    CMP2 --> SET[Set SR Flip Flop]
    RST --> FF[SR Flip Flop]
    SET --> FF
    FF --> OUT[Output Buffer]
    OUT --> OUTPIN[OUT Pin 3]
    FF --> DRV[Discharge Driver]
    DRV --> DIS[DIS Pin 7]
    RST_EXT[RESET Pin 4] --> FF
    CONT[CONT Pin 5] --> REF1
```

```mermaid
    RB --> NODEC[Capacitor Junction]
    NODEC --> CAP[Capacitor C 0.1uF]
    NODEC --> PIN2[Pin 2 Trigger]
    NODEC --> PIN6[Pin 6 Threshold]
    CAP --> GND_1[Ground]
    PIN7 --> TRAN[Internal NPN to GND]
    TRAN --> GND_1
    PIN2 --> CMP2A[Trigger CMP trips at VCC by 3]
    PIN6 --> CMP1A[Threshold CMP trips at 2VCC by 3]
    CMP1A --> FF_A[SR Flip Flop]

```

## 4.3 Monostable Multivibrator Circuit Diagram (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VCC_M[VCC plus 9V] --> R_M[Timing Resistor R 9.1k]

    NODEM --> PIN6M[Pin 6 Threshold]
    NODEM --> CAPM[Capacitor C 1uF]
```

## 4.5 Signal Flow Architecture — How the 555 Generates a Pulse

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([Trigger Arrives Pin 2 LOW]) --> DETECT{Trigger Comparator}
    DETECT -->|V TRIG less than VCC by 3| SET_FF[Set SR Flip Flop]
    SET_FF --> OUT_HI[Output Pin 3 goes HIGH]
    OUT_HI --> DIS_OFF[Discharge Transistor OFF]
    DIS_OFF --> CHARGE[Capacitor Charges via R]
    CHARGE --> CHECK{V C reaches 2VCC by 3}
    CHECK -->|No| CHARGE
    CHECK -->|Yes| RESET_FF[Reset SR Flip Flop]
    RESET_FF --> OUT_LO[Output Pin 3 goes LOW]
    OUT_LO --> DIS_ON[Discharge Transistor ON]
    DIS_ON --> DISCHG[Capacitor Rapidly Discharges]
    DISCHG --> END([Return to Stable State])
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Astable Multivibrator. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Model Answer:**
An **Astable Multivibrator** is a free-running oscillator circuit that has **no stable state** — its output continuously switches between HIGH and LOW states without any external trigger, producing a continuous train of rectangular pulses. When implemented using the **IC 555 timer**, the frequency of oscillation is given by:

$$f = \frac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B)C}$$

It is widely used as a square wave generator, clock pulse source, and LED flasher.

### Question 2: What is the function of the discharge transistor (Pin 7) in a 555 timer? [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**
The **discharge transistor** at Pin 7 is an open-collector NPN transistor that provides a low-impedance path to ground for the external timing capacitor when activated. It is controlled by the SR flip-flop inside the 555:
- When the flip-flop is **RESET** (output LOW), the transistor is **ON**, discharging the capacitor.
- When the flip-flop is **SET** (output HIGH), the transistor is **OFF**, allowing the capacitor to charge through external resistors.

In astable mode, this transistor alternately charges and discharges $C$, producing oscillation. In monostable mode, it rapidly resets the capacitor at the end of the timing cycle.

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: Design and Analyze an Astable Multivibrator Using 555 Timer [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Draw the circuit diagram of an astable multivibrator using 555 timer and explain its operation with neat waveforms.**

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Diagram:**

```
         VCC (+9V)
          |
          |
         [RA] 1k
          |
          |-------- Pin 7 (DIS)
          |
         [RB] 6.8k
          |
          +-----+------ Pin 6 (THR)
          |     |
          |    Pin 2 (TRIG)
          |
         [C] 0.1uF
          |
         GND
```

**Operation:**

**State 1 (Charging — Output HIGH):**
Assume at $t = 0$, the capacitor is just discharged to $\frac{V_{CC}}{3}$ and the trigger comparator has just set the flip-flop. The output Pin 3 goes HIGH, and the discharge transistor at Pin 7 is OFF. Capacitor $C$ now charges through resistors $R_A$ and $R_B$ in series from $V_{CC}$. The voltage across $C$ rises exponentially:

$$V_C(t) = V_{CC} - \frac{2V_{CC}}{3} e^{-t/[(R_A+R_B)C]}$$

Charging continues until $V_C = \frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$.

**State 2 (Discharging — Output LOW):**
At $V_C = \frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$, the threshold comparator resets the flip-flop. Output Pin 3 goes LOW, and the discharge transistor turns ON, connecting Pin 7 to ground. The capacitor now discharges through $R_B$ only (since Pin 7 is shorted to ground). The voltage falls:

$$V_C(t) = \frac{2V_{CC}}{3} \cdot e^{-t/(R_B C)}$$

When $V_C$ drops to $\frac{V_{CC}}{3}$, the trigger comparator sets the flip-flop again, and the cycle repeats.

**Waveforms:** Output is a rectangular wave with $t_H = 0.693(R_A + R_B)C$ and $t_L = 0.693 R_B C$.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**An astable multivibrator using 555 timer is designed with $R_A = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_B = 6.8\,\text{k}\Omega$, and $C = 0.1\,\mu\text{F}$. Calculate the frequency of oscillation and duty cycle.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Calculate the HIGH time (charging duration):

$$t_H = 0.693 \cdot (R_A + R_B) \cdot C = 0.693 \times (1000 + 6800) \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$t_H = 0.693 \times 7800 \times 10^{-7} = 5.4054 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{s} = 0.5405\,\text{ms}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the LOW time (discharging duration):

$$t_L = 0.693 \cdot R_B \cdot C = 0.693 \times 6800 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$t_L = 0.693 \times 6.8 \times 10^{-4} = 4.7124 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{s} = 0.4712\,\text{ms}$$

**Step 3:** Total period:

$$T = t_H + t_L = 0.5405 + 0.4712 = 1.0117\,\text{ms}$$

**Step 4:** Frequency:

$$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{1.0117 \times 10^{-3}} = 988.4\,\text{Hz} \approx 988\,\text{Hz}$$

**Step 5:** Duty cycle:

$$D = \frac{t_H}{T} = \frac{0.5405}{1.0117} = 0.5342 = 53.42\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Wrong charging path:** Students often write that charging occurs through $R_B$ only. **The charging path is $R_A + R_B$ in series.**
> - **Confusing duty cycle definition:** Some students report $D = 53.4\%$ as the LOW-time fraction. Verify whether the question asks for HIGH or LOW duty cycle.
> - **Forgetting the factor 0.693:** Always derive it as $\ln(2) = 0.693$, or use 1.44 directly in the frequency formula.

### Question B: Design and Analyze a Monostable Multivibrator Using 555 Timer [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With the help of a circuit diagram, explain the working of a monostable multivibrator using 555 timer. Derive the expression for the output pulse width.**

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Diagram:**

```
         VCC (+9V)
          |
         [R] 10k
          |
          +------ Pin 7 (DIS)
          |
          +------ Pin 6 (THR)
          |
         [C] 1uF
          |
         GND

External Trigger → Pin 2 (TRIG)
Output           → Pin 3 (OUT)
```

**Working:**

**Stable State (Output LOW):**
At power-on, the discharge transistor (Pin 7) is ON, keeping the capacitor $C$ fully discharged ($V_C = 0$). Since $V_C < \frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$, the threshold comparator output is LOW. The SR flip-flop is in the RESET state, and the output Pin 3 remains LOW. The circuit stays in this stable state indefinitely.

**Triggered State (Output HIGH — Quasi-stable):**
When an external **negative-going trigger pulse** is applied to Pin 2 with $V_{\text{TRIG}} < \frac{V_{CC}}{3}$, the trigger comparator output goes HIGH, **setting** the SR flip-flop. The output Pin 3 goes HIGH, and the discharge transistor turns OFF. Capacitor $C$ now begins charging through $R$ toward $V_{CC}$.

**Return to Stable State:**
The voltage across $C$ follows:

$$V_C(t) = V_{CC} \left(1 - e^{-t/RC}\right)$$

When $V_C$ reaches $\frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$, the threshold comparator output goes HIGH, **resetting** the flip-flop. Output Pin 3 goes LOW, the discharge transistor turns ON, and $C$ rapidly discharges back to 0 V. The circuit returns to its stable state, ready for the next trigger.

**Derivation of Pulse Width:**

Setting $V_C(T) = \frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$:

$$\frac{2V_{CC}}{3} = V_{CC} \left(1 - e^{-T/RC}\right)$$

$$e^{-T/RC} = \frac{1}{3}$$

$$T = RC \ln(3) = 1.0986 \cdot RC \approx 1.1 \cdot RC$$

$$\boxed{T = 1.1 \cdot R \cdot C}$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Design a monostable multivibrator using 555 timer to produce an output pulse of width 5 ms. Choose $C = 0.5\,\mu\text{F}$ and verify with a suitable trigger pulse waveform.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Apply the pulse width formula:

$$T = 1.1 \cdot R \cdot C$$

$$5 \times 10^{-3} = 1.1 \times R \times 0.5 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $R$:

$$R = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{1.1 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{5.5 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$R = \frac{5}{5.5} \times 10^{4} = 0.9091 \times 10^{4} = 9090.9\,\Omega$$

**Step 3:** Choose nearest standard value:

$$R \approx 9.1\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 4:** Verification:

$$T = 1.1 \times 9100 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-6} = 1.1 \times 4.55 \times 10^{-3} = 5.005 \times 10^{-3} = 5.005\,\text{ms} \approx 5\,\text{ms} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5:** Trigger and Output Waveform Sketch:

```
Trigger (Pin 2):  ___     ___              ___
                     |_____|
                     ↓
Output (Pin 3):   ____________     ____________
                  |            |   |            |
                  ←  5 ms  →        ←  5 ms  →
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Monostable Pitfalls:**
> - **Forgetting the constant:** Students sometimes use $T = RC$ instead of $T = 1.1 RC$. The factor $\ln(3) \approx 1.1$ comes from the $\frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$ threshold level — **never omit it.**
> - **Active-LOW trigger:** The trigger must be a **negative-going pulse** that pulls Pin 2 below $\frac{V_{CC}}{3}$. Stating "a HIGH trigger" loses full marks.
> - **Stable state confusion:** Some students incorrectly state that the stable state is HIGH. The monostable output rests LOW between triggers; HIGH is the quasi-stable (timing) state.
> - **No retriggering during pulse:** If a new trigger arrives during the 5 ms timing interval, it is **ignored** in the basic configuration. Mention this for full credit.
