## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam**

- **555 Timer Block:** Two comparators, one SR flip-flop, one discharge transistor, one output buffer. Internal divider creates $\frac{V_{CC}}{3}$ and $\frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$ references.
- **Pin 8 = VCC**, **Pin 1 = GND**, **Pin 3 = Output**, **Pin 4 = Reset (active LOW)**, **Pin 5 = Control Voltage**, **Pin 7 = Discharge**, **Pin 2 = Trigger (active LOW)**, **Pin 6 = Threshold**.
- **Astable Output:** No stable state, oscillates continuously; $f = \frac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B)C}$; charging through $R_A + R_B$, discharging through $R_B$ only.
- **Monostable Output:** One stable state (LOW); trigger produces a HIGH pulse of width $T = 1.1 RC$; trigger must be **negative-going** on Pin 2.
- **Key Constants to Memorize:** $\ln(2) = 0.693$, $\ln(3) = 1.0986 \approx 1.1$, and $\frac{1.44}{0.693} \approx 2.0795$ (the ratio used to derive frequency from period).
- **Duty Cycle Formula:** $D = \frac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B}$ (astable HIGH fraction). Can never be 50% or less in the standard 555 astable configuration; $D > 50\%$ always.
- **Duty Cycle < 50% Trick:** Add a diode across $R_B$ (anode at Pin 7, cathode at the junction) so charging and discharging both occur through $R_A$ only — produces $D \approx 50\%$.
- **Control Voltage Pin (Pin 5):** Bypassed with **0.01 µF** to ground for noise immunity; can be used to **frequency-modulate** the astable output.
- **Reset Pin (Pin 4):** Tie to VCC if not used; pulling LOW immediately resets the output and discharges the capacitor.
- **Output Current Limit:** Pin 3 can source or sink up to **200 mA**, making it capable of directly driving relays, LEDs, and small speakers.
- **Supply Voltage:** Standard operation from **+5 V to +15 V**; the bipolar 555 (e.g., NE555) has different output current capabilities than the CMOS 7555.
- **Astable vs Monostable Comparison:** Astable = continuous oscillation, no trigger needed; Monostable = one-shot, needs external trigger, returns to stable state.
- **Lab Simulation Tip:** In LTspice or Multisim, always include the **internal model of the 555** (e.g., LM555/NE555); check the discharge path carefully — the simulation often misses the asymmetric charging/discharging if Pin 7 is miswired.
- **Hardware Tip:** Always use a **0.01 µF ceramic decoupling capacitor** between VCC and GND (close to the IC) to suppress supply noise, and a **0.1 µF** on the Control Voltage pin (Pin 5) to prevent false triggering.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Waveform Generators Using Op Amps: Triangular and Sawtooth
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAEL508 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS AND SIMULATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Waveform Generators Using Op Amps: Triangular and Sawtooth

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Waveform Generator** is an electronic circuit that produces periodic, non-sinusoidal signals (such as square, triangular, and sawtooth waves) without requiring any external input signal — the output is generated purely through the action of an active device (op-amp) combined with passive RC components operating in a positive-feedback loop.

A **Triangular Wave Generator (TWG)** is an op-amp based signal generator that produces a symmetrical triangular waveform by cascading a **Schmitt Trigger (regenerative comparator)** with an **Active Integrator** in a closed-loop configuration. The triangular output is characterized by a linear rise and a linear fall of equal duration.

A **Sawtooth Wave Generator (SWG)** is a modified version of the triangular generator in which the charge and discharge paths of the integrator are made asymmetric using **diode steering** or **un-equal resistors**, producing a linear ramp with a sudden (fast) flyback — mathematically a *non-symmetric triangular wave*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCAEL508 – Module 2):** Students must be able to *design, simulate, and experimentally verify* triangular and sawtooth wave generators using op-amps (μA741 / LM741), to *plot the output waveform* using CRO / DSO, and to *calculate the frequency of oscillation* from component values.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Bucket and Swing" Analogy**

Imagine a **swing** in a playground. Someone pushes the swing gently each time it reaches a peak. Now imagine a **bucket with a hole** being filled and emptied alternately:

- The **comparator (Schmitt trigger)** is the *referee* — it constantly watches the bucket's water level (the integrator's output) and shouts "FULL!" or "EMPTY!" the moment a threshold is crossed. Its voice is either a *loud positive shout* or a *loud negative shout* — that is its square-wave output.
- The **integrator** is the *bucket itself* — when the square wave is +V, water flows in (output ramps down linearly); when the square wave is –V, water flows out (output ramps up linearly). The bucket's level over time traces out a perfect **triangle**.
- The **triangular output is fed back** to the referee's eyes. So the bucket and referee work *together in a loop* — the moment the bucket hits a threshold, the referee reverses, and the bucket starts filling the other way. This continuous, self-sustaining dance is what produces the oscillation.

For the **sawtooth**: simply give the bucket a *wide tap* in one direction and a *tiny tap* in the other. The fill is fast, the empty is slow (or vice-versa) — creating an asymmetric, ramp-like wave.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Key Metrics (Bolded)

- **Threshold voltage of Schmitt trigger:** $V_{th} = \pm \beta V_{sat}$, where $\beta = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}$
- **Saturation voltage of op-amp (μA741):** $V_{sat} \approx \pm 13\,\text{V}$ (typical with $\pm 15\,\text{V}$ supply)
- **Standard integrator time constant:** $\tau = R_i C_f$
- **Time period of triangular wave:** $T = \dfrac{4 R_1 C_f R_i}{R_2}$ (in the standard cascade form)
- **Units:** Time in **seconds (s)**, Frequency in **Hertz (Hz)**, Resistance in **Ohms (Ω)**, Capacitance in **Farads (F)**, Voltage in **Volts (V)**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Architecture — Building Block Logic

The waveform generator is a **two-stage feedback system** that exploits the regenerative (positive feedback) action of an op-amp:

**Stage 1 — Regenerative Comparator (Schmitt Trigger)**
- An op-amp with the inverting input grounded and the non-inverting input receiving a feedback signal.
- A voltage divider ($R_1$, $R_2$) samples the op-amp's output and feeds a fraction back to the non-inverting terminal.
- The output saturates to either $+V_{sat}$ or $-V_{sat}$ depending on the polarity of the input.
- The switching thresholds are set by $\pm V_{th} = \pm \beta \cdot V_{sat}$, where $\beta = R_2 / (R_1 + R_2)$.

**Stage 2 — Active Integrator**
- An op-amp with a feedback capacitor $C_f$ and input resistor $R_i$.
- The output is the time integral of the input (inverted): $V_{out} = -\dfrac{1}{R_i C_f} \displaystyle\int V_{in}\, dt$.
- When fed with a square wave of amplitude $V_{sat}$, the output becomes a **triangular wave** with slope $\pm V_{sat}/(R_i C_f)$.

**Closed-Loop Action (Why and How)**
- The triangular output of the integrator is fed back to the non-inverting input of the comparator.
- When the triangle crosses $+V_{th}$, the comparator output flips to $-V_{sat}$.
- This negative voltage at the integrator input causes the triangle to ramp **upward** linearly.
- When the triangle crosses $-V_{th}$, the comparator flips back to $+V_{sat}$, and the cycle repeats.
- The system is **astable** — it never sits in a stable DC state, oscillating continuously.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | Physical Reasoning |
|---|---|
| Why use positive feedback? | It creates **hysteresis** — the comparator switches cleanly and rapidly between two states, eliminating false triggering due to noise on the triangular ramp. |
| Why use an integrator? | A square wave integrated over time produces a **linear ramp** — mathematics of integration guarantees a triangular output if the input is constant during each half-period. |
| Why feed triangular back? | This completes the **closed loop**, making the system self-oscillating. The triangle itself decides when the comparator flips — no external trigger is needed. |
| Why zener diodes at the comparator output? | They **clamp** the square wave amplitude to $\pm V_Z$ (e.g., 5.1 V) instead of $\pm V_{sat}$, giving **precise amplitude control** and predictable frequency. |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these formulas — they are the only ones tested in the ESE for this experiment.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Boundary / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Schmitt Threshold | $V_{th} = \beta \cdot V_{sat} = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_{sat}$ | $\beta < 1$ for stable oscillation |
| 2 | Integrator Slope | $\dfrac{dV_{out}}{dt} = \pm \dfrac{V_{sat}}{R_i C_f}$ | Sign depends on input polarity |
| 3 | Peak Triangular Amplitude | $V_p = \beta \cdot V_{sat}$ | Equals the Schmitt threshold |
| 4 | Half-Period (T/2) | $\dfrac{T}{2} = R_i C_f \cdot \beta = R_i C_f \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Time for one ramp |
| 5 | Time Period (TWG) | $T = \dfrac{4 R_1 R_i C_f}{R_2}$ | Valid when $R_2 \ll R_1$ |
| 6 | Frequency (TWG) | $f = \dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{R_2}{4 R_1 R_i C_f}$ | Symmetric waveform |
| 7 | Sawtooth Ramp Time (slow) | $T_1 = R_{slow} C_f \cdot \beta$ | Slow charging half |
| 8 | Sawtooth Flyback Time (fast) | $T_2 = R_{fast} C_f \cdot \beta$ | Fast discharge half |
| 9 | Sawtooth Frequency | $f = \dfrac{1}{T_1 + T_2} = \dfrac{1}{\beta C_f (R_{slow} + R_{fast})}$ | Asymmetric waveform |
| 10 | Duty Cycle (Sawtooth) | $D = \dfrac{T_1}{T_1 + T_2}$ | Usually $\ll 1$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Triangular and sawtooth waves are extensively used in **time-base generators of oscilloscopes and CRTs**, **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation) controllers**, **function generators**, **ADC test signals**, **switched-mode power supplies (SMPS)**, and **music synthesizers** (VCOs). The op-amp based version is preferred for **low-frequency, high-linearity** applications (audio range to ~100 kHz).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Application

- **Cathode Ray Oscilloscope (CRO):** The horizontal sweep of the electron beam across the screen is a precisely linear **sawtooth wave**.
- **Class-D Audio Amplifiers:** The triangle wave acts as the carrier, and the input audio modulates the comparator's switching — generating a PWM output.
- **Inverter Control Circuits (e.g., SPWM):** A triangular reference is compared with a sinusoidal modulating wave to generate firing pulses for power MOSFETs/IGBTs.
- **Voltage-Controlled Oscillators (VCOs):** Replacing the input resistor $R_i$ with a JFET or photoresistor makes the frequency voltage- or light-dependent.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Threshold Voltage of the Schmitt Trigger

Consider the comparator stage with $R_1$ from output to inverting input (which is grounded through reference) and $R_2$ providing positive feedback to the non-inverting input.

By superposition, the voltage at the non-inverting input is:

$$V^{+} = \dfrac{R_1 \cdot V_{in} + R_2 \cdot V_{out}}{R_1 + R_2}$$

The comparator flips when $V^+ = 0$ (since the inverting input is at 0 V):

$$\dfrac{R_1 \cdot V_{in} + R_2 \cdot V_{out}}{R_1 + R_2} = 0$$

Solving for $V_{in}$ (the input needed to flip the comparator):

$$V_{in} = -\dfrac{R_2}{R_1} \cdot V_{out}$$

When $V_{out} = +V_{sat}$, the upper threshold is:
$$V_{th}^{+} = -\dfrac{R_2}{R_1} \cdot (+V_{sat}) = -\beta V_{sat}$$

When $V_{out} = -V_{sat}$, the lower threshold is:
$$V_{th}^{-} = -\dfrac{R_2}{R_1} \cdot (-V_{sat}) = +\beta V_{sat}$$

Therefore, the threshold magnitudes are:
$$\vert V_{th} \vert = \beta V_{sat} = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1} V_{sat} \quad \text{(using the alternative feedback convention)}$$

> **Note:** Depending on which resistor is the feedback element, the formula uses either $R_2/R_1$ or $R_2/(R_1+R_2)$. The **KTU 2024 convention** uses $\beta = R_2 / R_1$ for the simplified form. The full derivation is given above so that you can use either form correctly.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Time Period of the Triangular Wave Generator

The integrator's output during one half-cycle is a linear ramp:

$$V_{out}(t) = V_{th} + \left(-\dfrac{1}{R_i C_f}\right) \cdot V_{sat} \cdot t$$

At the end of the half-cycle, $V_{out}$ must change from $+V_{th}$ to $-V_{th}$. The required change in voltage is:

$$\Delta V = -V_{th} - (+V_{th}) = -2V_{th}$$

Equating:
$$-2 V_{th} = -\dfrac{V_{sat}}{R_i C_f} \cdot \dfrac{T}{2}$$

Solving for T/2:
$$\dfrac{T}{2} = \dfrac{2 V_{th} R_i C_f}{V_{sat}} = \dfrac{2 \beta V_{sat} R_i C_f}{V_{sat}} = 2 \beta R_i C_f$$

Therefore:
$$T = 4 \beta R_i C_f$$

Substituting $\beta = R_2/R_1$:

$$\boxed{T = \dfrac{4 R_2 R_i C_f}{R_1}}$$

$$\boxed{f = \dfrac{R_1}{4 R_2 R_i C_f}}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Frequency of the Sawtooth Wave Generator

In the sawtooth generator, the integrator has **two different input resistors** selected by diodes:

- During the positive half-cycle of the square wave, diode $D_1$ is **OFF** and $D_2$ is **ON** → charging through $R_{fast}$ → fast ramp.
- During the negative half-cycle, $D_1$ is **ON** and $D_2$ is **OFF** → discharging through $R_{slow}$ → slow ramp.

The slow ramp time:
$$T_1 = 2 \beta R_{slow} C_f = \dfrac{2 R_2 R_{slow} C_f}{R_1}$$

The fast ramp time:
$$T_2 = 2 \beta R_{fast} C_f = \dfrac{2 R_2 R_{fast} C_f}{R_1}$$

Total time period:
$$T = T_1 + T_2 = \dfrac{2 R_2 C_f (R_{slow} + R_{fast})}{R_1}$$

Frequency:
$$\boxed{f = \dfrac{R_1}{2 R_2 C_f (R_{slow} + R_{fast})}}$$

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Typical Problem)

**Problem:** Design a triangular wave generator using op-amp μA741 with $R_1 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 5\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_i = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, $C_f = 0.1\,\mu\text{F}$, and $V_{sat} = \pm 13\,\text{V}$. Calculate the frequency and peak-to-peak output voltage.

**Step 1:** Calculate feedback fraction $\beta$.

$$\beta = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1} = \dfrac{5\,\text{k}\Omega}{10\,\text{k}\Omega} = 0.5$$

**Step 2:** Calculate threshold voltage (peak triangular amplitude).

$$V_{th} = \beta V_{sat} = 0.5 \times 13 = 6.5\,\text{V}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate peak-to-peak triangular voltage.

$$V_{pp} = 2 V_{th} = 2 \times 6.5 = 13\,\text{V}$$

**Step 4:** Calculate time period.

$$T = \dfrac{4 R_2 R_i C_f}{R_1} = \dfrac{4 \times 5 \times 10^3 \times 10 \times 10^3 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}}{10 \times 10^3}$$

$$T = \dfrac{4 \times 5 \times 10 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{6}}{10} = \dfrac{20 \times 10^{-6}}{10} = 2 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{s} = 2\,\text{ms}$$

**Step 5:** Calculate frequency.

$$f = \dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{1}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 500\,\text{Hz}$$

**Final Answer:** Frequency = **500 Hz**, $V_{pp}$ = **13 V**.

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing Component Values

| Design Goal | Action |
|---|---|
| Increase frequency | Decrease $R_i$ or $C_f$ or $R_2$; or increase $R_1$ |
| Increase amplitude | Increase $\beta$ (increase $R_2/R_1$) — but $\beta < 1$ required |
| Convert to sawtooth | Add diode-steered parallel resistor at integrator input |
| Stabilize amplitude | Add back-to-back zeners (5.1 V typical) at comparator output |
| Make it a VCO | Replace $R_i$ with a JFET or photoresistor (LDR) |
| Reduce distortion | Use LF353 / OP-07 (low-offset, high-slew-rate) instead of μA741 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Triangular Wave Generator)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[DC Power Supply Plus Minus 15V] --> B[Op Amp 1 Comparator]
    A --> C[Op Amp 2 Integrator]
    D[Triangular Wave Output Vtri] --> E[Feedback Path R2 to Plus Input]
    B --> F[Square Wave Output Vs]
    F --> G[Input Resistor Ri of Integrator]
    G --> C
    C --> D
    D --> B
    H[Zener Diodes Back to Back] --> B
    style A fill:#1e90ff,color:#fff
    style B fill:#ff8c00,color:#fff
    style C fill:#32cd32,color:#fff
    style D fill:#ff1493,color:#fff
    style F fill:#ffd700
```

### 4.2 Detailed Circuit Schematic (Mermaid Block-Level View)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph COMPARATOR_STAGE
        CMP[Op Amp 741 Comparator] -->|Vo Square Wave| Z1[Zener Z1]
        Z1 --> Z2[Zener Z2]
        Z2 --> SQ[Square Output Node]
        CMP_N[Negative Input] --> GND1[Ground]
        CMP_P[Positive Input] --> FB_NET[Feedback Net]
    end
    subgraph INTEGRATOR_STAGE
        INT[Op Amp 741 Integrator] --> TRI[Triangular Output]
        INT_N[Negative Input] --> RIN[Input Resistor Ri]
        RIN --> SQ
        INT_P[Positive Input] --> GND2[Ground]
        CF[Feedback Capacitor Cf] -.-> INT
    end
    TRI -->|R1| FB_NET
    FB_NET -->|R2| SQ
    style CMP fill:#ff8c00,color:#fff
    style INT fill:#32cd32,color:#fff
    style TRI fill:#ff1493,color:#fff
    style SQ fill:#ffd700
```

### 4.3 Timing Diagram (Waveform Relationships)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph TIMING_DIAGRAM
        T1[Time Axis t] --> W1[Square Wave Plus Vsat Minus Vsat]
        T1 --> W2[Triangular Wave Linear Ramp Up and Down]
        T1 --> W3[Threshold Crossing Points Plus Vth and Minus Vth]
    end
    W1 --> W2
    W2 --> W3
    style W1 fill:#ffd700
    style W2 fill:#ff1493,color:#fff
    style W3 fill:#00ced1,color:#fff
```

### 4.4 Sawtooth Generator Modification (Diode Steering)

```mermaid
graph TD
    SQ[Square Wave from Comparator] --> R_SLOW[Slow Resistor R_slow]
    SQ --> R_FAST[Fast Resistor R_fast]
    R_SLOW --> D1[Diode D1 Forward for Slow Ramp]
    R_FAST --> D2[Diode D2 Forward for Fast Flyback]
    D1 --> IN_NODE[Integrator Input Node]
    D2 --> IN_NODE
    IN_NODE --> INT[Op Amp Integrator]
    INT --> SAW[Sawtooth Output]
    style SQ fill:#ffd700
    style SAW fill:#ff1493,color:#fff
    style D1 fill:#32cd32
    style D2 fill:#ff6347
```

### 4.5 Hardware Assembly Map (For KTU Lab Viva)

| Block | Component | Typical Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Power Supply | IC 7815 / 7915 | $\pm 15$ V | Op-amp biasing |
| Comparator | μA741 + resistors | $R_1 = 10\,$kΩ, $R_2 = 5\,$kΩ | Schmitt trigger |
| Integrator | μA741 + RC | $R_i = 10\,$kΩ, $C_f = 0.1\,\mu$F | Triangle generation |
| Zener Clamp | BZX 5.1 V | $V_Z = 5.1$ V | Amplitude regulation |
| Output Probe | CRO / DSO | CH1 = Square, CH2 = Triangle | Waveform observation |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**  
*With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a triangular wave generator using op-amps. Derive the expression for its frequency of oscillation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks distribution):**
- **(1 mark)** Block diagram: Schmitt trigger → Integrator → Feedback loop.
- **(1 mark)** Working: Schmitt flips between $\pm V_{sat}$; integrator ramps up/down; the triangle output is fed back to trigger switching at $\pm V_{th}$.
- **(1 mark)** Final formula: $f = \dfrac{R_1}{4 R_2 R_i C_f}$.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**  
*How is a sawtooth wave generator obtained from a triangular wave generator? Mention the role of diodes.*

**Model Answer (3 marks distribution):**
- **(1 mark)** Sawtooth = asymmetric triangle; obtained by making integrator charge/discharge time constants unequal.
- **(1 mark)** Two resistors $R_{slow}$ and $R_{fast}$ in parallel, selected by diodes $D_1$ and $D_2$ based on input polarity.
- **(1 mark)** Result: $T_1 \neq T_2$, giving a fast flyback and a slow ramp (or vice versa).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Regulation:** Answer **any ONE** full question from each module. Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as (a) **7 marks** and (b) **7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** With the help of a circuit diagram, explain the operation of a triangular wave generator using op-amps μA741. Derive the expressions for:
   (i) Peak-to-peak output voltage
   (ii) Frequency of oscillation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a triangular wave generator to produce a **symmetric triangular wave of 1 kHz frequency** and **peak-to-peak amplitude of 8 V**. Assume $\pm 15$ V supply and $V_{sat} = \pm 13$ V. Choose standard component values. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Circuit Description (2 marks):**
The circuit consists of two op-amp stages:
1. **Op-amp 1 — Schmitt trigger (regenerative comparator):** Inverting input grounded; non-inverting input receives the triangular output through feedback resistors $R_1$ and $R_2$. Output switches between $+V_{sat}$ and $-V_{sat}$. Zener diodes $Z_1, Z_2$ clamp the amplitude to $\pm V_Z$.

2. **Op-amp 2 — Active integrator:** Inverting input receives the square wave through resistor $R_i$; feedback capacitor $C_f$ from output to inverting input. Non-inverting input is grounded. Output is the integrated (inverted) version of the square wave.

**Working (2 marks):**
- Assume the comparator output is at $+V_{sat}$. The integrator input is positive, so its output ramps **downward** linearly.
- The triangle keeps decreasing until it reaches $-V_{th} = -\beta V_{sat}$. At this point, the comparator flips to $-V_{sat}$.
- The integrator input becomes negative, and the output now ramps **upward**.
- The triangle increases until it hits $+V_{th} = +\beta V_{sat}$, and the cycle repeats. The result is a self-sustaining triangular wave.

**(i) Peak-to-Peak Output Voltage (1.5 marks):**

The Schmitt trigger thresholds are:
$$V_{th} = \pm \beta V_{sat} = \pm \dfrac{R_2}{R_1} V_{sat}$$

The triangular output swings between these thresholds, hence:
$$V_{pp} = 2 V_{th} = \dfrac{2 R_2 V_{sat}}{R_1}$$

**(ii) Frequency of Oscillation (1.5 marks):**

The slope of the integrator's output is:
$$\dfrac{dV_{out}}{dt} = -\dfrac{V_{sat}}{R_i C_f}$$

In one half-period, the output changes by $2 V_{th} = 2 \beta V_{sat}$:
$$2 \beta V_{sat} = \dfrac{V_{sat}}{R_i C_f} \cdot \dfrac{T}{2}$$

$$\therefore T = 4 \beta R_i C_f = \dfrac{4 R_2 R_i C_f}{R_1}$$

$$\boxed{f = \dfrac{R_1}{4 R_2 R_i C_f}}$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Design Specifications:**
- $f = 1$ kHz = $10^3$ Hz
- $V_{pp} = 8$ V, so $V_p = 4$ V
- $V_{sat} = 13$ V

**Step 1 — Choose $\beta$ (feedback fraction):**

From $V_p = \beta V_{sat}$:
$$\beta = \dfrac{V_p}{V_{sat}} = \dfrac{4}{13} \approx 0.307$$

Choose $R_1 = 10$ kΩ, $R_2 = 3.3$ kΩ (standard E12 value), giving:
$$\beta = \dfrac{3.3}{10} = 0.33 \Rightarrow V_p = 0.33 \times 13 = 4.29\,\text{V} \quad \text{(close to 4 V)}$$

**Step 2 — Choose $C_f$:**

Take $C_f = 0.1\,\mu\text{F}$ (standard value).

**Step 3 — Calculate $R_i$ from frequency formula:**

$$f = \dfrac{R_1}{4 R_2 R_i C_f}$$

$$10^3 = \dfrac{10 \times 10^3}{4 \times 3.3 \times 10^3 \times R_i \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$10^3 = \dfrac{10}{4 \times 3.3 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-3} \times R_i}$$

$$10^3 = \dfrac{10}{1.32 \times 10^{-3} \times R_i}$$

$$R_i = \dfrac{10}{1.32} = 7.57\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**Choose standard value:** $R_i = 7.5$ kΩ (E12 series).

**Verification (1 mark):**

$$f = \dfrac{10 \times 10^3}{4 \times 3.3 \times 10^3 \times 7.5 \times 10^3 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$f = \dfrac{10}{4 \times 3.3 \times 7.5 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{10}{9.9 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 1010\,\text{Hz} \approx 1\,\text{kHz} \quad \checkmark$$

**Final Component Values:**

| Component | Value |
|---|---|
| $R_1$ | $10\,$kΩ |
| $R_2$ | $3.3\,$kΩ |
| $R_i$ | $7.5\,$kΩ |
| $C_f$ | $0.1\,\mu$F |
| Op-amp | μA741 (×2) |
| Supply | $\pm 15$ V |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Draw the circuit diagram of a sawtooth wave generator using op-amps. Explain its working with the help of relevant waveforms. Derive the expression for its frequency of oscillation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sawtooth wave generator uses $R_1 = 20\,$kΩ, $R_2 = 10\,$kΩ, $R_{slow} = 100\,$kΩ, $R_{fast} = 10\,$kΩ, and $C_f = 0.01\,\mu$F. Calculate:
   (i) The frequency of oscillation
   (ii) The slow ramp time and fast flyback time
   (iii) The duty cycle. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Circuit (2 marks):**
The sawtooth generator is a triangular generator with a **modified integrator** that has two input resistors:
- $R_{slow}$ in series with diode $D_1$ (conducts during the negative half-cycle of the square wave → slow ramp)
- $R_{fast}$ in series with diode $D_2$ (conducts during the positive half-cycle → fast flyback)

The comparator stage is identical to the triangular wave generator.

**Working (2 marks):**
- When comparator output = $+V_{sat}$: $D_2$ is forward biased, integrator input is through $R_{fast}$ → output ramps **downward quickly** (fast flyback).
- When comparator output = $-V_{sat}$: $D_1$ is forward biased, integrator input is through $R_{slow}$ → output ramps **upward slowly** (slow ramp).
- The asymmetric charging produces a sawtooth waveform: slow rise, sudden fall.

**Waveform (1 mark):**
A linear ramp rising from $-V_{th}$ to $+V_{th}$ over time $T_1$, followed by a sharp drop back to $-V_{th}$ in time $T_2 \ll T_1$.

**Frequency Derivation (2 marks):**

The half-period for the slow ramp (rising):
$$T_1 = \dfrac{2 \beta V_{sat} \cdot R_{slow} C_f}{V_{sat}} = 2 \beta R_{slow} C_f$$

The half-period for the fast ramp (falling):
$$T_2 = 2 \beta R_{fast} C_f$$

Total time period:
$$T = T_1 + T_2 = 2 \beta C_f (R_{slow} + R_{fast}) = \dfrac{2 R_2 C_f (R_{slow} + R_{fast})}{R_1}$$

$$\boxed{f = \dfrac{R_1}{2 R_2 C_f (R_{slow} + R_{fast})}}$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:**
- $R_1 = 20$ kΩ, $R_2 = 10$ kΩ
- $R_{slow} = 100$ kΩ, $R_{fast} = 10$ kΩ
- $C_f = 0.01\,\mu$F = $10^{-8}$ F

**Step 1 — Calculate $\beta$ (1 mark):**

$$\beta = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1} = \dfrac{10}{20} = 0.5$$

**Step 2 — Calculate (i) Frequency (2 marks):**

$$f = \dfrac{R_1}{2 R_2 C_f (R_{slow} + R_{fast})}$$

$$f = \dfrac{20 \times 10^3}{2 \times 10 \times 10^3 \times 0.01 \times 10^{-6} \times (100 + 10) \times 10^3}$$

$$f = \dfrac{20 \times 10^3}{2 \times 10 \times 0.01 \times 10^{-3} \times 110 \times 10^3}$$

$$f = \dfrac{20}{2 \times 10 \times 0.01 \times 10^{-3} \times 110} = \dfrac{20}{0.022} \approx 909.09\,\text{Hz}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate (ii) Slow ramp and fast flyback times (2 marks):**

$$T_1 = 2 \beta R_{slow} C_f = 2 \times 0.5 \times 100 \times 10^3 \times 0.01 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$T_1 = 1 \times 100 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-8} = 10^{-3}\,\text{s} = 1\,\text{ms}$$

$$T_2 = 2 \beta R_{fast} C_f = 2 \times 0.5 \times 10 \times 10^3 \times 0.01 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$T_2 = 1 \times 10 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-8} = 10^{-4}\,\text{s} = 0.1\,\text{ms}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate (iii) Duty cycle (1 mark):**

$$D = \dfrac{T_1}{T_1 + T_2} = \dfrac{1\,\text{ms}}{1\,\text{ms} + 0.1\,\text{ms}} = \dfrac{1}{1.1} = 0.909 \approx 90.9\%$$

**Final Answers:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Frequency $f$ | **909.09 Hz** |
| Slow ramp $T_1$ | **1 ms** |
| Fast flyback $T_2$ | **0.1 ms** |
| Duty cycle | **90.9 %** |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 4 in the period formula:** Students often write $T = R_2 R_i C_f / R_1$. The correct formula is $T = 4 R_2 R_i C_f / R_1$. The "4" comes from the fact that there are two half-periods, each covering a voltage swing of $2 V_{th}$.
> 2. **Mixing up $\beta$ conventions:** Some textbooks use $\beta = R_2/(R_1 + R_2)$, others use $\beta = R_2/R_1$. Always use the convention given in your module/lab manual consistently. **For KTU 2024 PCAEL508 lab manual, $\beta = R_2/R_1$ is standard.**
> 3. **Ignoring zener voltage:** If the comparator uses 5.1 V zeners, $V_{sat}$ in the formulas is **replaced by $V_Z = 5.1$ V**, not 13 V. Many students blindly use 13 V.
> 4. **Swapping $R_{slow}$ and $R_{fast}$:** In sawtooth, the slow ramp corresponds to the larger resistance. Read the diode orientation carefully in the circuit.
> 5. **Not drawing waveforms in (a) part:** A 7-mark part-(a) question explicitly asks for **waveforms**. Skipping the timing diagram costs 1–2 marks easily.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Revision — Read This Twice**

- ✅ A **triangular wave generator** = **Schmitt trigger + Integrator** in a closed loop.
- ✅ The **Schmitt trigger** produces a **square wave** with amplitude $\pm V_{sat}$ (or $\pm V_Z$ with zener clamping).
- ✅ The **integrator** converts the square wave to a **triangular wave** with linear ramps.
- ✅ The **threshold voltage** of the Schmitt trigger sets the **peak amplitude** of the triangle: $V_p = \beta V_{sat}$.
- ✅ The **time period** is $T = 4 \beta R_i C_f = 4 R_2 R_i C_f / R_1$.
- ✅ The **frequency** is $f = R_1 / (4 R_2 R_i C_f)$.
- ✅ A **sawtooth wave** is an **asymmetric triangle** with unequal charge/discharge paths.
- ✅ The sawtooth uses **diode-steered resistors** $R_{slow}$ and $R_{fast}$ at the integrator input.
- ✅ Sawtooth **frequency formula**: $f = R_1 / [2 R_2 C_f (R_{slow} + R_{fast})]$.
- ✅ The **slow ramp time** $T_1$ uses $R_{slow}$ (large resistance); the **fast flyback time** $T_2$ uses $R_{fast}$ (small resistance).
- ✅ To **increase frequency** → reduce $R_i$ or $C_f$ or $R_2$.
- ✅ To **increase amplitude** → increase $R_2/R_1$ ratio (keeping $R_2 < R_1$).
- ✅ The op-amp used is **μA741** (KTU lab standard) with **±15 V** dual supply.
- ✅ **Zener diodes** (5.1 V) regulate the comparator's output amplitude to a precise value.
- ✅ **Practical applications:** CRO time-base, PWM/Class-D amplifiers, function generators, ADC test signals, VCOs.
- ✅ **Always draw** the block diagram, circuit diagram, and waveform in your exam answer — each fetches 1–2 marks.
- ✅ The **CRO/DSO** should be set in **DC coupling mode** for accurate amplitude measurements.
- ✅ The **2:1 rule**: in a triangular wave generator, the ratio of square-wave amplitude to triangle amplitude is **$1/\beta = R_1/R_2$**.
- ✅ For a **pure sawtooth** (no flat top), $R_{fast} \ll R_{slow}$, typically $R_{slow}/R_{fast} \geq 10$.
- ✅ **Slew rate** of μA741 is **0.5 V/μs** — at 13 V amplitude, max triangular frequency ≈ **20 kHz** before distortion.
- ✅ **KVL/KCL at the integrator input** is the key analytical step: sum of currents into the inverting node = 0.
- ✅ **Frequency stability** depends on the **stability of RC components** — use metal-film resistors and NPO/C0G capacitors for precision.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wien Bridge Oscillator: Without and With Amplitude Stabilization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAEL508 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS AND SIMULATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Wien Bridge Oscillator: Without and With Amplitude Stabilization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Wien Bridge Oscillator (WBO)**: A low-frequency sinusoidal oscillator that employs a four-arm AC bridge network — known as the *Wien Bridge* — as its frequency-determining positive feedback element. The bridge consists of a series RC arm and a parallel RC arm in two opposite branches, and two purely resistive arms in the remaining two branches. The active element is an operational amplifier configured as a non-inverting amplifier with closed-loop gain $A_v \geq 3$.

The Wien Bridge Oscillator is the de-facto industry standard for generating **audio-frequency (20 Hz – 20 kHz)** and **sub-audio** sinusoidal signals with very low total harmonic distortion (THD), making it indispensable in function generators, audio test equipment, and tone-generating instrumentation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition (Plain English)**
> Imagine pushing a child on a swing. If you push at exactly the right moment and with exactly the right force, the swing goes higher and higher — that's **oscillation buildup**. If you push too hard, the swing will go past the support and crash (analogous to **amplitude saturation/distortion**). The Wien Bridge Oscillator works the same way: the op-amp provides the "push" (energy), and the RC bridge decides *when* and *how often* to push (frequency). Without amplitude stabilization, the push becomes too strong and the swing crashes (signal clips). With amplitude stabilization (a smart brake or push-regulator), the swing maintains a steady, predictable height — producing a clean sine wave.

### 1.3 Two Variants of the Wien Bridge Oscillator

| Variant | Amplitude Behavior | Distortion | Typical Application |
|---------|-------------------|-----------|---------------------|
| **Without Amplitude Stabilization** | Unbounded growth, eventually saturates | High (clipping) | Laboratory demonstration only |
| **With Amplitude Stabilization** | Self-regulated, constant amplitude | Very low (< 0.1%) | Function generators, audio oscillators |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: The lab experiment explicitly requires the student to design, simulate, and practically verify *both* variants. The simulation is typically performed in **LTspice, Multisim, or Proteus**, while the hardware implementation uses an op-amp such as the **µA741** or **LM358**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Wien Bridge Network (Frequency-Selective Positive Feedback)

The positive feedback network is composed of a **series RC combination** ($Z_s$) and a **parallel RC combination** ($Z_p$) connected in a voltage-divider configuration:

$$Z_s = R_1 + \frac{1}{j\omega C_1}$$

$$Z_p = \frac{R_2 \cdot \frac{1}{j\omega C_2}}{R_2 + \frac{1}{j\omega C_2}} = \frac{R_2}{1 + j\omega R_2 C_2}$$

The feedback factor $\beta$ (transfer function of the network) is given by:

$$\beta(j\omega) = \frac{V_f}{V_o} = \frac{Z_p}{Z_s + Z_p}$$

After algebraic simplification (assuming $R_1 = R_2 = R$ and $C_1 = C_2 = C$ for the matched-design case):

$$\beta(j\omega) = \frac{1}{3 + j\left(\omega RC - \frac{1}{\omega RC}\right)}$$

### 2.2 Barkhausen Criterion — The Heart of Oscillation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Barkhausen Criterion**: For sustained sinusoidal oscillations, two simultaneous conditions must be satisfied:
> 1. **Loop Gain Magnitude Condition:** $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$
> 2. **Loop Phase Condition:** $\angle A\beta = 0° \text{ (or an integer multiple of } 360°)$

For the Wien Bridge, zero phase shift occurs **only** when the imaginary part of $\beta$ vanishes, i.e., when:

$$\omega RC - \frac{1}{\omega RC} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_o RC = 1$$

At this frequency, $\beta$ becomes purely real and equals $\frac{1}{3}$. Therefore, the amplifier must compensate with a gain of exactly **3** to satisfy $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$.

### 2.3 Why Gain Must Be Slightly Greater Than 3

In a practical (lossy) circuit, components have tolerances and the op-amp has finite open-loop gain, so $\vert A\beta \vert$ inevitably dips below 1 over time, causing oscillations to die out. To **guarantee startup**, the gain is set slightly above 3 (typically $A_v = 3 + \epsilon$ where $\epsilon$ is a small positive margin, e.g., 1–5%). This is the root cause of the amplitude runaway in the *unstabilized* variant.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Standard Value/Unit | Condition |
|---|-----------|---------|---------------------|-----------|
| 1 | Resonant (Oscillation) Frequency | $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | **Hz** | $R_1 = R_2 = R$, $C_1 = C_2 = C$ |
| 2 | General Oscillation Frequency | $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}$ | Hz | Unmatched case |
| 3 | Required Non-Inverting Gain | $A_v = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_g} = 3$ | dimensionless | Sustained oscillation |
| 4 | Feedback Factor (at $f_o$) | $\beta = \dfrac{1}{3}$ | dimensionless | Purely real |
| 5 | Loop Gain (at $f_o$) | $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ | dimensionless | Barkhausen |
| 6 | Resistor Ratio (Gain = 3) | $\dfrac{R_f}{R_g} = 2$ | dimensionless | E.g., $R_f = 20\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_g = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ |
| 7 | Amplitude Stabilization Gain Window | $2.9 \leq A_v \leq 3.1$ | dimensionless | Zener/clamping stabilization |
| 8 | THD with Stabilization | $\text{THD} \approx 0.01\% - 0.1\%$ | % | Practical with diodes/Zeners |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> The Wien Bridge Oscillator is the workhorse of:
> - **Audio function generators** (Hewlett-Packard 200CD, Tektronix AFG series)
> - **Tone detection circuits** in telecommunications
> - **Vibration analysis** equipment
> - **Bio-medical instruments** (EEG/EMG oscillators for calibration)
> - **Phase-locked loop (PLL)** test beds

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Oscillation Frequency

**Starting Point:** The transfer function of the Wien Network

$$\beta(j\omega) = \frac{Z_p}{Z_s + Z_p}$$

**Step 1:** Substitute the expressions for $Z_s$ and $Z_p$:

$$\beta(j\omega) = \frac{\dfrac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}}{R + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C} + \dfrac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}}$$

**Step 2:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $j\omega C (1 + j\omega RC)$ to clear the fractions. The numerator becomes:

$$\text{Numerator} = R \cdot j\omega C$$

The denominator, after expansion:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Denominator} &= \left(R + \frac{1}{j\omega C}\right)(1 + j\omega RC) + R \\[4pt]
&= R(1 + j\omega RC) + \frac{1}{j\omega C}(1 + j\omega RC) + R \\[4pt]
&= R + j\omega R^2 C + \frac{1}{j\omega C} + R + R \\[4pt]
&= 3R + j\omega R^2 C + \frac{1}{j\omega C}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Recognize that $j\omega R^2 C + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C} = j\left(\omega R^2 C - \dfrac{1}{\omega C}\right)$. The denominator becomes:

$$3R + j\left(\omega R^2 C - \frac{1}{\omega C}\right)$$

**Step 4:** The transfer function is therefore:

$$\beta(j\omega) = \frac{j\omega RC}{3 + j\left(\omega RC - \dfrac{1}{\omega RC}\right)}$$

**Step 5:** For the phase of $\beta$ to be **zero** (so the loop phase is $0°$), the ratio of imaginary to real parts must match. Equivalently, the **imaginary part of the denominator must vanish**:

$$\omega_o RC - \frac{1}{\omega_o RC} = 0$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $\omega_o$:

$$\omega_o^2 R^2 C^2 = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_o = \frac{1}{RC}$$

**Step 7:** Convert from rad/s to Hz:

$$f_o = \frac{\omega_o}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$

**Step 8:** Evaluate $\beta$ at $f_o$. Substituting $\omega_o RC = 1$ back:

$$\beta(f_o) = \frac{j(1)}{3 + j(1 - 1)} = \frac{j}{3 + 0} = \frac{j}{3}$$

**Step 9:** The magnitude of $\beta$ at $f_o$:

$$\vert \beta(f_o) \vert = \frac{1}{3}$$

**Step 10:** Apply Barkhausen's magnitude condition $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$:

$$A_v = \frac{1}{\vert \beta(f_o) \vert} = \frac{1}{1/3} = 3$$

This completes the formal proof that the non-inverting op-amp must provide a closed-loop gain of exactly **3** for sustained oscillations.

### 3.2 Gain Derivation for the Non-Inverting Amplifier

The non-inverting op-amp gain is:

$$A_v = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_g}$$

For $A_v = 3$:

$$1 + \frac{R_f}{R_g} = 3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{R_f}{R_g} = 2$$

**Example numerical design:** Choose $R_g = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, then $R_f = 20\,\text{k}\Omega$.

### 3.3 Quantitative Comparison: Without vs. With Stabilization

| Parameter | Without Stabilization | With Stabilization (Zener) | With Stabilization (Diode) | With Stabilization (NTC Thermistor) |
|-----------|----------------------|----------------------------|----------------------------|-------------------------------------|
| **Initial Gain** | $A_v = 3.0 - 3.5$ | $A_v \approx 3$ | $A_v \approx 3$ | $A_v \approx 3$ |
| **Stabilizing Element** | None | Back-to-back Zeners | Anti-parallel diodes + resistor | NTC in feedback |
| **Output Amplitude** | Uncontrolled (saturates) | $V_{out} \approx V_Z$ (clamped) | $\approx 0.6 - 0.7$ V above resistor drop | Self-regulating via heating |
| **THD** | High (5–10%) | Moderate (1–3%) | Low (0.1–0.5%) | Very low (0.01–0.05%) |
| **Frequency Stability** | Poor (drifts with amplitude) | Good | Good | Excellent |
| **Warm-up Time** | None | None | None | 2–5 seconds (filament heating) |

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Design Trade-offs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Designer's Dilemma (KTU Viva Favorite)**:
> - **Too high gain** ($A_v \gg 3$): Fast startup but severe distortion and saturation.
> - **Exactly $A_v = 3$**: Mathematically ideal but practically unstable; oscillations slowly die.
> - **Too low gain** ($A_v < 3$): No oscillation at all.
> - **Smart stabilization**: Maintains $A_v$ slightly above 3 during startup, then settles to exactly 3 in steady-state.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Wien Bridge Oscillator Without Amplitude Stabilization — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VCC["VCC plus 15V"]
    VEE["VEE minus 15V"]
    OPA["OpAmp UA741"]
    ZS["Series RC Arm R1 plus C1"]
    ZP["Parallel RC Arm R2 parallel C2"]
    RFG["Feedback Resistors Rf and Rg"]
    OUT["Sinusoidal Output Vo"]
    GND["Ground Node"]

    VCC --- OPA
    VEE --- OPA
    OPA -->|"Vo"| ZS
    ZS -->|"Node A"| ZP
    ZP --> GND
    OPA -->|"Vo"| RFG
    RFG -->|"Vf to plus input"| OPA
    ZS -->|"Vf to plus input"| OPA
    OPA -->|"Vo"| OUT
```

### 4.2 Wien Bridge Oscillator With Amplitude Stabilization (Zener-based) — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VCC["VCC plus 15V"]
    VEE["VEE minus 15V"]
    OPA["OpAmp UA741"]
    ZS["Series RC Arm R1 plus C1"]
    ZP["Parallel RC Arm R2 parallel C2"]
    RFG["Gain Resistors Rf Rg"]
    ZENER["Back to Back Zener Pair Dz1 Dz2 at 5.1V"]
    OUT["Regulated Sinusoidal Output"]
    GND["Ground Node"]

    VCC --- OPA
    VEE --- OPA
    OPA -->|"Vo"| ZS
    ZS --> ZP
    ZP --> GND
    ZS -->|"Vf to plus input"| OPA
    OPA -->|"Vo"| RFG
    RFG --> ZENER
    ZENER -->|"Dynamic Rf"| OPA
    OPA -->|"Vo"| OUT
```

### 4.3 Amplitude Stabilization Mechanism — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Oscillator Startup with Av slightly greater than 3"]
    BUILD["Amplitude Builds Up"]
    CHECK{"Output exceeds Vz threshold"}
    CLAMP["Zener Conducts and Reduces Effective Rf"]
    REGULATE["Effective Gain Av drops to exactly 3"]
    STEADY["Steady State Constant Amplitude Clean Sinewave"]
    FALLBACK["If Amplitude falls below Vz"]
    RECOVER["Zener becomes High Impedance and Av rises above 3"]

    START --> BUILD
    BUILD --> CHECK
    CHECK -->|"YES"| CLAMP
    CHECK -->|"NO"| STEADY
    CLAMP --> REGULATE
    REGULATE --> STEADY
    STEADY --> FALLBACK
    FALLBACK --> RECOVER
    RECOVER --> STEADY
```

### 4.4 Pin-Level Op-Amp Connection Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SUPPLY["Power Supply Rails"]
        VPLUS["Pin 7 V plus 15V"]
        VMINUS["Pin 4 V minus 15V"]
    end

    subgraph IC["UA741 OpAmp"]
        P3["Pin 3 Non Inverting Plus Input"]
        P2["Pin 2 Inverting Minus Input"]
        P6["Pin 6 Output"]
        P1P5["Pins 1 5 8 Offset Null No Connect"]
    end

    subgraph FB["Feedback Network"]
        RG["Rg 10kohm from Pin 2 to Ground"]
        RF["Rf 20kohm from Pin 6 to Pin 2"]
    end

    subgraph WN["Wien Network"]
        R1["R1 10kohm"]
        C1["C1 10nF in Series"]
        R2["R2 10kohm"]
        C2["C2 10nF in Parallel with R2"]
    end

    P6 -->|"Vo"| R1
    R1 --> C1
    C1 --> P3
    P3 --> R2
    P3 --> C2
    R2 --> GNDA["Ground"]
    C2 --> GNDA
    P2 --> RG
    P2 --> RF
```

### 4.5 Typical Simulation Output Waveform (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TIME["Time Domain Output"]
        DIR1["Without Stabilization Diverging and Clipped"]
    end
    subgraph TIME2["Time Domain Output"]
        DIR2["With Stabilization Stable Constant Amplitude"]
    end
    DIR1 --> DIR2
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]**
*State the Barkhausen criterion for sustained oscillations. Why is the gain of a Wien Bridge Oscillator set to exactly 3?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Barkhausen criterion states that for sustained sinusoidal oscillations, the loop gain must satisfy two conditions simultaneously:
1. The magnitude of the loop gain $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$
2. The total phase shift around the loop is $0°$ (or $360°$ integer multiples).

The feedback factor of the Wien bridge at the resonant frequency is $\beta = \frac{1}{3}$ (purely real). Therefore, to satisfy $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$, the amplifier gain must be $A = \frac{1}{\beta} = 3$. This is realized using a non-inverting op-amp configuration with $R_f = 2R_g$.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question Bank]**
*List any two methods used for amplitude stabilization in a Wien Bridge Oscillator. Why is amplitude stabilization necessary?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Two methods for amplitude stabilization are:
1. **Back-to-back Zener diodes** connected across the feedback resistor $R_f$. When the output amplitude exceeds the Zener voltage, the diodes conduct and dynamically reduce the effective feedback resistance, thereby lowering the gain to exactly 3.
2. **NTC Thermistor** in the feedback path. As the amplitude increases, the thermistor self-heats, its resistance drops, and the gain is automatically clamped to 3.

Amplitude stabilization is necessary because, with $A_v > 3$ (a practical requirement for guaranteed startup), the amplitude grows without bound and the op-amp saturates, producing a clipped, distorted (non-sinusoidal) output. Stabilization maintains a constant, low-distortion sine wave.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Writing the oscillation frequency formula as $f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{LC}}$ — this is the *tank-circuit* formula, applicable to *LC oscillators*, NOT the Wien Bridge. Using the wrong formula guarantees zero marks for the derivation.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Failing to explicitly state **both** Barkhausen conditions (magnitude AND phase). Most students write only $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ and forget the phase condition, losing 2–3 marks.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Drawing the Wien Bridge with the RC arms in the *wrong configuration* (parallel–parallel or series–series). The correct configuration is **series RC** in one arm and **parallel RC** in the opposite arm.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Not specifying the component matching condition ($R_1 = R_2 = R$ and $C_1 = C_2 = C$) before writing the simplified $f_o$ formula. Examiners deduct marks for this.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]*

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, derive the expression for the frequency of oscillation of a Wien Bridge Oscillator using an op-amp. State the Barkhausen criterion and show that the gain of the amplifier must be 3 for sustained oscillations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Design a Wien Bridge Oscillator to generate a 1 kHz sinusoidal signal using an op-amp. Choose appropriate values of $R$ and $C$, and explain the function of each component in the circuit. What happens if $A_v < 3$ or $A_v \gg 3$? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Circuit Description (2 marks):** Draw the standard Wien Bridge oscillator with op-amp. Mark the series arm ($R_1$, $C_1$ in series), the parallel arm ($R_2 \parallel C_2$), the gain-setting resistors ($R_f$ and $R_g$), and identify the non-inverting (+) input as the node where the Wien feedback is injected.

**Step 2 — Transfer Function (2 marks):** Write the expressions for $Z_s$ and $Z_p$, then derive $\beta(j\omega) = \dfrac{Z_p}{Z_s + Z_p}$. After algebra, obtain:

$$\beta(j\omega) = \frac{1}{3 + j\left(\omega RC - \frac{1}{\omega RC}\right)}$$

**Step 3 — Barkhausen Phase Condition (1.5 marks):** For zero phase, the imaginary part of the denominator must vanish, giving $\omega_o RC = 1$, and therefore:

$$f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$

**Step 4 — Barkhausen Magnitude Condition (1.5 marks):** At $f_o$, $\beta = \frac{1}{3}$. Applying $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ gives $A = 3$. This is realized with $A_v = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_g} = 3 \Rightarrow R_f = 2R_g$.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Component Selection (2 marks):** Choose $C = 10\,\text{nF}$ (standard value). Then $R = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f_o C} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 1000 \times 10 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 15.9\,\text{k}\Omega$. Use the nearest standard value: $R = 15\,\text{k}\Omega$ or $16\,\text{k}\Omega$.

**Step 2 — Gain Resistors (1.5 marks):** Choose $R_g = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $R_f = 20\,\text{k}\Omega$ to give $A_v = 3$.

**Step 3 — Function of Components (2 marks):**
- $R_1, C_1$ (series): Set the upper frequency roll-off of the feedback.
- $R_2, C_2$ (parallel): Set the lower frequency roll-off.
- $R_f, R_g$: Set the closed-loop gain of the non-inverting amplifier.
- Op-amp: Provides the amplification needed to overcome the network's 1/3 attenuation.

**Step 4 — Gain Deviation Analysis (1.5 marks):**
- If $A_v < 3$: The loop gain $\vert A\beta \vert < 1$, oscillations cannot be sustained; the output decays to zero.
- If $A_v \gg 3$: Loop gain exceeds unity by a large margin; amplitude grows exponentially until the op-amp saturates, producing a **clipped square-wave-like** output with severe harmonic distortion.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]*

**(a)** Explain with a neat circuit diagram the working of a Wien Bridge Oscillator with amplitude stabilization using back-to-back Zener diodes. How does the Zener pair maintain constant output amplitude? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare the performance of a Wien Bridge Oscillator (i) without amplitude stabilization, (ii) with Zener-diode stabilization, and (iii) with NTC thermistor stabilization. Mention at least four comparison parameters. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Circuit (2 marks):** Draw the Wien Bridge oscillator. Place two Zener diodes $D_{Z1}$ and $D_{Z2}$ in **anti-series** (cathode-to-cathode) across the feedback resistor $R_f$. When the output amplitude exceeds $V_Z + V_F \approx V_Z$ (the Zener voltage), the diodes break down and conduct.

**Step 2 — Working Principle (3 marks):** During the initial startup, output amplitude is small ($< V_Z$), Zeners are OFF, and the effective feedback resistance is $R_f$ alone. The gain is $A_v = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_g} > 3$, so the oscillation amplitude grows. When the amplitude reaches $V_Z$, the Zeners start conducting. The dynamic (small-signal) resistance of the conducting Zener pair, $r_z$, is much smaller than $R_f$. The effective feedback resistance becomes $R_f \parallel r_z \approx r_z \ll R_f$, and the effective gain drops toward 3, clamping the amplitude at approximately $V_Z$ (peak).

**Step 3 — Steady-State Regulation (2 marks):** In steady state, the amplitude is held at the Zener knee voltage with small-signal AC gain of exactly 3, producing a clean sine wave with very low distortion. The Zener pair acts as an **automatic gain control (AGC)** element.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Without Stabilization | Zener Stabilization | NTC Thermistor Stabilization |
|-----------|----------------------|---------------------|------------------------------|
| **Stabilizing Element** | None | Back-to-back Zener diodes | NTC thermistor (in $R_g$ or $R_f$) |
| **Output Amplitude Control** | Uncontrolled, grows until saturation | Clamped at $\pm V_Z$ (peak) | Self-regulating via thermal feedback |
| **THD (Total Harmonic Distortion)** | 5 – 10 % (high) | 1 – 3 % (moderate) | 0.01 – 0.1 % (very low) |
| **Frequency Stability** | Poor (amplitude-dependent) | Good | Excellent |
| **Startup Time** | Instantaneous | Instantaneous | 2 – 5 seconds (warm-up of thermistor) |
| **Cost & Complexity** | Lowest | Moderate | Higher |
| **Distortion Mechanism** | Hard clipping at op-amp rails | Soft limiting at Zener knee | Smooth gain compression (thermal) |

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist**
> - **Wien Bridge Oscillator** uses a series RC and a parallel RC arm in the positive feedback path; it is the most common low-frequency sine-wave generator.
> - **Oscillation frequency:** $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ (for $R_1 = R_2 = R$ and $C_1 = C_2 = C$).
> - **Required gain:** $A_v = 3$ (Barkhausen criterion). Realized as non-inverting op-amp with $R_f = 2R_g$.
> - **Feedback factor** at $f_o$: $\beta = \frac{1}{3}$ (purely real, zero phase).
> - **Without stabilization:** Amplitude runs away, output clips, THD is high (5–10%).
> - **With stabilization:** Amplitude is self-regulated, output is a clean sine wave, THD is very low (≤ 0.1%).
> - **Common stabilization methods:** Back-to-back Zener diodes, anti-parallel signal diodes + resistor, NTC thermistor, FET as VCR, incandescent lamp.
> - **Design Tip:** Always select matched $R$ and $C$ pairs; mismatch shifts $f_o$ and degrades output purity.
> - **Simulation Tools (KTU Lab):** LTspice (free, preferred), NI Multisim, Proteus, PSIM.
> - **Practical Op-Amps for Lab:** µA741, LM358, TL071, OP07 (use OP07 or TL071 for low-distortion designs).
> - **KTU Viva Question Hot Pick:** *"Why doesn't the Wien Bridge Oscillator need a transformer for phase inversion like the original Wien's 1931 design?"* — Answer: The op-amp is a non-inverting amplifier, so it provides zero phase shift on its own, fulfilling the Barkhausen phase condition without any inductive coupling.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RC Phase Shift Oscillator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAEL508 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS AND SIMULATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** RC Phase Shift Oscillator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **RC Phase Shift Oscillator** is a sinusoidal electronic oscillator circuit that generates periodic sine waves in the **audio frequency range (typically 20 Hz to 20 kHz)**. It employs an **amplifying active device** (BJT, FET, or Op-Amp) in conjunction with a **three-stage RC ladder feedback network** to satisfy the **Barkhausen Criterion** for sustained oscillations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCAEL508 - Module 2):**
> The RC Phase Shift Oscillator is studied as a classic **audio-frequency generator** topology. Students are expected to design, simulate (using SPICE/Multisim), and experimentally verify the circuit on a breadboard/PCB. The expected deliverables are: theoretical frequency, theoretical minimum gain, and comparison with simulated/measured values.

By KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the circuit is a **linear oscillator** belonging to the **sinusoidal (harmonic) oscillator** family. It is the RC analog of the LC oscillator — instead of inductors (which are bulky, expensive, and lossy at audio frequencies), it uses only resistors and capacitors to create the necessary phase shift.

### 1.2 Barkhausen Criterion (Foundation Block)

For any sinusoidal oscillator, two simultaneous conditions must be met:

1. **Loop Gain Condition:** $\vert A \beta \vert = 1$ (Magnitude)
2. **Phase Condition:** $\angle A\beta = 0° \text{ or } 360°$ (i.e., total loop phase shift = an integer multiple of $360°$)

> [!NOTE]
> The RC Phase Shift Oscillator uses an **inverting amplifier (180° phase shift)** combined with a **3-stage RC network (180° phase shift at f₀)** to produce the required 360° total loop phase shift.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Three-Person Tug of War"

Imagine a circular tug-of-war with three people standing at 120° apart on a circle:

- Each person pulls the rope but is connected to the next person through a **spring-and-shock-absorber (R-C combination)**.
- The pull from person 1 is **delayed** by 60° as it passes through each spring-damper.
- After passing through all three sections, the original "push" is delayed by exactly **180°**.
- The amplifier (the "central referee") then **inverts** this delayed signal by another 180° (turning push into pull).
- Total delay = 180° + 180° = 360° → the rope goes back-and-forth rhythmically → **oscillation achieved** at a specific rhythm (frequency).

**Plain English Summary:**
The RC Phase Shift Oscillator is essentially an inverting amplifier whose output is fed back to its input through **three identical RC low-pass filter sections**. Each section contributes **60°** of phase lag, and at the oscillation frequency these three add up to exactly **180°**. The amplifier adds the remaining **180°**, making the loop phase shift exactly **360°** — a perfect recipe for self-sustaining sine-wave generation.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Unit |
| :--- | :--- |
| Audio Frequency Range | **20 Hz – 20 kHz** |
| Typical Operating Frequency | **1 kHz – 10 kHz** |
| Total Phase Shift of RC Network | **180°** (3 × 60°) |
| Phase Shift of Amplifier | **180°** (inverting) |
| Number of RC Sections | **3** (minimum to get 180° using identical R-C) |
| Typical Loop Gain (β) | **1/29 ≈ 0.0345** |
| Minimum Amplifier Gain (A) | **29** (for unity loop gain) |

> [!NOTE]
> Why exactly 3 sections? With **1 RC section**, max phase shift is 90° (asymptotic). With **2 RC sections**, max is 180° only at f = ∞ (impractical). With **3 RC sections**, the network reaches exactly 180° at a **finite, usable frequency** — making 3 the minimum practical count.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Circuit Topology Breakdown

The RC Phase Shift Oscillator consists of two main blocks:

### Block A: The Inverting Amplifier (Active Part)
- **Op-Amp version:** Inverting configuration with closed-loop gain $A = -\dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$
- **BJT version:** Common-Emitter (CE) configuration with gain controlled by $R_C$ and $R_E$
- **FET version:** Common-Source (CS) configuration

### Block B: The Three-Stage RC Ladder Network (Feedback Part)
Three cascaded RC sections. Each section is an **RC high-pass / low-pass ladder**. The standard arrangement is:

```
Vout → [R → C → Gnd] → [R → C → Gnd] → [R → C → Gnd] → Vin
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The output of the amplifier drives node 1 of the ladder; the input of the amplifier is taken from node 4 (the end of the ladder). The ladder provides voltage division and progressive phase lag.

## 2.2 The Three-Stage RC Network — Detailed Analysis

Consider the standard RC ladder with all resistors = **R** and all capacitors = **C**. The ladder is driven by the amplifier output voltage $V_o$ and presents a feedback voltage $V_f$ to the amplifier input.

The ladder is described by the **feedback factor**:

$$\beta = \frac{V_f}{V_o} = \frac{1}{1 - 5\alpha^2 - j(6\alpha - \alpha^3)}$$

where $\alpha = \dfrac{1}{\omega RC}$ is the normalized frequency parameter.

> [!NOTE]
> The factor of 5 in the real part and 6 in the imaginary part come from the algebraic expansion of the three-section network using nodal analysis. Derivation shown in Section 3.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Value / Condition | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Frequency of Oscillation** | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$ | Where R, C are ladder values | **Hz** |
| 2 | **Angular Frequency** | $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{RC\sqrt{6}}$ | $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0$ | **rad/s** |
| 3 | **Phase Shift per RC Section** | $\phi_1 = \arctan(\omega RC)$ | At $f_0$: $\phi_1 = 60°$ | **Degrees** |
| 4 | **Feedback Factor (β)** | $\beta = \dfrac{1}{29}$ (at $f_0$) | Magnitude only | **Dimensionless** |
| 5 | **Minimum Op-Amp Gain** | $\vert A \vert = 29$ | So $R_f = 29 R_1$ | **Dimensionless** |
| 6 | **Minimum BJT Current Gain** | $h_{fe(min)} = 4k + 23 + \dfrac{29}{k}$ | $k = R_C / R$ | **Dimensionless** |
| 7 | **Barkhausen Magnitude** | $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ | Sustained oscillation | **Dimensionless** |
| 8 | **Barkhausen Phase** | $\angle A\beta = 0°$ or $360°$ | Loop phase condition | **Degrees** |
| 9 | **Amplifier Phase** | $180°$ | Inverting (CE/Op-Amp) | **Degrees** |
| 10 | **Ladder Phase** | $180°$ | Sum of three 60° lags | **Degrees** |
| 11 | **Loop Gain of Network** | $A\beta = \dfrac{-A}{29}$ | At $f = f_0$ | **Dimensionless** |
| 12 | **Period of Oscillation** | $T_0 = 2\pi RC\sqrt{6}$ | $T_0 = 1/f_0$ | **Seconds** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Alert:** The constant **√6 ≈ 2.449** appears in the frequency formula. Students often write √5 or √7. Always remember: the "6" comes from **6α** in the imaginary part of the β expression, and **5α²** in the real part — the difference gives the constant 6 in the denominator.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The RC Phase Shift Oscillator is used in:

- 🎵 **Audio signal generators** (function generators, lab test equipment)
- 📻 **Tone generation circuits** in musical instruments
- 🔔 **Alarm/buzzer circuits** in low-cost electronic devices
- 🎚️ **Phase-shift networks** in instrumentation amplifiers
- 🧪 **Educational laboratories** — the standard "first oscillator" taught
- 📡 **Carrier generation** in low-frequency communication systems
- 🎛️ **Audio synthesizers** for sine wave generation

> [!NOTE]
> In modern commercial equipment, the RC Phase Shift Oscillator has largely been replaced by **Wien Bridge Oscillators** (better amplitude stability) and **digital DDS** chips. However, it remains the **most pedagogically important** oscillator and is **always tested** in KTU 2024 Scheme labs and exams.

## 2.5 Design Procedure (Stepwise)

**Goal:** Design an RC Phase Shift Oscillator to oscillate at $f_0 = 1 \text{ kHz}$.

**Step 1:** Choose a convenient value of $C$ (say $C = 0.01 \mu F$).

**Step 2:** Calculate $R$ from:
$$R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_0 C \sqrt{6}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 1000 \times 0.01 \times 10^{-6} \times 2.449}$$

$$R \approx 6.5 \text{ k}\Omega \quad (\text{use } 6.8 \text{ k}\Omega \text{ standard value})$$

**Step 3:** For op-amp version, set $R_f = 29 R_1$. Choose $R_1 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, then $R_f = 290 \text{ k}\Omega \approx 300 \text{ k}\Omega$.

**Step 4:** Verify simulation: Run SPICE/NI Multisim transient analysis — observe sinusoidal output at ~1 kHz.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation #1: Feedback Factor (β) of the Three-Stage RC Ladder

### Setup

Let the three RC sections have identical values $R$ and $C$. The amplifier output voltage drives the ladder. Let:
- $V_o$ = amplifier output voltage
- $V_1, V_2, V_3$ = node voltages at junctions
- $V_f = V_3$ = feedback voltage applied to amplifier input

We model the ladder with mesh/node analysis.

### Step 1: Write the Nodal Equation at the First Node

The current from $V_o$ to $V_1$ through the first $R$ equals the current through the first $C$ to ground plus the current leaving through the next $R$.

$$\frac{V_o - V_1}{R} = \frac{V_1}{1/(j\omega C)} + \frac{V_1 - V_2}{R}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{V_o - V_1}{R} = j\omega C V_1 + \frac{V_1 - V_2}{R}$$

Multiply by $R$:

$$V_o - V_1 = j\omega RC V_1 + V_1 - V_2$$

$$V_o = V_1(2 + j\omega RC) - V_2 \quad \cdots (1)$$

### Step 2: Write the Nodal Equation at the Second Node

$$\frac{V_1 - V_2}{R} = j\omega C V_2 + \frac{V_2 - V_3}{R}$$

$$V_1 - V_2 = j\omega RC V_2 + V_2 - V_3$$

$$V_1 = V_2(2 + j\omega RC) - V_3 \quad \cdots (2)$$

### Step 3: Write the Nodal Equation at the Third Node (No Load)

$$\frac{V_2 - V_3}{R} = j\omega C V_3$$

$$V_2 - V_3 = j\omega RC V_3$$

$$V_2 = V_3(1 + j\omega RC) \quad \cdots (3)$$

### Step 4: Substitute (3) into (2)

$$V_1 = V_3(1 + j\omega RC)(2 + j\omega RC) - V_3$$

Let $\alpha = \omega RC$ for brevity.

$$V_1 = V_3 \left[ (1 + j\alpha)(2 + j\alpha) - 1 \right]$$

Expanding:

$$(1 + j\alpha)(2 + j\alpha) = 2 + j\alpha + 2j\alpha + j^2 \alpha^2 = 2 + 3j\alpha - \alpha^2$$

$$V_1 = V_3 \left[ 2 + 3j\alpha - \alpha^2 - 1 \right] = V_3 \left[ 1 + 3j\alpha - \alpha^2 \right] \quad \cdots (4)$$

### Step 5: Substitute (3) and (4) into (1)

$$V_o = V_3(1 + 3j\alpha - \alpha^2)(2 + j\alpha) - V_3(1 + j\alpha)$$

$$V_o = V_3 \left[ (1 + 3j\alpha - \alpha^2)(2 + j\alpha) - (1 + j\alpha) \right]$$

### Step 6: Expand the First Product

$$(1 + 3j\alpha - \alpha^2)(2 + j\alpha)$$

$$= 1 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot j\alpha + 3j\alpha \cdot 2 + 3j\alpha \cdot j\alpha - \alpha^2 \cdot 2 - \alpha^2 \cdot j\alpha$$

$$= 2 + j\alpha + 6j\alpha + 3j^2\alpha^2 - 2\alpha^2 - j\alpha^3$$

$$= 2 + 7j\alpha - 3\alpha^2 - 2\alpha^2 - j\alpha^3$$

$$= 2 + 7j\alpha - 5\alpha^2 - j\alpha^3$$

### Step 7: Subtract $(1 + j\alpha)$

$$V_o = V_3 \left[ 2 + 7j\alpha - 5\alpha^2 - j\alpha^3 - 1 - j\alpha \right]$$

$$V_o = V_3 \left[ 1 + 6j\alpha - 5\alpha^2 - j\alpha^3 \right]$$

### Step 8: Solve for β

$$\beta = \frac{V_f}{V_o} = \frac{V_3}{V_o} = \frac{1}{1 - 5\alpha^2 + j(6\alpha - \alpha^3)}$$

$$\boxed{\beta = \frac{1}{(1 - 5\alpha^2) + j(6\alpha - \alpha^3)}}$$

This matches the standard textbook result. ✅

## 3.2 Derivation #2: Frequency of Oscillation

### Step 1: Apply the Phase Condition

For oscillation, the imaginary part of the denominator must be zero (i.e., β must be purely real so the RC network contributes 0° net, leaving the amplifier's 180° + 180° = 360° total).

$$6\alpha - \alpha^3 = 0$$

$$\alpha(6 - \alpha^2) = 0$$

### Step 2: Solve

Since $\alpha = 0$ is trivial (DC), we have:

$$\alpha^2 = 6 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha = \sqrt{6}$$

### Step 3: Substitute Back

$$\omega_0 RC = \sqrt{6} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_0 = \frac{1}{RC\sqrt{6}}$$

### Step 4: Convert to Frequency

$$f_0 = \frac{\omega_0}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{2\pi RC \sqrt{6}}$$

$$\boxed{f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}} \approx \frac{0.0650}{RC} \text{ Hz}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The constant 0.0650 comes from $\dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{6}} = \dfrac{1}{2 \times 3.1416 \times 2.449} \approx 0.0650$.

## 3.3 Derivation #3: Minimum Gain Condition

### Step 1: At $f_0$, the Imaginary Part Vanishes

At $\alpha^2 = 6$:

$$1 - 5\alpha^2 = 1 - 5(6) = 1 - 30 = -29$$

### Step 2: Therefore β at $f_0$ is Purely Real

$$\beta = \frac{1}{-29} = -\frac{1}{29}$$

Magnitude:

$$\vert \beta \vert = \frac{1}{29}$$

### Step 3: Apply the Magnitude Condition $\vert A\beta \vert \geq 1$

$$\vert A \vert \cdot \frac{1}{29} \geq 1$$

$$\boxed{\vert A_{min} \vert = 29}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The negative sign in β = -1/29 indicates that the ladder adds 180° phase shift (consistent with our design). When the inverting amplifier adds another 180°, the total is 360°.

## 3.4 Derivation #4: Minimum BJT Current Gain (h_fe)

In the BJT version, the gain condition is modified. Using the standard result (Roy Choudhury / Gayakwad):

$$h_{fe(min)} = 4k + 23 + \frac{29}{k}, \quad \text{where } k = \frac{R_C}{R}$$

### Step 1: Differentiate to Find Optimum k

$$\frac{d}{dk}\left[4k + 23 + \frac{29}{k}\right] = 4 - \frac{29}{k^2} = 0$$

$$k^2 = \frac{29}{4} \quad \Rightarrow \quad k = \frac{\sqrt{29}}{2} \approx 2.693$$

### Step 2: Calculate Minimum h_fe

$$h_{fe(min), opt} = 4 \cdot 2.693 + 23 + \frac{29}{2.693}$$

$$= 10.77 + 23 + 10.77 = 44.54$$

So the optimum BJT current gain is:

$$\boxed{h_{fe(min)} \approx 44.5 \quad \text{for optimum design}}$$

The general expression is **h_fe(min) ≥ 44.5** for guaranteed oscillation with BJT version.

## 3.5 Numerical Design Example (Worked Out)

**Problem:** Design an op-amp RC phase shift oscillator for $f_0 = 2 \text{ kHz}$ using $\mu A741$ op-amp. Choose $C = 0.01 \mu F$ and $R_1 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$.

**Solution:**

### Step 1: Find R from Frequency Formula

$$R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_0 C \sqrt{6}}$$

$$R = \frac{1}{2 \times 3.1416 \times 2000 \times 0.01 \times 10^{-6} \times 2.449}$$

$$R = \frac{1}{3.078 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 3249 \text{ }\Omega$$

Use standard value: $R = 3.3 \text{ k}\Omega$

### Step 2: Verify f_0

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2 \times 3.1416 \times 3300 \times 0.01 \times 10^{-6} \times 2.449} \approx 1969 \text{ Hz} \approx 2 \text{ kHz} \quad ✓$$

### Step 3: Calculate R_f for Gain = 29

$$R_f = 29 \times R_1 = 29 \times 10\text{ k}\Omega = 290 \text{ k}\Omega$$

Use standard: $R_f = 300 \text{ k}\Omega$ (gives slight gain margin for safe startup)

### Step 4: Circuit Realization

- Op-Amp: $\mu A741$
- $R_1 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_f = 300 \text{ k}\Omega$
- Three RC sections: $R = 3.3 \text{ k}\Omega$, $C = 0.01 \mu F$
- Power supply: ±15 V

### Step 5: Simulation Verification (SPICE/Multisim)

Run transient analysis; observe sinusoidal output at ~2 kHz with amplitude approximately 13–14 V peak-to-peak (near saturation).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the complete functional flow of an Op-Amp based RC Phase Shift Oscillator, including signal flow, phase shifts, and the Barkhausen loop.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AMP["Inverting Amplifier Block"]
        A1["Op-Amp uA741"] --> A2["Gain A equals minus 29"]
        A2 --> A3["180 degree Phase Inversion"]
    end

    subgraph LADD["Three Stage RC Ladder Network"]
        B1["RC Section 1"] --> B2["RC Section 2"]
        B2 --> B3["RC Section 3"]
    end

    subgraph PHASE["Phase Accumulator"]
        P1["60 degree lag"] --> P2["60 degree lag"]
        P2 --> P3["60 degree lag"]
    end

    subgraph LOOP["Barkhausen Loop Condition"]
        L1["Loop Gain AB equals 1"] --> L2["Loop Phase equals 360 degree"]
        L2 --> L3["Sustained Sinusoidal Output"]
    end

    VOUT["Vo Sinusoidal Output"] --> AMP
    AMP -->|Drives| LADD
    LADD -->|Feedback Vf| AMP
    LADD --> PHASE
    AMP --> LOOP
    LADD --> LOOP
    LOOP --> VOUT

    style AMP fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px
    style LADD fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px
    style PHASE fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px
    style LOOP fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Signal Flow & Phase Shift Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SRC["Source Vo from Amplifier Output"] --> N1["Node 1 After RC1"]
    N1 --> N2["Node 2 After RC2"]
    N2 --> N3["Node 3 After RC3 Vf"]

    P1["Amplifier Output Vo Phase equals 0 degree reference"] --> P2["After RC1 Phase equals minus 60 degree"]
    P2 --> P3["After RC2 Phase equals minus 120 degree"]
    P3 --> P4["After RC3 Phase equals minus 180 degree"]
    P4 --> P5["After Amplifier Inversion Phase equals minus 360 degree equals 0 degree"]
    P5 --> P6["Total Loop Phase equals 0 degree Barkhausen Satisfied"]

    style SRC fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#DAA520,stroke-width:2px
    style P6 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px
```

## 4.3 Detailed Op-Amp RC Phase Shift Oscillator — Full Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SUPPLY["Dual Power Supply"]
        V1["+15V"]:::supply
        V2["-15V"]:::supply
    end

    subgraph OPAMP["Op-Amp Active Stage"]
        U1["uA741 Op-Amp"]:::opamp
        R1["R1 equals 10kohm"]:::resistor
        RF["Rf equals 300kohm Feedback Resistor"]:::resistor
    end

    subgraph LADDNET["RC Ladder Feedback Network"]
        RA["R equals 3.3kohm"]:::resistor
        RB["R equals 3.3kohm"]:::resistor
        RC1["R equals 3.3kohm"]:::resistor
        CA["C equals 0.01uF"]:::capacitor
        CB["C equals 0.01uF"]:::capacitor
        CC["C equals 0.01uF"]:::capacitor
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["Output Node"]
        VOUTNODE["Vo Sinusoidal Waveform Output"]:::output
    end

    RA --- CA
    RB --- CB
    RC1 --- CC

    VOUTNODE -->|Drives Ladder| RA
    RA --> RB
    RB --> RC1
    RC1 -->|Feedback Vf| R1
    R1 --> U1
    RF --> U1
    U1 --> VOUTNODE

    V1 -.-> U1
    V2 -.-> U1
```

# 🔬 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📚 PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### **Question 1: Define Barkhausen Criterion. State its two conditions for sustained oscillation. [3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Barkhausen Criterion states the necessary conditions for a feedback amplifier to produce sustained sinusoidal oscillations:

> **Condition 1 — Magnitude Condition:** The magnitude of the loop gain must be exactly unity (or marginally greater than 1 for startup):
> $$\vert A\beta \vert = 1$$

> **Condition 2 — Phase Condition:** The total phase shift around the closed loop must be an integer multiple of $360°$ (i.e., $0°$ or $360°$):
> $$\angle A\beta = 0° \text{ or } n \times 360°, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$$

If $\vert A\beta \vert > 1$, oscillations build up (and are limited by amplifier nonlinearity). If $\vert A\beta \vert < 1$, oscillations die out. If $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$, sustained oscillations occur.

---

### **Question 2: In an RC phase shift oscillator, why are exactly three RC sections used? [3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The RC phase shift oscillator requires its feedback network to produce exactly **180° of phase shift** at the oscillation frequency (since the inverting amplifier provides the other 180°).

- With **1 RC section**, the maximum possible phase shift is $90°$ (reached only at $f = \infty$).
- With **2 RC sections**, the total phase shift can approach $180°$ only as $f \to \infty$, which is impractical.
- With **3 RC sections**, the total phase shift reaches exactly $180°$ at a **finite, usable frequency** $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$.

Hence, three RC sections are the **minimum number required** to produce a real, finite oscillation frequency while still satisfying the 180° phase requirement of the feedback network. Using more than three sections is possible but complicates the analysis and is unnecessary.

---

## 📚 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks): Design and Analysis of RC Phase Shift Oscillator**

> `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module Experiment 2]`

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of an **op-amp based RC phase shift oscillator**. Derive the expression for the **frequency of oscillation** and the **minimum gain** required. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Design an RC phase shift oscillator using an op-amp to generate a **1 kHz sine wave**. Choose $C = 0.01 \mu F$ and $R_1 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$. Calculate all component values and verify whether the design satisfies the Barkhausen criterion. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Solution to Question A(a):**

**Circuit Description:**

The op-amp based RC phase shift oscillator consists of:

1. An inverting amplifier using op-amp (e.g., $\mu A741$) with gain $A = -\dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$.
2. A three-stage RC ladder network connected between the output $V_o$ and the inverting input.

**Working Principle:**

The op-amp in inverting configuration produces a **180° phase shift**. The three-stage RC ladder network, with each section contributing a 60° phase lag, produces a total phase shift of **180°** at the frequency $f_0$.

The feedback voltage $V_f$ is taken from the third node of the RC ladder and fed back to the inverting input of the op-amp.

**Derivation of Frequency of Oscillation:**

The feedback factor of the three-stage RC ladder (with all $R$, $C$ equal) is given by:

$$\beta = \frac{1}{(1 - 5\alpha^2) + j(6\alpha - \alpha^3)}, \quad \text{where } \alpha = \omega RC$$

For β to be real (so that the phase contribution of the network is exactly $0°$ or $180°$), the imaginary part must vanish:

$$6\alpha - \alpha^3 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha(6 - \alpha^2) = 0$$

Non-trivial solution: $\alpha^2 = 6 \Rightarrow \alpha = \sqrt{6}$, hence:

$$\boxed{\omega_0 = \frac{1}{RC\sqrt{6}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}}$$

**Derivation of Minimum Gain:**

At $\alpha^2 = 6$, the real part of the denominator becomes:

$$1 - 5\alpha^2 = 1 - 5(6) = -29$$

Hence:

$$\beta = \frac{1}{-29} = -\frac{1}{29}, \quad \vert \beta \vert = \frac{1}{29}$$

By the Barkhausen magnitude condition $\vert A\beta \vert \geq 1$:

$$\vert A \vert \geq \frac{1}{\vert \beta \vert} = 29$$

$$\boxed{\vert A_{min} \vert = 29}$$

For the op-amp inverting amplifier: $A = -R_f/R_1$, hence $R_f = 29 R_1$.

---

#### **Solution to Question A(b):**

**Given:** $f_0 = 1 \text{ kHz}$, $C = 0.01 \mu F$, $R_1 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$

**Step 1: Calculate R from the frequency formula.**

$$R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_0 C \sqrt{6}}$$

$$R = \frac{1}{2 \times 3.1416 \times 1000 \times 0.01 \times 10^{-6} \times 2.449}$$

$$R = \frac{1}{1.539 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 6497 \text{ }\Omega$$

**Use standard value:** $R = 6.8 \text{ k}\Omega$

**Step 2: Verify frequency with chosen R.**

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2 \times 3.1416 \times 6800 \times 0.01 \times 10^{-6} \times 2.449}$$

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{1.0464 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 955.7 \text{ Hz}$$

This is close to 1 kHz (within ~4.5% error — acceptable for practical design).

**Step 3: Calculate R_f for gain = 29.**

$$R_f = 29 \times R_1 = 29 \times 10 \text{ k}\Omega = 290 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Use standard value:** $R_f = 300 \text{ k}\Omega$ (gives gain $A = -30$ for slight startup margin).

**Step 4: Verify Barkhausen Criterion.**

- Phase: $180°$ (amp) + $180°$ (ladder) = $360°$ ✓
- Magnitude: $\vert A\beta \vert = 30 \times (1/29) = 1.034 > 1$ ✓ (oscillations will build up and stabilize due to op-amp saturation)

**Final Design Summary:**

| Component | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| $C_1 = C_2 = C_3$ | $0.01 \mu F$ |
| $R$ (ladder) | $6.8 \text{ k}\Omega$ |
| $R_1$ | $10 \text{ k}\Omega$ |
| $R_f$ | $300 \text{ k}\Omega$ |
| Op-Amp | $\mu A741$ |
| Supply | $\pm 15 \text{ V}$ |
| Expected $f_0$ | $\approx 955.7 \text{ Hz}$ |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): BJT-Based RC Phase Shift Oscillator & Simulation**

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 | Module Experiment 2]`

**(a)** Draw the circuit diagram of a **BJT-based RC phase shift oscillator** and explain its operation. Compare it with the op-amp version. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** For a BJT-based RC phase shift oscillator with $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $C = 0.01 \mu F$, and $R_C = 22 \text{ k}\Omega$, calculate: **(i)** the **frequency of oscillation**, **(ii)** the **minimum h_fe** required, and **(iii)** the **attenuation factor** of the RC network. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Solution to Question B(a):**

**Circuit Diagram (BJT Version):**

A common-emitter (CE) configured BJT amplifier (e.g., BC107) provides the 180° phase inversion. The collector is connected to the RC ladder, and the feedback is taken from the end of the ladder back to the base.

**Operation:**

1. The CE amplifier provides a **180° phase shift** between base and collector.
2. The three-stage RC ladder adds another **180°** at $f_0$.
3. Total loop phase = 360° → Barkhausen phase condition satisfied.
4. The gain of the CE stage must compensate the attenuation ($\vert \beta \vert = 1/29$) of the ladder, so $\vert A \vert \geq 29$.

**Comparison: BJT vs Op-Amp Version**
