| Feature | BJT Version | Op-Amp Version |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Active device | BJT (BC107, 2N2222) | Op-Amp ($\mu A741$, LM358) |
| Gain control | Set by $R_C$ and $R_E$ | Set by $R_f$ and $R_1$ |
| Minimum gain | $h_{fe} \geq 44.5$ (optimum) | $\vert A \vert \geq 29$ |
| Output amplitude | Limited by transistor cut-off/saturation | Limited by op-amp saturation (±13V) |
| Frequency range | 5 Hz to 500 kHz | 1 Hz to 1 MHz |
| Power consumption | Low (single supply) | Higher (dual supply ±15V) |
| Input impedance | Moderate | Very high |
| Stability | Affected by temperature | Highly stable |
| Cost | Low | Moderate |

---

#### **Solution to Question B(b):**

**Given:** $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $C = 0.01 \mu F$, $R_C = 22 \text{ k}\Omega$

**(i) Frequency of Oscillation:**

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC \sqrt{6}}$$

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2 \times 3.1416 \times 10^4 \times 0.01 \times 10^{-6} \times 2.449}$$

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{1.539 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 649.7 \text{ Hz}$$

$$\boxed{f_0 \approx 650 \text{ Hz}}$$

**(ii) Minimum h_fe Required:**

For BJT version, the gain condition gives:

$$h_{fe(min)} = 4k + 23 + \frac{29}{k}, \quad \text{where } k = \frac{R_C}{R}$$

Calculate k:

$$k = \frac{22 \text{ k}\Omega}{10 \text{ k}\Omega} = 2.2$$

Substitute:

$$h_{fe(min)} = 4(2.2) + 23 + \frac{29}{2.2}$$

$$= 8.8 + 23 + 13.18 = 44.98$$

$$\boxed{h_{fe(min)} \approx 45}$$

**(iii) Attenuation Factor of RC Network:**

The magnitude of the feedback factor (attenuation) at the oscillation frequency:

$$\vert \beta \vert = \frac{1}{29} \approx 0.0345$$

In decibels:

$$\vert \beta \vert_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{29}\right) = -29.25 \text{ dB}$$

$$\boxed{\vert \beta \vert = \frac{1}{29} \approx 0.0345 \quad (\text{or } -29.25 \text{ dB})}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Forgetting the √6 constant:** Many students write $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$, missing the crucial $\sqrt{6}$ factor. This causes a 2.45× error in the frequency. Maximum marks deduction: **2 marks**.
> 2. **Using 4 RC sections:** Some students add a fourth RC section by mistake. The standard topology uses **exactly three**. The √6 constant changes to √29 for a 4-section ladder.
> 3. **Confusing BJT and Op-Amp formulas:** The BJT version uses $h_{fe(min)} = 4k + 23 + 29/k$ (≈ 45), not 29. The op-amp version uses $\vert A \vert = 29$. Mixing these up costs **3–4 marks**.
> 4. **Forgetting Barkhausen Criterion:** Any design answer must explicitly state the **two conditions** and **verify** them. Marks are lost if verification is missing.
> 5. **Using non-standard component values:** Always quote **standard E12/E24 values** (1.0, 1.2, 1.5, 1.8, 2.2, 2.7, 3.3, 3.9, 4.7, 5.6, 6.8, 8.2 kΩ, etc.) in design problems.
> 6. **Not mentioning amplitude stabilization:** In practical design, a slight gain margin (e.g., $A = 30$ instead of 29) is used to ensure reliable startup. Mention this to fetch **extra marks**.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🚀 Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU PCAEL508 Module 2:**

- 🔹 RC Phase Shift Oscillator is an **audio-frequency sinusoidal oscillator** using resistors and capacitors (no inductors).
- 🔹 It uses a **3-stage RC ladder** to produce **180° phase shift** at $f_0$.
- 🔹 The amplifier (inverting CE or op-amp) provides the other **180°**, completing 360°.
- 🔹 **Frequency formula:** $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$ — the √6 ≈ 2.449 is **non-negotiable**.
- 🔹 **Feedback factor:** $\vert \beta \vert = \dfrac{1}{29}$ — this is a constant, not frequency-dependent at $f_0$.
- 🔹 **Minimum op-amp gain:** $\vert A_{min} \vert = 29 \Rightarrow R_f = 29 R_1$.
- 🔹 **Minimum BJT current gain:** $h_{fe(min)} \approx 45$ (for optimum design, $k = \sqrt{29}/2$).
- 🔹 **Barkhausen Criterion** must be satisfied: $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ AND $\angle A\beta = 0°$ or $360°$.
- 🔹 In simulation, use a slight gain margin (e.g., $A = 30$) to guarantee **startup of oscillations**.
- 🔹 **Op-amp version** is preferred for **better amplitude stability and higher input impedance**.
- 🔹 **BJT version** is preferred for **single-supply, low-power, and cost-sensitive applications**.
- 🔹 Typical lab component values: $C = 0.01 \mu F$, $R = 6.8 \text{–} 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_1 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_f = 300 \text{ k}\Omega$.
- 🔹 Typical measured frequency in lab: **500 Hz to 2 kHz** for audio applications.
- 🔹 **Number of RC sections = 3 is minimum** for a finite, real oscillation frequency.
- 🔹 The output amplitude is limited by **op-amp saturation** (typically ±13V for ±15V supply).
- 🔹 **Comparison with Wien Bridge:** RC phase shift has **poorer frequency stability** but is **simpler to design** at low frequencies.
- 🔹 For PCB/breadboard implementation, use **matched R and C values** (1% tolerance) for accurate $f_0$.
- 🔹 In NI Multisim/SPICE, use **transient analysis** with stop time = 5–10 periods of expected $f_0$.
- 🔹 Always **compare theoretical, simulated, and measured** $f_0$ in the lab record with **error percentage**.

> **🎯 Final Tip:** For KTU 2024 lab exams, remember to bring your **DSO screenshots**, **Multisim transient plots**, and a **tabulated comparison of theoretical vs. measured values** — these fetch the highest marks in the practical record.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Active First and Second Order Filters (LPF, BPF, and BRF)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAEL508 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS AND SIMULATION LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Active First and Second Order Filters (LPF, BPF, and BRF) ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is an Active Filter? An **Active Filter** is a frequency-selective electronic network that uses one or more **active components** (typically operational amplifiers, transistors, or gyrators) combined with **passive components** (resistors and capacitors) to achieve a desired frequency response. Unlike passive filters, active filters can provide **voltage gain**, **impedance buffering**, and are capable of **signal amplification** while filtering. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** An active filter is an analog electronic filter that employs amplifying elements (mainly op-amps) with passive RC components to realize low-pass, high-pass, band-pass, or band-reject (band-stop) transfer functions with defined cutoff frequencies and quality factors. ### 1.2 Classification Based on Frequency Response | Filter Type | Passes | Attenuates | Common Use | |---|---|---|---| | **LPF** (Low Pass Filter) | Low frequencies (0 to $f_c$) | High frequencies ($> f_c$) | Audio bass, anti-aliasing | | **HPF** (High Pass Filter) | High frequencies ($> f_c$) | Low frequencies (0 to $f_c$) | DC blocking, AC coupling | | **BPF** (Band Pass Filter) | A specific band ($f_L$ to $f_H$) | Frequencies outside the band | Radio tuning, communication | | **BRF** (Band Reject / Notch) | All except a specific band | A specific narrow band | 50 Hz hum removal, noise rejection | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Volume Knob for Frequencies" > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a music studio mixer. Each slider on the equalizer (EQ) panel is essentially an active filter. The "Bass" slider is a **Low Pass Filter** — it lets only the low rumbling frequencies pass through to your sub-woofer. The "Treble" slider is a **High Pass Filter** — it removes the bass muddiness and lets only crisp high notes through. A **Band Pass Filter** is like tuning your FM radio to exactly 98.3 MHz — you want only that narrow station, not others. A **Band Reject Filter** is the opposite — like the **noise-canceling headphones** that block only the 50 Hz transformer hum of an aeroplane but let you hear the music clearly. ### 1.4 Why Use Active Filters? - **Gain Capability**: Op-amps can amplify the filtered signal. - **No Loading Effect**: High input impedance and low output impedance provide **isolation** between stages. - **Inductorless Design**: At low frequencies, large inductors are bulky; active filters replace them with **RC networks + op-amps**. - **Cascadability**: Stages can be easily cascaded for higher order responses. - **Tunability**: Cutoff frequency can be adjusted by changing R or C. ### 1.5 Key Parameters (KTU High-Yield Terms) - **Cutoff Frequency ($f_c$ or $f_{-3dB}$)**: The frequency at which output power falls to **50%** of its maximum (voltage falls to **0.707** of its maximum), i.e., a **−3 dB** point. - **Passband**: The range of frequencies passed with minimal attenuation. - **Stopband**: The range of frequencies heavily attenuated. - **Roll-off Rate**: The rate at which gain decreases beyond cutoff, expressed in **dB/decade** or **dB/octave**. - **Quality Factor (Q)**: For BPF/BRF, $Q = \frac{f_0}{BW}$ where $BW$ is the bandwidth. - **Order of the Filter**: Determined by the number of reactive components; higher order = sharper roll-off. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Order vs Roll-off:** A **first-order** filter rolls off at **−20 dB/decade**. A **second-order** filter rolls off at **−40 dB/decade**. Doubling the order doubles the steepness of attenuation. --- ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 First-Order Active Filters — The Building Blocks A **first-order filter** contains only one reactive element (capacitor) in its RC network, providing a single pole in the transfer function. The op-amp is used in non-inverting or inverting configuration to provide gain and isolation. #### 2.1.1 First-Order Active Low Pass Filter (LPF) **Operational Logic Steps:** - The input signal is applied to an **RC low-pass network** (series R, shunt C to ground). - The voltage across the capacitor is the filtered output — at low frequencies, capacitor impedance is **high** ($X_C = \frac{1}{2\pi fC}$), so output ≈ input. - At high frequencies, $X_C$ becomes **small**, shunting the signal to ground. - This RC voltage is fed to a **non-inverting op-amp** providing gain $A_v = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_i}$ (also called a *unity-gain buffer* when $R_f = 0$). **Cutoff Frequency Derivation:** At cutoff, $|X_C| = R$, i.e., $2\pi f_c C = \frac{1}{R}$, giving: $$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$ **Transfer Function (Magnitude Response):** The gain magnitude is: $$A_v(f) = \frac{A_0}{\sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{f}{f_c}\right)^2}}$$ where $A_0 = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_i}$ is the DC (passband) gain. #### 2.1.2 First-Order Active High Pass Filter (HPF) **Operational Logic Steps:** - The input is applied to a **series capacitor** followed by a **shunt resistor** to ground. - At low frequencies, $X_C$ is **large**, blocking DC and low frequencies. - At high frequencies, $X_C$ is **small**, allowing signals to pass. - Followed by a non-inverting op-amp for gain and isolation. **Cutoff Frequency:** $$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$ Same formula as LPF, but the frequency band roles are reversed. #### 2.1.3 First-Order Active Band Pass Filter (BPF) — Cascaded Approach A simple BPF is built by cascading a **first-order HPF** and a **first-order LPF** with an op-amp buffer between them. - Lower cutoff: $f_L = \frac{1}{2\pi R_1 C_1}$ (from HPF section) - Upper cutoff: $f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_2 C_2}$ (from LPF section) - Center frequency: $f_0 = \sqrt{f_L \cdot f_H}$ - Bandwidth: $BW = f_H - f_L$ ### 2.2 Second-Order Active Filters — The Sallen-Key Topology A **second-order filter** contains **two reactive elements**, providing **two poles** and a roll-off of **−40 dB/decade**. The most common configuration is the **Sallen-Key** topology (also called *Voltage-Controlled Voltage-Source* or VCVS filter), which uses a single op-amp with two RC sections. #### 2.2.1 Second-Order Sallen-Key LPF **Operational Logic Steps:** - Two capacitors $C_1$ and $C_2$ are arranged in a feedback ladder with resistors $R_1$ and $R_2$. - The op-amp provides a **non-inverting gain** of $K = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_i}$. - The feedback path through $C_2$ to the op-amp's non-inverting input provides positive feedback that sharpens the roll-off. - The output of the op-amp is the filtered low-pass signal. **Standard Transfer Function (Unity Gain Case, $K = 1$):** $$H(s) = \frac{\omega_0^2}{s^2 + s \cdot \frac{\omega_0}{Q} + \omega_0^2}$$ where $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}$ is the natural (resonant) frequency and $Q$ is the quality factor. **Cutoff Frequency:** $$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}$$ #### 2.2.2 Second-Order Sallen-Key HPF The HPF is the **dual** of the LPF — swap R's and C's positions. The capacitors are now in series, and resistors are shunt to ground. $$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}$$ Same cutoff formula, but the transfer function numerator is $s^2$ instead of $\omega_0^2$. #### 2.2.3 Multiple Feedback (MFB) Band Pass Filter For a **true second-order BPF** with high Q, the **Multiple Feedback topology** is preferred. **Center Frequency:** $$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi C}\sqrt{\frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1 R_2 R_3}}$$ **Quality Factor:** $$Q = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{R_3(R_1 + R_2)}{R_1 R_2}}$$ **Center Frequency Gain ($H_0$):** $$H_0 = -\frac{R_3}{2R_1}$$ #### 2.2.4 Twin-T Notch Filter (Band Reject / BRF) The **Twin-T notch filter** uses two T-networks: one low-pass T and one high-pass T, summed at the op-amp input. **Notch Frequency:** $$f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$ The Twin-T provides a **sharp notch** at $f_n$, ideally with **infinite attenuation** at that frequency and **unity gain** elsewhere. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Examiner's Focus:** The most asked Sallen-Key variants are **(a) LPF**, **(b) HPF**, and **(c) Notch (BRF) using Twin-T or active Wien-Robinson topology**. Students MUST remember the relationship $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$ and the Q-factor expressions. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | Filter Type | Topology | Cutoff / Notch Frequency | Roll-off | Quality Factor Q | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1st Order LPF | Non-inverting + RC | $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | −20 dB/decade | Not applicable | | 1st Order HPF | Non-inverting + CR | $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | −20 dB/decade | Not applicable | | 1st Order BPF | Cascade HPF + LPF | $f_L, f_H$ separate | −20 dB/dec on each side | $Q = \frac{f_0}{f_H - f_L}$ | | 2nd Order Sallen-Key LPF | Sallen-Key VCVS | $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}$ | −40 dB/decade | $\frac{1}{Q} = 3 - K$ for equal R, C | | 2nd Order Sallen-Key HPF | Sallen-Key VCVS (dual) | $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}$ | −40 dB/decade | $\frac{1}{Q} = 3 - K$ for equal R, C | | MFB Band Pass | Multiple Feedback | $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi C}\sqrt{\frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1 R_2 R_3}}$ | ±20 dB/decade | $\frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{R_3(R_1 + R_2)}{R_1 R_2}}$ | | Twin-T Notch | Two T-networks + op-amp | $f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Sharp null | $Q = \frac{1}{4(1 - K)}$ for active variants | > [!NOTE] > In the formulas above, $\vert x \vert$ is not used, but where magnitude appears, it is denoted by single $\vert$ characters only outside the markdown table to avoid syntax conflicts. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Audio Equipment**: Graphic equalizers, sub-woofers, and tweeters. - **Communication Systems**: IF (Intermediate Frequency) stages in superheterodyne receivers. - **Instrumentation**: Anti-aliasing filter before ADC, removing 50 Hz power line hum. - **Biomedical**: ECG/EEG signal conditioning to remove baseline drift and high-frequency noise. - **Control Systems**: Noise suppression in feedback loops. --- ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: First-Order Active Low Pass Filter **Circuit Description:** A series resistor $R$ is connected from input to the non-inverting terminal of an op-amp. A capacitor $C$ connects from that same node to ground. The op-amp is configured as a non-inverting amplifier with gain $A_0 = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_i}$. **Step 1 — Voltage Divider Across the RC Network:** The output of the RC network is the voltage across the capacitor: $$V_c(s) = V_{in}(s) \cdot \frac{\frac{1}{sC}}{R + \frac{1}{sC}}$$ **Step 2 — Simplification (multiply numerator and denominator by $sC$):** $$V_c(s) = V_{in}(s) \cdot \frac{1}{1 + sRC}$$ **Step 3 — Apply Non-Inverting Op-Amp Gain:** The op-amp multiplies the voltage at its non-inverting input by $A_0$: $$V_{out}(s) = A_0 \cdot V_c(s) = A_0 \cdot \frac{V_{in}(s)}{1 + sRC}$$ **Step 4 — Transfer Function:** $$H(s) = \frac{V_{out}(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = \frac{A_0}{1 + sRC}$$ **Step 5 — Identify the Pole and Cutoff:** The pole occurs where the denominator equals zero: $1 + sRC = 0 \Rightarrow s = -\frac{1}{RC}$. For a sinusoid $s = j\omega$: $$H(j\omega) = \frac{A_0}{1 + j\omega RC}$$ **Step 6 — Magnitude Response:** $$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{A_0}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$$ **Step 7 — Cutoff Frequency (3-dB point):** At $f_c$, $\vert H \vert = \frac{A_0}{\sqrt{2}}$: $$\frac{A_0}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega_c RC)^2}} = \frac{A_0}{\sqrt{2}} \Rightarrow (\omega_c RC)^2 = 1$$ $$\omega_c = \frac{1}{RC} \Rightarrow f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The pole is at $s = -\frac{1}{RC}$ on the real axis. The **−20 dB/decade** roll-off is a direct consequence of this single pole. ### 3.2 Derivation: Second-Order Sallen-Key LPF Transfer Function **Circuit Description:** Two RC sections ($R_1, C_1$ in series; $R_2, C_2$ as the feedback shunt) with an op-amp providing gain $K = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_i}$ in non-inverting configuration. **Step 1 — KCL at Node A (between $R_1$ and $R_2$):** Let $V_A$ be the voltage at the junction of $R_1$ and $R_2$ (which is also the op-amp non-inverting input): $$\frac{V_{in} - V_A}{R_1} = \frac{V_A - V_{out}}{R_2} + \frac{V_A - V_{op}}{Z_{C1}}$$ where $Z_{C1} = \frac{1}{sC_1}$ and $V_{op}$ is the output of the op-amp first stage. **Step 2 — Recognize $V_{op} = K \cdot V_A$ (non-inverting gain):** Substituting: $$\frac{V_{in} - V_A}{R_1} = \frac{V_A - K V_A}{R_2} + sC_1(V_A - K V_A)$$ **Step 3 — Simplify the right side:** $$= V_A(1 - K)\left[\frac{1}{R_2} + sC_1\right]$$ **Step 4 — Apply KCL at Node B (op-amp inverting input is virtual ground via feedback):** After algebraic manipulation (full derivation): $$\frac{V_{in} - V_A}{R_1} = V_A(1 - K)\left[\frac{1}{R_2} + sC_1\right] - sC_2 V_A$$ **Step 5 — Solving for $\frac{V_A}{V_{in}}$ and substituting $V_{out} = K V_A$:** $$H(s) = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{K \cdot \frac{1}{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}{s^2 + s\left[\frac{1}{R_1 C_1} + \frac{1}{R_2 C_1} + \frac{1-K}{R_2 C_2}\right] + \frac{1}{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}$$ **Step 6 — Standard Form Identification:** Comparing with $H(s) = \frac{K\omega_0^2}{s^2 + s\frac{\omega_0}{Q} + \omega_0^2}$: $$\omega_0^2 = \frac{1}{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}$$ $$\frac{\omega_0}{Q} = \frac{1}{R_1 C_1} + \frac{1}{R_2 C_1} + \frac{1 - K}{R_2 C_2}$$ **Step 7 — Simplification for Equal Components ($R_1 = R_2 = R$, $C_1 = C_2 = C$):** $$\omega_0 = \frac{1}{RC} \Rightarrow f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$ $$\frac{1}{Q} = 3 - K \Rightarrow Q = \frac{1}{3 - K}$$ > [!NOTE] > This last equation is **the most critical KTU 2024 relationship**: for Sallen-Key LPF with equal R's and C's, the quality factor is determined entirely by the gain $K$. When $K = 1$ (unity), $Q = 0.5$ (Butterworth-like maximally flat response is approached at $K = \sqrt{2}$, giving $Q = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$). ### 3.3 Derivation: Twin-T Notch Filter (BRF) **Circuit Description:** The Twin-T network consists of two T-sections: - **Low-pass T**: Two series resistors $R$ and a shunt capacitor $2C$ to ground. - **High-pass T**: Two series capacitors $C$ and a shunt resistor $R/2$ to ground. The two T-networks are connected in **parallel** from input to op-amp input. The op-amp provides a fixed gain of $K$. **Step 1 — Transfer Function of the Passive Twin-T (no op-amp):** The combined admittance of the parallel T's yields: $$H_{TT}(s) = \frac{s^2 + \omega_n^2}{s^2 + 4s\omega_n + \omega_n^2}$$ where $\omega_n = \frac{1}{RC}$. **Step 2 — Active Twin-T with Op-Amp Gain K:** The op-amp in non-inverting configuration multiplies the signal. The numerator contains $s^2 + \omega_n^2$, which equals **zero** at $s = \pm j\omega_n$. **Step 3 — Notch Frequency:** At $s = j\omega_n = j2\pi f_n$: $$\omega_n = \frac{1}{RC} \Rightarrow f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$ **Step 4 — Quality Factor for Active Twin-T:** $$Q = \frac{1}{4(1 - K)}$$ **Step 5 — Behavior at $f_n$:** - Numerator $= 0$, so $V_{out} = 0$ (theoretically infinite attenuation at $f_n$). - The notch width is determined by $Q$: higher $Q$ → narrower notch. ### 3.4 Summary of Pole Locations and Stability | Filter | Number of Poles | Pole Locations | Stability | |---|---|---|---| | 1st Order LPF | 1 | $s = -\frac{1}{RC}$ (real, LHP) | Always stable | | 1st Order HPF | 1 | $s = -\frac{1}{RC}$ (real, LHP) | Always stable | | 2nd Order LPF | 2 | Complex conjugate pair in LHP | Stable if $Q > 0$ | | 2nd Order BPF | 2 | Complex conjugate pair in LHP | Stable if $K < 3$ for equal R, C | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Stability Rule for Sallen-Key:** The op-amp must have sufficient **slew rate** and **gain-bandwidth product (GBW)** such that $GBW \geq 100 \cdot f_0$ to avoid distortion at the cutoff. Forgetting this is a common KTU lab-viva mistake. --- ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of an Active Filter System ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[Input Signal Source
Sine Wave Function Generator] --> nodeB[First RC Network
R1 and C1] nodeB --> nodeC[Op-Amp Stage
Non-Inverting Amplifier] nodeC --> nodeD[Second RC Network
R2 and C2] nodeD --> nodeE[Output to CRO / DSO
Filtered Waveform Display] nodeF[DC Power Supply
Plus Minus 15 Volt] --> nodeC nodeG[Cutoff Frequency Calculator
fc equals 1 by 2 pi RC] -.-> nodeB nodeG -.-> nodeD nodeH[Quality Factor Controller
Rf and Ri Gain Resistors] -.-> nodeC style nodeA fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style nodeE fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style nodeG fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style nodeH fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.2 Sallen-Key LPF Architecture (Block-Level Functional View) ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA[Vin Input] --> nodeR1[Resistor R1] nodeR1 --> nodeN1[Node A
Junction Point] nodeN1 --> nodeR2[Resistor R2] nodeR2 --> nodeVout[Vout Output] nodeN1 --> nodeC1[Capacitor C1] nodeC1 --> nodeN2[Node B
Op-Amp Positive Input] nodeN2 --> nodeC2[Capacitor C2] nodeC2 --> nodeVout nodeN2 --> nodeOP[Op-Amp IC741
Non-Inverting Configuration] nodeOP --> nodeVout nodeOP -.-> nodeFB[Feedback Network
Rf and Ri Determines Gain K] style nodeN1 fill:#FFFF99,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style nodeN2 fill:#FFFF99,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style nodeOP fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.3 Twin-T Notch Filter Functional Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[Vin Input Signal] --> nodeB1[Low Pass T Section
R series plus 2C shunt] nodeA --> nodeB2[High Pass T Section
C series plus R by 2 shunt] nodeB1 --> nodeC[Summing Junction at Op-Amp Input] nodeB2 --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD[Op-Amp IC741
Gain K equals 1 plus Rf by Ri] nodeD --> nodeE[Output Vout
Notch at fn equals 1 by 2 pi RC] style nodeB1 fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style nodeB2 fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style nodeE fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.4 Frequency Response Comparison (Bode Plot Description) ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Filter Type Selection] --> nodeB[First Order LPF] nodeA --> nodeC[Second Order LPF] nodeA --> nodeD[Band Pass Filter] nodeA --> nodeE[Notch BRF] nodeB --> nodeF[Slope: Minus 20 dB per decade
Cutoff: fc equals 1 by 2 pi RC] nodeC --> nodeG[Slope: Minus 40 dB per decade
Cutoff: f0 equals 1 by 2 pi sqrt R1 R2 C1 C2] nodeD --> nodeH[Center f0 and Bandwidth BW
Q equals f0 divided by BW] nodeE --> nodeI[Notch at fn equals 1 by 2 pi RC
Infinite attenuation at fn] style nodeA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style nodeF fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px style nodeG fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px style nodeH fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px style nodeI fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px ``` ### 4.5 Simulation / Hardware Setup Block Diagram (KTU Lab) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[Function Generator
0.1 Hz to 1 MHz] --> nodeB[Breadboard with Filter Circuit
IC741 plus RC Components] nodeB --> nodeC[CRO or DSO
Channel 1 Input and Channel 2 Output] nodeD[Plus Minus 15 V Dual Power Supply] --> nodeB nodeE[Component List
Resistors 1k, 10k, Capacitors 0.01uF, 0.1uF] -.-> nodeB style nodeA fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px nodeD --> nodeB style nodeB fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style nodeC fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px ``` --- ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]** **Define an active filter. List any two advantages of active filters over passive filters.** **Model Answer:** An active filter is an electronic filter circuit that uses active components (typically op-amps like IC741) along with passive components (R and C) to realize a desired frequency-selective response. **Two Advantages:** 1. **Gain Capability**: Active filters can provide voltage gain greater than unity in the passband, whereas passive filters always have a gain $\leq 1$ (insertion loss). 2. **No Inductors Required**: Active filters can realize inductor-like behavior using RC networks and op-amps, eliminating bulky and expensive inductors — especially useful at low frequencies. 3. (Bonus) High input impedance and low output impedance eliminate loading effects during cascading. --- #### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **What is meant by cutoff frequency of a filter? Write the expression for the cutoff frequency of a first-order RC low pass filter.** **Model Answer:** The **cutoff frequency ($f_c$)** of a filter is the frequency at which the output signal power drops to **50%** of its maximum passband value, equivalently where the output voltage falls to $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707$ of its maximum value. This corresponds to a **−3 dB** attenuation point. **Expression:** $$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$ where $R$ is the resistance in ohms ($\Omega$) and $C$ is the capacitance in farads (F), giving $f_c$ in hertz (Hz). --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam - July 2024, Supplied]** **(a)** Design a **first-order active low pass filter** using op-amp IC741 with cutoff frequency $f_c = 1\text{ kHz}$ and passband gain $A_0 = 2$. Assume $C = 0.1\ \mu F$. Draw the circuit diagram and derive the transfer function. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With the same circuit, calculate the output voltage magnitude when input is $V_{in} = 1\text{ V}$ peak at frequency $f = 5\text{ kHz}$. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Design Procedure:** Given: $f_c = 1\text{ kHz} = 1000\text{ Hz}$, $A_0 = 2$, $C = 0.1\ \mu F = 0.1 \times 10^{-6}\text{ F}$. **Step 1** — Calculate R from cutoff formula: $$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} \Rightarrow R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_c C}$$ $$R = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 1000 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$R = \frac{1}{6.2832 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 1591.5\ \Omega \approx 1.59\text{ k}\Omega$$ Choose standard value: **$R = 1.6\text{ k}\Omega$**. **Step 2** — Determine feedback resistors for gain $A_0 = 2$: $$A_0 = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_i} = 2 \Rightarrow R_f = R_i$$ Choose $R_i = R_f = 10\text{ k}\Omega$ (standard value). **Step 3** — Circuit: Op-amp non-inverting input receives voltage from the RC network ($R$ in series, $C$ shunt). Output is the filtered, amplified signal. **Step 4** — Transfer Function (as derived in Section 3.1): $$H(s) = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{A_0}{1 + sRC} = \frac{2}{1 + s(1.6\text{ k}\Omega)(0.1\ \mu F)}$$ --- **(b) Calculation at $f = 5\text{ kHz}$:** **Step 1** — Compute the magnitude: $$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{A_0}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_c)^2}} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{1 + (5000/1000)^2}} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{1 + 25}} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{26}}$$ **Step 2** — Numerical evaluation: $$\vert H \vert = \frac{2}{5.099} \approx 0.392$$ **Step 3** — Output voltage: $$V_{out} = \vert H \vert \times V_{in} = 0.392 \times 1\text{ V} = 0.392\text{ V peak}$$ **Step 4** — Phase shift: $$\phi = -\arctan\left(\frac{f}{f_c}\right) = -\arctan(5) = -78.69^\circ$$ **Comment:** At $f = 5\text{ kHz} = 5 f_c$ (one decade above cutoff), the output is attenuated to about **20%** of input (since 0.392/2 ≈ 0.196 of the passband gain), and there is a phase lag of approximately **78.7°**, consistent with the −20 dB/decade roll-off characteristic of a first-order LPF. --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **(a)** Design a **second-order Sallen-Key low pass filter** with cutoff frequency $f_0 = 10\text{ kHz}$ and quality factor $Q = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ (Butterworth response). Assume $C_1 = C_2 = 10\text{ nF}$. Derive the transfer function and find the op-amp gain K. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the operation of a **Twin-T Notch Filter** (BRF) for eliminating 50 Hz power line hum. Calculate R and C values for $f_n = 50\text{ Hz}$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Sallen-Key LPF Design:** Given: $f_0 = 10\text{ kHz}$, $Q = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707$ (Butterworth), $C_1 = C_2 = C = 10\text{ nF}$. **Step 1** — Using equal R, C simplification: $R_1 = R_2 = R$ and $C_1 = C_2 = C$. $$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} \Rightarrow R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_0 C}$$ $$R = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10000 \times 10 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{1}{6.2832 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 1591.5\ \Omega$$ Choose standard **$R = 1.6\text{ k}\Omega$**. **Step 2** — Find K from Q: For equal R and C: $\frac{1}{Q} = 3 - K$ $$K = 3 - \frac{1}{Q} = 3 - \sqrt{2} = 3 - 1.414 = 1.586$$ **Step 3** — Determine $R_f$ and $R_i$: $$K = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_i} = 1.586 \Rightarrow \frac{R_f}{R_i} = 0.586$$ Choose $R_i = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, then $R_f = 5.86\text{ k}\Omega \approx 5.6\text{ k}\Omega$ (standard). **Step 4** — Transfer Function: $$H(s) = \frac{1.586 \cdot \omega_0^2}{s^2 + s\frac{\omega_0}{Q} + \omega_0^2}$$ where $\omega_0 = 2\pi \times 10000 = 62831.85\text{ rad/s}$. **Step 5** — Magnitude at $f = f_0$: At cutoff, $\vert H \vert = \frac{K \cdot Q}{\sqrt{1 + (Q^2 - 1)^2}} \cdot \text{...} $ — for Butterworth, $\vert H(f_0) \vert = \frac{K}{\sqrt{2}}$. --- **(b) Twin-T Notch Filter for 50 Hz Hum:** **Principle:** The Twin-T network provides **infinite attenuation at $f_n$** and unity gain elsewhere. To remove 50 Hz hum from a power line interference in audio/signaling circuits, the notch frequency must be exactly 50 Hz. **Step 1** — Design equation: $$f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} = 50\text{ Hz}$$ **Step 2** — Choose $C = 0.1\ \mu F = 0.1 \times 10^{-6}\text{ F}$: $$R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_n C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 50 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$R = \frac{1}{3.1416 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 31830\ \Omega \approx 33\text{ k}\Omega$$ **Step 3** — Component selection for the Twin-T: - Low-pass T: Two resistors of **33 kΩ** in series and a shunt capacitor of **0.2 μF** ($2C$). - High-pass T: Two capacitors of **0.1 μF** in series and a shunt resistor of **16.5 kΩ** ($R/2$). **Step 4** — Active version with op-amp gain $K$ for tunable Q: $$Q = \frac{1}{4(1 - K)}$$ To get a sharp notch (high Q), $K$ should be slightly less than 1. For $K = 0.95$, $Q = 5$. **Operation:** At 50 Hz, the numerator of the transfer function is zero, so the output is zero. At all other frequencies, the filter passes the signal with minimal attenuation. This effectively eliminates the **50 Hz hum** picked up from AC power lines, common in biomedical (ECG) and audio recordings. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting units in $f_c$ formula** — Always convert $C$ to farads and $R$ to ohms before computation. Writing $R$ in kΩ without converting causes a factor of **1000** error. > 2. **Confusing HPF and LPF cutoff formulas** — Both have the same $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$ expression, but their *responses* are mirrored. > 3. **Wrong K for Sallen-Key Q** — The relation $\frac{1}{Q} = 3 - K$ applies **only when $R_1 = R_2$ and $C_1 = C_2$**. Writing it for unequal components is a 3-mark penalty. > 4. **Forgetting to derive the transfer function** — In KTU valuation, the question "design and derive" requires both the **circuit constants** AND the **mathematical transfer function**. Skipping the derivation costs up to **4 marks**. > 5. **Confusing Twin-T passive vs active** — Passive Twin-T has limited Q; **active Twin-T** with op-amp gain K near 1 gives a much sharper notch. Students often forget the $+R_f, R_i$ feedback network. > 6. **Phase response ignored** — KTU often asks "comment on phase shift". Always state the **−90°** lag at very high frequencies for first-order LPF. > 7. **Bandwidth vs Center frequency** — In BPF, $BW = f_H - f_L$ and $Q = \frac{f_0}{BW}$. Swapping these is a frequent error. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **Active filter** = Op-amp + RC network; provides gain + filtering. - **First-order** roll-off = **−20 dB/decade**; **Second-order** = **−40 dB/decade**. - **Universal cutoff formula** (1st order): $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$. - **Sallen-Key (2nd order) cutoff**: $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}$. - **Equal R, C Sallen-Key relation**: $K = 3 - \frac{1}{Q}$; for Butterworth $K = 3 - \sqrt{2} \approx 1.586$. - **MFB BPF center frequency**: $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi C}\sqrt{\frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1 R_2 R_3}}$. - **Twin-T Notch**: $f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$; uses $2C$ and $R/2$ in T-sections. - **Quality factor for BPF**: $Q = \frac{f_0}{BW} = \frac{f_0}{f_H - f_L}$. - **Common op-amp used in lab**: **IC741** with $\pm 15$ V dual supply. - **Gain in passband for active LPF**: $A_0 = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_i}$. - **Pole location rule**: All poles must lie in the **Left Half Plane (LHP)** for stability. - **Order of filter** = number of energy-storing elements (capacitors) in the signal path. - **Slew rate consideration**: Op-amp GBW should be $\geq 100 \times f_0$. - **3 dB point**: At $f_c$, output power = **50%**, output voltage = **70.7%** of passband value. - **Cascading**: Two 1st-order LPF stages don't make a 2nd-order LPF directly unless the op-amp stages are properly buffered (no loading). - **Notch applications**: 50 Hz hum removal, 60 Hz removal (US), 400 Hz avionics interference rejection. - **Twin-T active Q**: $Q = \frac{1}{4(1-K)}$; $K$ must be **less than 1** for stability. ---

topics.title: Active Notch Filter to Eliminate the 50Hz Power Line Frequency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAEL508 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS AND SIMULATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Active Notch Filter to Eliminate the 50Hz Power Line Frequency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Active Notch Filter (Band-Reject Filter / Band-Stop Filter):** A second-order active filter that attenuates a very narrow band of frequencies centred around a specific notch frequency ($f_N$) while passing all other frequencies with unity (or near-unity) gain. When implemented using operational amplifiers, resistors, and capacitors, it is termed an *active* notch filter and offers the distinct advantages of gain, high input impedance, low output impedance, and the absence of bulky inductors.

In the context of biomedical and instrumentation signal conditioning, an **Active Notch Filter tuned to 50 Hz** is a critical sub-circuit used to suppress the **50 Hz power line interference (mains hum)** that inevitably couples into low-level physiological signals such as ECG, EMG, and EEG through capacitive coupling, electromagnetic induction, and shared grounding paths.

### 1.2 Standard Performance Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Definitions to Memorise for the Board Exam:**
> - **Notch Frequency ($f_N$ or $\omega_0$):** The exact frequency at which maximum attenuation occurs. For power line rejection, this is fixed at **$f_N = 50 \text{ Hz}$**.
> - **Quality Factor ($Q$):** The selectivity or sharpness of the notch. Higher $Q$ means a narrower, sharper notch.
> - **Notch Bandwidth (BW):** The band of frequencies attenuated below the $-3 \text{ dB}$ threshold on either side of $f_N$.
> - **Stopband Attenuation:** The depth of the notch, ideally tending towards **infinity ($- \infty \text{ dB}$)** at $f_N$.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Acoustic Hole" Intuition

Imagine standing in a large room where a band is playing music (your desired signal: heart signals, sensor output) and a nearby electrical transformer is humming loudly at a constant pitch (the 50 Hz mains hum). The hum is not useful information, but it overwhelms the soft music. A notch filter is conceptually like a **surgical acoustic hole** — a precise "volume-zero button" that you press *only* for that exact humming pitch, while leaving all other musical notes (frequencies) completely unaffected. The "active" part (op-amp) acts like a powerful amplifier that *restores* the music's volume around the hole so the listener hears a clean, hum-free melody.

### 1.4 Why Specifically 50 Hz?

- In India (and most of Asia, Europe, Africa, and parts of South America), the AC power grid operates at a fundamental frequency of **$f_{line} = 50 \text{ Hz}$**.
- Any signal acquisition near power lines picks up this 50 Hz component and its harmonics (100 Hz, 150 Hz, 200 Hz …).
- The 50 Hz component is the **dominant noise source** in unshielded biomedical recordings.
- An active notch filter centred precisely at 50 Hz is therefore an **essential first-stage signal conditioning block**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Block-Level Architecture

A typical active notch filter experiment built in the Linear ICs Lab (PCAEL508) is composed of four sub-blocks:

1. **Twin-T Network (Passive Notch Section):** The frequency-selective core formed by two T-shaped RC networks (one with two series resistors and one shunt capacitor, the other with two series capacitors and one shunt resistor).
2. **Operational Amplifier (Active Element):** Standard op-amps used include the **µA741**, **LM741**, or **TL081**. It provides gain, buffering, and loading isolation.
3. **Feedback Network:** Determines the Quality Factor ($Q$) of the notch and converts the passive Twin-T into an active, sharp, high-$Q$ filter.
4. **Biasing & Power Supply:** Symmetrical dual supply ($\pm 12 \text{ V}$ or $\pm 15 \text{ V}$) to allow bipolar signal swing.

### 2.2 The Twin-T Notch Topology — Step-by-Step Logic

The Twin-T network is the heart of the experiment. The two T-sections are:

- **Low-Pass T-Section:** Two series resistors $R$ and a shunt capacitor $2C$ to ground.
- **High-Pass T-Section:** Two series capacitors $C$ and a shunt resistor $R/2$ to ground.

These two sections are *paralleled* between the input and the non-inverting input of the op-amp. At one critical frequency, the two sections produce equal-amplitude, **180° phase-shifted** outputs that cancel each other perfectly, creating a deep null.

### 2.3 Transfer Function Analysis

The transfer function of an active notch filter (using a non-inverting op-amp configuration with feedback fraction $\beta$) is given by the general form:

$$H(s) = \frac{V_{out}(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = \frac{K \left( s^2 + \omega_N^2 \right)}{s^2 + \frac{\omega_N}{Q} s + \omega_N^2}$$

where:
- $K$ = Passband gain of the active filter (set by feedback resistors)
- $\omega_N$ = Notch (centre) angular frequency in rad/sec
- $Q$ = Quality factor (sharpness of notch)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Numerator $s^2 + \omega_N^2$ is the secret of the notch.** Setting $s = j\omega_N$ makes the numerator zero, forcing $H(j\omega_N) = 0$ regardless of the denominator — a perfect, mathematically ideal null.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they are the most frequently asked derivations in the KTU ESE for Linear ICs Lab.**

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Notch Frequency $f_N$ | $f_N = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi R C}$ | Centre frequency of rejection | Hertz (Hz) |
| Angular Notch Frequency $\omega_N$ | $\omega_N = 2 \pi f_N = \dfrac{1}{RC}$ | Same in radian form | rad/s |
| Quality Factor (Twin-T) | $Q = \dfrac{1}{2(1-K)}$ | Sharpness; $K$ = passband gain | Dimensionless |
| Quality Factor (Alt. Form) | $Q = \dfrac{f_N}{BW}$ | $BW$ = $-3$ dB bandwidth | Dimensionless |
| Passband Gain $K$ | $K = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_{in}}$ | Non-inverting op-amp gain | Dimensionless |
| Stopband Attenuation | $\vert H(j\omega_N) \vert \to 0$ | Ideal notch depth | dB (or linear) |
| Notch Bandwidth $BW$ | $BW = \dfrac{f_N}{Q}$ | Width of $-3$ dB band | Hz |
| Selectivity $\zeta$ | $\zeta = \dfrac{1}{2Q}$ | Damping ratio | Dimensionless |
| Mains Period $T_{50}$ | $T_{50} = \dfrac{1}{50} = 20 \text{ ms}$ | Period of 50 Hz interference | seconds |
| Standard Twin-T Component Ratio | $R_1 = R_2 = R$, $C_1 = C_2 = C$, $R_3 = R/2$, $C_3 = 2C$ | Component matching rule | — |

### 2.5 Design Procedure — Stepwise for 50 Hz Rejection

**Step 1:** Choose a convenient capacitor value, typically $C = 0.1 \mu F$ or $C = 0.01 \mu F$ (readily available in the lab).

**Step 2:** Rearrange the notch frequency equation to solve for $R$:

$$R = \frac{1}{2 \pi f_N C}$$

**Step 3:** For $f_N = 50 \text{ Hz}$ and $C = 0.1 \mu F$:

$$R = \frac{1}{2 \pi (50) (0.1 \times 10^{-6})} \approx 31{,}831 \text{ }\Omega$$

Use the nearest preferred value, $R = 33 \text{ k}\Omega$ (E12 series).

**Step 4:** Determine the matching component pairs:
- $R_1 = R_2 = 33 \text{ k}\Omega$
- $C_1 = C_2 = 0.1 \mu F$
- $R_3 = R_2/2 = 16.5 \text{ k}\Omega$ (use $15 \text{ k}\Omega$ in series with $1.5 \text{ k}\Omega$)
- $C_3 = 2 C_1 = 0.22 \mu F$

**Step 5:** Choose feedback resistors for desired $Q$ (e.g., $Q = 5$):
$$K = 1 - \frac{1}{2Q} = 1 - \frac{1}{10} = 0.9$$
$$R_f = R_{in} \cdot (K - 1) = R_{in} \cdot (-0.1)$$

Since $K < 1$ (for stable notch), the gain must be slightly less than unity, achieved by making $R_f$ a small fraction of $R_{in}$.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Biomedical Instrumentation:** Front-end of ECG, EEG, and EMG machines to remove mains hum.
- **Audio Engineering:** Eliminates the 50 Hz hum buzz in stage microphones and recording systems.
- **Industrial Process Control:** Suppresses power line pickup in transducer signals (strain gauges, thermocouples).
- **Communication Receivers:** Rejects single-tone interference in narrowband receivers.
- **Scientific Instrumentation:** Used in lock-in amplifiers and spectrum analysers to null out the line frequency reference.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Notch Frequency of the Twin-T Network

**Step 1: Define the Twin-T admittance.**

Consider the two T-sections connected in parallel between the input voltage $V_{in}$ and the output node $V_{out}$ (which is tied to the non-inverting input of the op-amp, drawing no current). The total admittance is the sum of the admittances of the two T-sections.

The **low-pass T** has impedance:
$$Z_{LP}(s) = R + \frac{1}{sC} \cdot \frac{R}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{R + \frac{1}{2sC}}$$

The **high-pass T** has impedance:
$$Z_{HP}(s) = \frac{1}{sC} + \frac{R}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2R + \frac{1}{sC}}$$

A more elegant approach uses the standard result. The transfer ratio of the Twin-T network is:

$$\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{s^2 R^2 C^2 + 1}{s^2 R^2 C^2 + 4 s R C + 1}$$

**Step 2: Identify the zero of the numerator (the notch).**

Set the numerator to zero:

$$s^2 R^2 C^2 + 1 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad s^2 = -\frac{1}{R^2 C^2}$$

**Step 3: Convert to frequency domain using $s = j\omega$.**

$$-\omega^2 R^2 C^2 + 1 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega^2 = \frac{1}{R^2 C^2}$$

**Step 4: Solve for $\omega$ and convert to Hz.**

$$\omega_N = \frac{1}{RC} \quad \text{and} \quad f_N = \frac{\omega_N}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the single most important equation in the entire experiment.** Memorise it: $f_N = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ for the Twin-T topology.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Quality Factor and Stability Condition

**Step 1: Express the active filter's general transfer function.**

For a non-inverting op-amp with feedback factor $\beta$, the closed-loop transfer function modifies the Twin-T response as:

$$H(s) = \frac{K(s^2 + \omega_N^2)}{s^2 + \frac{\omega_N}{Q} s + \omega_N^2}$$

where $K = \dfrac{1}{\beta}$ is the DC passband gain.

**Step 2: Relate $K$ to the passive network elements.**

The Twin-T network's natural damping is set by the matching condition. Introducing active feedback modifies the effective damping. Comparing coefficients with the standard second-order form, we obtain:

$$Q = \frac{1}{2(1 - K)}$$

**Step 3: Apply the stability criterion.**

For $Q > 0$ (a real, finite, stable filter), we require:
$$1 - K > 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad K < 1$$

For $K = 1$, $Q \to \infty$ (theoretically infinite selectivity, but practically leads to oscillation).

**Step 4: Solve for the design rule.**

Setting $K = 1 - \epsilon$ for small $\epsilon$ gives $Q \approx \dfrac{1}{2\epsilon}$. This means the closer $K$ is to 1, the sharper the notch — but also the more sensitive and unstable the circuit becomes to component tolerances.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Design Worked Example for 50 Hz

**Given:** $f_N = 50 \text{ Hz}$, choose $C = 0.1 \mu F = 1 \times 10^{-7} F$, target $Q = 5$.

**Step 1: Compute $R$.**

$$R = \frac{1}{2 \pi f_N C} = \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 50 \times 1 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$R = \frac{1}{3.14159 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 3183.1 \text{ }\Omega$$

**Step 2: Round to a standard preferred value.**

Use $R = 3.3 \text{ k}\Omega$ (E12 series preferred value).

**Step 3: Verify the actual notch frequency.**

$$f_N = \frac{1}{2 \pi (3300)(1 \times 10^{-7})} \approx 48.23 \text{ Hz}$$

This is within the design tolerance and the notch is still effective for 50 Hz rejection when combined with a buffer.

**Step 4: Compute matched components for the Twin-T.**

$$R_1 = R_2 = 3.3 \text{ k}\Omega, \quad C_1 = C_2 = 0.1 \mu F$$
$$R_3 = \frac{R_1}{2} = 1.65 \text{ k}\Omega, \quad C_3 = 2 C_1 = 0.22 \mu F$$

**Step 5: Determine feedback resistor for $Q = 5$.**

$$Q = \frac{1}{2(1-K)} = 5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1 - K = \frac{1}{10} \quad \Rightarrow \quad K = 0.9$$

For a non-inverting amplifier: $K = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_{in}} = 0.9$, so $\dfrac{R_f}{R_{in}} = -0.1$.

A negative resistance is not physical, so we implement $K < 1$ using a **voltage divider in the feedback path** rather than a true non-inverting amp. This is a common board-exam trick question!

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Frequency Response Behaviour

| **Frequency Region** | **Twin-T Behaviour** | **Output** |
|---|---|---|
| $f \ll f_N$ (e.g., DC, 5 Hz) | Low-pass T dominates; high-pass T blocks | $V_{out} \approx V_{in}$ (passband) |
| $f = f_N = 50 \text{ Hz}$ | T-sections cancel exactly (180° out of phase) | $V_{out} \approx 0$ (deep null) |
| $f \gg f_N$ (e.g., 500 Hz, 5 kHz) | High-pass T dominates; low-pass T blocks | $V_{out} \approx V_{in}$ (passband) |
| $f = f_N \pm \Delta f$ | Partial cancellation, transitions to passband | Attenuated by $-3$ dB at $\Delta f = BW/2$ |

### 3.5 Sensitivity and Tolerance Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Tip — Tolerance Mismatch:**
> If the matching condition $R_1 = R_2$ and $C_1 = C_2$ is violated by even 5%, the notch depth degrades from $- \infty$ dB (ideal) to roughly $-30$ dB. Use **1% metal-film resistors** and **polystyrene or NP0 ceramic capacitors** for precise matching. This is a frequent question: *"Why are precision components required in notch filters?"*

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA[Input Signal Source] --> mixerA[Input Summing Node]
    lfsigA[Low Frequency ECG Signal] --> mixerA
    mainsA[50 Hz Mains Hum Noise] --> mixerA
    mixerA --> twinA[Twin T Notch Network]
    twinA --> opaA[Operational Amplifier Buffer]
    opaA --> fbA[Feedback Network Q Control]
    fbA --> opaA
    opaA --> outA[Clean Output to CRO or DSO]
    outA --> scopeA[CRO or DSO Display]
    outA --> meterA[True RMS Multimeter]
    psA[Dual Power Supply Plus Minus 12 V] --> opaA
```

### 4.2 Detailed Functional Sub-Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeV1[Vin AC Signal Generator] --> nodeV2[Twin T Low Pass Branch]
    nodeV1 --> nodeV3[Twin T High Pass Branch]
    nodeV2 --> nodeV4[Junction Node Vout Passive]
    nodeV3 --> nodeV4
    nodeV4 --> nodeV5[Non Inverting Op Amp Plus Input]
    nodeV6[Inverting Op Amp Minus Input] --> nodeV5
    nodeV7[Feedback Resistor Rf] --> nodeV5
    nodeV8[Ground Resistor Rg] --> nodeV6
    nodeV5 --> nodeV9[Op Amp Output Buffer]
    nodeV9 --> nodeV10[Final Filtered Output]
    nodeV10 --> nodeV11[Spectrum Analyser Display]
    nodeV10 --> nodeV12[Dual Trace Oscilloscope]
```

### 4.3 Signal Flow and Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start Experiment] --> stepB[Set Signal Generator to Sweep 10 Hz to 1 kHz]
    stepB --> stepC[Set Signal Amplitude to 1 V peak to peak]
    stepC --> stepD[Apply Signal to Notch Filter Input]
    stepD --> stepE[Measure Output Amplitude at Each Frequency]
    stepE --> stepF{Is Frequency Equal to 50 Hz}
    stepF --> stepG[Yes Record Minimum Output]
    stepF --> stepH[No Record Passband Output]
    stepG --> stepI[Compute Attenuation in dB]
    stepH --> stepI
    stepI --> stepJ[Plot Frequency Response Curve]
    stepJ --> stepK[Measure Minus 3 dB Bandwidth]
    stepK --> stepL[Calculate Q Factor Q equals fN divided by BW]
    stepL --> stopM[End Experiment and Tabulate Results]
```

### 4.4 Component Layout Schematic (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    r1a[Resistor R1 equals 3.3 kohm] --> nodeJ1[Junction Node X]
    c3a[Capacitor C3 equals 0.22 microfarad] --> nodeJ1
    nodeJ1 --> r2a[Resistor R2 equals 3.3 kohm]
    nodeJ1 --> c1a[Capacitor C1 equals 0.1 microfarad]
    c1a --> nodeJ2[Junction Node Y]
    c2a[Capacitor C2 equals 0.1 microfarad] --> nodeJ2
    r3a[Resistor R3 equals 1.65 kohm] --> nodeJ2
    nodeJ2 --> opa1[Op Amp Non Inverting Plus]
    rfa[Feedback Resistor Rf] --> opa1
    rga[Ground Resistor Rg] --> opa1
    opa1 --> outp1[Filtered Output Node]
    vpos[Plus 12 V Supply] --> opa1
    vneg[Minus 12 V Supply] --> opa1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Circuit Diagram Note for the Lab Record:**
> When drawing the circuit in your record, always include: (i) the op-amp power supply pins connected to $\pm 12$ V with bypass capacitors (0.1 µF) to ground, (ii) the offset-null pins either left open or connected through a 10 kΩ potentiometer, and (iii) the input signal source with explicit AC coupling if a transformer-coupled source is used.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** Define an *active notch filter*. State two advantages of using an active notch filter over its passive counterpart for the rejection of 50 Hz power line interference.

**Model Answer:**
An active notch filter is a second-order active band-reject filter that uses an op-amp along with RC networks to sharply attenuate a narrow band of frequencies centred at a specific notch frequency while passing all other frequencies with minimal loss. The 50 Hz power line hum is a single, narrowband interferer, making a notch filter the optimal choice.

*Advantages over passive notch filter:*
1. **Gain buffering:** The op-amp provides isolation between source and load, preventing the loading effect that severely degrades the $Q$ of a passive Twin-T network.
2. **Sharper null (higher $Q$):** Active feedback can boost the quality factor from a typical passive value of $Q \approx 0.25$ to $Q > 10$, producing a much deeper and narrower notch.
3. **No inductors required:** The op-amp synthesises the inductive behaviour, allowing the entire circuit to be fabricated as a compact, low-cost, integrated PCB module.

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** Why is the Twin-T configuration preferred over a simple RC notch network for biomedical instrumentation applications? Mention any one disadvantage of the Twin-T topology.

**Model Answer:**
The Twin-T configuration is preferred because it produces a **theoretically infinite attenuation** (an ideal null) at the notch frequency, which is essential for completely eliminating the strong 50 Hz mains hum that otherwise overwhelms low-amplitude biomedical signals (ECG, EEG, EMG). It uses only resistors and capacitors, making it inexpensive and easy to integrate.

*Disadvantage:* The Twin-T network is **highly sensitive to component tolerance mismatch**. Even a 5% deviation in the $R$ or $C$ matching pairs degrades the notch depth from $-\infty$ dB to roughly $-30$ dB, requiring precision 1% metal-film resistors and NP0/C0G capacitors.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style Question]**
>
> **Q3 (A). (a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of an **active Twin-T notch filter** designed to eliminate 50 Hz power line interference. Derive the expression for the notch frequency. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Design an active notch filter for a notch frequency of 50 Hz. Choose $C = 0.1 \mu F$, determine the resistor values, and state the matched component pairs. Compute the quality factor for a passband gain of $K = 0.9$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q3(A)(a):

**Circuit Description:**
The active Twin-T notch filter consists of two parallel T-networks feeding the non-inverting input of a $\mu A741$ op-amp.

- **Low-pass T-section:** $R_1$ in series with $R_2$, and a shunt capacitor $C_3 = 2C$ from the junction to ground.
- **High-pass T-section:** $C_1$ in series with $C_2$, and a shunt resistor $R_3 = R/2$ from the junction to ground.
- The two T-sections share common input and output nodes.
- A feedback network of $R_f$ and $R_g$ sets the passband gain $K$ at the inverting input.

**Derivation of Notch Frequency:**

The transfer function of the passive Twin-T network (ignoring loading from the op-amp, which has infinite input impedance) is:

$$T(s) = \frac{s^2 R^2 C^2 + 1}{s^2 R^2 C^2 + 4 s R C + 1}$$

The numerator becomes zero when:

$$s^2 R^2 C^2 + 1 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad s = \pm j \frac{1}{RC}$$

Substituting $s = j \omega$ at the notch:

$$j \omega_N = j \frac{1}{RC} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_N = \frac{1}{RC}$$

Converting to Hz:

$$f_N = \frac{1}{2 \pi R C} = 50 \text{ Hz}$$

#### Model Solution for Q3(A)(b):

**Step 1: Solve for $R$.**

$$R = \frac{1}{2 \pi f_N C} = \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 50 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1}{\pi \times 10^{-4}} \approx 31{,}831 \text{ }\Omega$$

Standard preferred value: $R = 33 \text{ k}\Omega$ (E12 series).

**Step 2: Matched component pairs.**

- $R_1 = R_2 = 33 \text{ k}\Omega$
- $C_1 = C_2 = 0.1 \mu F$
- $R_3 = R/2 = 16.5 \text{ k}\Omega$ (use $15 \text{ k}\Omega + 1.5 \text{ k}\Omega$ in series)
- $C_3 = 2C = 0.22 \mu F$

**Step 3: Compute the quality factor.**

For $K = 0.9$:

$$Q = \frac{1}{2(1 - K)} = \frac{1}{2(1 - 0.9)} = \frac{1}{0.2} = 5$$

**Step 4: Compute the $-3$ dB bandwidth.**

$$BW = \frac{f_N}{Q} = \frac{50}{5} = 10 \text{ Hz}$$

So the notch attenuates frequencies from 45 Hz to 55 Hz by at least 3 dB — an extremely sharp rejection of the 50 Hz line.

---

> **OR**
>
> **Q3 (B). (a)** Derive the transfer function of an active notch filter and identify the condition for the notch (zero) frequency. Explain how the quality factor $Q$ is controlled by the feedback network. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** With experimental data: $V_{in} = 1 \text{ V}_{pp}$, at 50 Hz the measured $V_{out} = 5 \text{ mV}_{pp}$. At 45 Hz, $V_{out} = 0.707 \text{ V}_{pp}$. Calculate the **notch depth in dB** and the **quality factor $Q$**. Comment on the suitability of the filter for ECG signal conditioning. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q3(B)(a):

**Step 1: General second-order transfer function.**

$$H(s) = \frac{K(s^2 + \omega_N^2)}{s^2 + \frac{\omega_N}{Q} s + \omega_N^2}$$

**Step 2: Condition for the notch.**

Setting the numerator to zero:
$$s^2 + \omega_N^2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad s = \pm j \omega_N$$

This means at $s = j\omega_N$, the output $V_{out} = 0$ regardless of the denominator, producing a perfect null. Hence $\omega_N = \dfrac{1}{RC}$ for the Twin-T.

**Step 3: $Q$ control via feedback.**

For a non-inverting op-amp, $K = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_g}$. Substituting the coefficient comparison:

$$Q = \frac{1}{2(1 - K)}$$

Setting $R_f$ and $R_g$ such that $K$ is slightly less than 1 (e.g., $K = 0.9$ to $0.99$) gives a $Q$ of 5 to 50, producing a very sharp, narrow notch.

#### Model Solution for Q3(B)(b):

**Step 1: Notch depth in dB.**

$$\text{Notch Depth} = 20 \log_{10} \left( \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} \right) = 20 \log_{10} \left( \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{1} \right)$$

$$\text{Notch Depth} = 20 \log_{10} (0.005) = 20 \times (-2.301) = -46.02 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 2: Quality factor $Q$.**

Since 45 Hz is the $-3$ dB frequency and the notch is at 50 Hz:

$$BW = 2 \times (50 - 45) = 10 \text{ Hz}$$

$$Q = \frac{f_N}{BW} = \frac{50}{10} = 5$$

**Step 3: Suitability comment.**

A 46 dB notch depth and $Q = 5$ are excellent values for ECG signal conditioning. The ECG bandwidth (0.05 Hz to 150 Hz) has its critical diagnostic content well above and below 50 Hz, so the sharp notch at 50 Hz removes the mains hum without distorting the P-QRS-T waveform. The filter is therefore **highly suitable** for clinical ECG front-ends.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 4:** Students often write the Twin-T denominator as $s^2 R^2 C^2 + 2 s R C + 1$. The correct coefficient is **4** (not 2), arising from the parallel combination of the two T-sections. A wrong coefficient gives a wrong $Q$ and a wrong bandwidth.
> 2. **Sign error in $Q$ formula:** $Q = \dfrac{1}{2(1-K)}$ requires $K < 1$. Writing $Q = \dfrac{1}{2(K-1)}$ produces a negative $Q$ — a guaranteed loss of 2 marks.
> 3. **Unit confusion in $C$:** Always convert microfarads to farads ($0.1 \mu F = 1 \times 10^{-7} F$) before substituting into $R = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f_N C}$. A common mistake is to plug in $0.1$ directly, leading to absurd resistor values.
> 4. **Not drawing bypass capacitors:** For 1 mark, the lab examiner expects $\pm 12$ V supply pins decoupled with 0.1 µF capacitors to ground. Omitting them costs an easy mark.
> 5. **Confusing notch and band-pass:** Some students write the transfer function upside down, putting the zero in the denominator. Always remember: **notch = zero in numerator**, band-pass = zero in numerator but different topology.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Must Memorise for KTU ESE:**

- **Core Formula:** $f_N = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi R C}$ — the heart of the Twin-T notch filter.
- **Component Matching Rule:** $R_1 = R_2 = R$, $C_1 = C_2 = C$, $R_3 = R/2$, $C_3 = 2C$. Any mismatch degrades notch depth.
- **Quality Factor:** $Q = \dfrac{1}{2(1-K)}$, valid only when $K < 1$.
- **Passband Gain:** $K = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_g}$ for non-inverting topology; must be slightly less than 1 to keep $Q$ finite and the circuit stable.
- **Notch Bandwidth:** $BW = \dfrac{f_N}{Q}$. The narrower the bandwidth, the sharper the rejection of the 50 Hz hum.
- **50 Hz Standard:** In India and most of the world, the power line frequency is 50 Hz. In the USA, Japan (partly), and a few other countries, it is 60 Hz. The design adjusts via the $R$ or $C$ value.
- **Op-Amp Choice:** $\mu A741$ or TL081 with $\pm 12$ V supply is standard. Bypass supply pins with 0.1 µF ceramic capacitors.
- **Transfer Function Signatures:**
  - Notch filter: zero in the **numerator** at $s = \pm j\omega_N$.
  - Band-pass filter: zero in the **numerator** at $s = 0$ and $s = \infty$.
  - Low-pass: zero at $s = \infty$.
  - High-pass: zero at $s = 0$.
- **Measurement Procedure:** Sweep the input frequency from 10 Hz to 1 kHz using a function generator, record $V_{out}$ at each step using a multimeter or DSO, plot $V_{out}$ vs. frequency on semi-log paper, and identify the notch.
- **Real-World Relevance:** ECG machines, EEG recorders, EMG units, audio mixers, spectrum analysers — all rely on 50 Hz notch filters as the first line of defence against mains hum.
- **Sensitivity Rule of Thumb:** A 1% mismatch in $R$ or $C$ causes roughly a 6 dB degradation in notch depth. Always use 1% metal-film resistors and NP0/C0G (or polystyrene) capacitors.
- **Stability Boundary:** As $K \to 1$, $Q \to \infty$, and the circuit tends to oscillate. Practical designs keep $K$ in the range $0.9$ to $0.99$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Astable and Monostable Multivibrator Using Timer IC NE555
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Astable and Monostable Multivibrator Using Timer IC NE555

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAEL508 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS AND SIMULATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Astable and Monostable Multivibrator Using Timer IC NE555

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The NE555 Timer IC — Formal Definition

The **NE555** is a highly stable, precision monolithic timing circuit capable of producing accurate time delays or oscillations. As formally defined in the KTU 2024 syllabus, it is an **8-pin DIP (Dual In-line Package)** analog integrated circuit that functions as a precision timer, oscillator, or pulse generator. Its internal architecture is built around three precision components:

- A **resistor divider network** consisting of **three identical 5 kΩ resistors** (this is the origin of the name "**555**").
- **Two precision voltage comparators** (an upper threshold comparator and a lower trigger comparator).
- An **SR Flip-Flop** with reset capability, driving a high-current **totem-pole output stage** and an open-collector **discharge transistor**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operating Range:** The NE555 operates from a single supply of **+4.5 V to +16 V**, with a maximum output current of **±200 mA**, and typical threshold levels at precisely **1/3 VCC** (trigger) and **2/3 VCC** (threshold).

### 1.2 Multivibrator — Formal Definition

A **multivibrator** is an electronic switching circuit used to implement simple two-state memory devices, oscillators, or timers by employing positive feedback. The three classical types are:

- **Astable (free-running):** No stable state; continuously switches between two states, generating a square wave.
- **Monostable (one-shot):** One stable state; on receiving a trigger, it switches to a quasi-stable state for a fixed duration and returns.
- **Bistable (flip-flop):** Two stable states; requires explicit trigger pulses to change state.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **light switch in a corridor with two automatic sensors**:

- **Astable Mode:** Think of a **swing at a playground**. Once pushed, it keeps swinging back and forth forever without any external force. Similarly, an astable 555 circuit keeps flipping between HIGH and LOW on its own, producing a continuous **rectangular clock pulse** — like the ticking of a digital clock.

- **Monostable Mode:** Think of a **staircase light timer**. The light is normally OFF (stable state). When you press the button (trigger), the light turns ON for exactly 30 seconds (quasi-stable state), and then automatically turns OFF. You cannot extend the ON time by pressing the button again during those 30 seconds.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The NE555 is called the "**workhorse of analog electronics**" because the same chip, by simply changing two external resistors and a capacitor, can produce either an **astable oscillator** or a **monostable timer** — making it a cornerstone device in any linear integrated circuits laboratory.

### 1.4 Pin Configuration of NE555

| Pin No. | Pin Name | Function |
|:-------:|:---------|:---------|
| 1 | **GND** | Ground reference (**0 V**) |
| 2 | **TRIGGER** | Active-LOW input; triggers the flip-flop when voltage drops below **1/3 VCC** |
| 3 | **OUTPUT** | Drives the load; sources/sinks up to **200 mA** |
| 4 | **RESET** | Active-LOW input; resets the flip-flop and forces OUTPUT LOW |
| 5 | **CONTROL VOLTAGE** | Allows direct external modulation of threshold/trigger levels |
| 6 | **THRESHOLD** | Active-HIGH input; resets the flip-flop when voltage exceeds **2/3 VCC** |
| 7 | **DISCHARGE** | Open-collector transistor used to discharge the timing capacitor |
| 8 | **VCC** | Positive supply voltage (**+4.5 V to +16 V**) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Internal Block Diagram of NE555

The internal architecture of the NE555 can be functionally decomposed into five major blocks:

- **Resistor Divider Network:** Three equal 5 kΩ resistors form a voltage divider, establishing reference voltages of **1/3 VCC** at the inverting input of the lower comparator (TRIGGER comparator), and **2/3 VCC** at the non-inverting input of the upper comparator (THRESHOLD comparator).

- **Threshold Comparator (Comparator 1):** Compares the external voltage at Pin 6 (THRESHOLD) with **2/3 VCC**. Output is HIGH when **V₆ > 2/3 VCC**.

- **Trigger Comparator (Comparator 2):** Compares the external voltage at Pin 2 (TRIGGER) with **1/3 VCC**. Output is LOW when **V₂ < 1/3 VCC**.

- **SR Flip-Flop:** The Q̄ output drives the OUTPUT pin and the DISCHARGE transistor. The S input comes from the trigger comparator (active LOW), and the R input comes from the threshold comparator (active HIGH). The RESET pin asynchronously clears the flip-flop.

- **Discharge Transistor (NPN):** Connected between Pin 7 and GND. When ON (saturated), it provides a low-impedance discharge path for the external timing capacitor.

### 2.2 Astable Multivibrator — Operational Logic

The astable multivibrator has **no stable state**. It continuously transitions between two quasi-stable states, generating a rectangular waveform at the output.

**Step-by-step operating sequence:**

- **Initial State (Power ON):** Capacitor C charges through **RA** and **RB** toward VCC. Initially, Vc = 0, so Pin 2 < 1/3 VCC → Trigger comparator output is LOW → SR flip-flop SET → OUTPUT = HIGH, Discharge transistor OFF.

- **Charging Phase (HIGH Duration, T_HIGH):** Capacitor charges through **RA + RB**. Output remains HIGH until Vc exceeds **2/3 VCC**. Duration: **T_HIGH = 0.693 × (R_A + R_B) × C**.

- **Threshold Triggered:** When Vc > 2/3 VCC, the Threshold comparator output goes HIGH → SR flip-flop RESET → OUTPUT = LOW, Discharge transistor ON.

- **Discharging Phase (LOW Duration, T_LOW):** Capacitor discharges through **RB** and the DISCHARGE pin to GND. Output remains LOW until Vc drops below **1/3 VCC**. Duration: **T_LOW = 0.693 × R_B × C**.

- **Trigger Triggered:** When Vc < 1/3 VCC, the Trigger comparator output goes LOW → SR flip-flop SET → OUTPUT = HIGH again. The cycle repeats indefinitely.

### 2.3 Monostable Multivibrator — Operational Logic

The monostable multivibrator has **one stable state and one quasi-stable state**. The stable state is the default state when no trigger is present.

**Step-by-step operating sequence:**

- **Stable State (No Trigger):** In the stable state, OUTPUT = LOW, Discharge transistor is ON, and the capacitor is held discharged at near 0 V (Vc ≈ 0 V). The circuit remains here indefinitely until a trigger pulse arrives.

- **Trigger Pulse Applied:** A **negative-going trigger pulse** (< 1/3 VCC) is applied to Pin 2. This causes the Trigger comparator to output LOW → SR flip-flop SET → OUTPUT = HIGH, Discharge transistor OFF.

- **Quasi-Stable State (HIGH Duration, T):** Capacitor C now charges through **R** toward VCC. Output remains HIGH until Vc reaches **2/3 VCC**. Duration: **T = 1.1 × R × C**.

- **Return to Stable State:** When Vc = 2/3 VCC, the Threshold comparator sets the flip-flop RESET → OUTPUT returns LOW, Discharge transistor turns ON, capacitor rapidly discharges to 0 V.

> [!NOTE]
> **Inherent Retriggering Immunity:** During the quasi-stable state, any additional trigger pulses are ignored because Pin 2 is already above 1/3 VCC. This makes the standard monostable 555 circuit **non-retriggerable** during its timing cycle.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Astable Multivibrator | Monostable Multivibrator |
|:----------|:----------------------|:-------------------------|
| Time HIGH ($T_H$) | $T_H = 0.693 \cdot (R_A + R_B) \cdot C$ | $T = 1.1 \cdot R \cdot C$ |
| Time LOW ($T_L$) | $T_L = 0.693 \cdot R_B \cdot C$ | Not applicable (single pulse) |
| Total Period ($T$) | $T = T_H + T_L = 0.693 \cdot (R_A + 2R_B) \cdot C$ | $T = 1.1 \cdot R \cdot C$ |
| Frequency ($f$) | $f = \dfrac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B) \cdot C}$ | $f = \dfrac{1}{1.1 \cdot R \cdot C}$ |
| Duty Cycle ($D$) | $D = \dfrac{(R_A + R_B)}{R_A + 2R_B} \times 100\%$ | Fixed single pulse |
| Minimum Duty Cycle | $\approx 50\%$ (when $R_A \ll R_B$) | 100% for one cycle |
| Charging Path | Through $R_A$ and $R_B$ | Through $R$ only |
| Discharging Path | Through $R_B$ and Pin 7 | Direct to GND via Pin 7 |
| Trigger Source | Self-triggered (internal) | External negative pulse at Pin 2 |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Astable Applications:** Square-wave clock generators, LED flashers, tone generators, PWM motor control, pulse-position modulation, automotive turn-signal flashers, simple audio oscillators, and clock pulse sources for digital logic.
- **Monostable Applications:** Missing-pulse detectors, debounce circuits for mechanical switches, pulse stretching, time-delay relays, frequency dividers, analog-to-digital converters (as sample-and-hold timing), and one-shot trigger circuits in instrumentation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Trade-off:** For astable circuits requiring **duty cycle close to 50%**, add a diode in parallel with $R_B$ to allow the charging and discharging paths to use independent resistances. This breaks the inherent **50% minimum duty cycle** limitation of the standard topology.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Astable Timing Equations

**Step 1: Define the charging phase using Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)**

When the flip-flop is SET, OUTPUT = HIGH, Discharge transistor OFF. The capacitor charges through $R_A$ and $R_B$ (in series). Applying KVL around the charging loop:

$$V_{CC} = i(t) \cdot (R_A + R_B) + V_C(t)$$

**Step 2: Write the capacitor charging equation**

Since $i(t) = C \cdot \dfrac{dV_C(t)}{dt}$, we substitute and rearrange:

$$V_{CC} - V_C(t) = C \cdot (R_A + R_B) \cdot \dfrac{dV_C(t)}{dt}$$

Rearranging into standard first-order linear ODE form:

$$\dfrac{dV_C(t)}{dt} + \dfrac{V_C(t)}{C \cdot (R_A + R_B)} = \dfrac{V_{CC}}{C \cdot (R_A + R_B)}$$

**Step 3: Solve the differential equation**

The general solution for charging from initial voltage $V_0$ toward $V_{CC}$ with time constant $\tau = C \cdot (R_A + R_B)$:

$$V_C(t) = V_{CC} - (V_{CC} - V_0) \cdot e^{-t / \tau}$$

**Step 4: Apply the boundary conditions**

- Initial condition: At the moment of trigger, the capacitor was just discharged to **1/3 VCC** (the lower threshold). Therefore $V_0 = \dfrac{V_{CC}}{3}$.
- Final condition: At the moment of threshold trigger, $V_C(t) = \dfrac{2 \cdot V_{CC}}{3}$.

Substituting $V_0 = V_{CC}/3$ and $V_C(T_H) = 2V_{CC}/3$:

$$\dfrac{2 V_{CC}}{3} = V_{CC} - \left(V_{CC} - \dfrac{V_{CC}}{3}\right) \cdot e^{-T_H / \tau}$$

$$\dfrac{2 V_{CC}}{3} = V_{CC} - \dfrac{2 V_{CC}}{3} \cdot e^{-T_H / \tau}$$

**Step 5: Isolate the exponential term**

$$\dfrac{2 V_{CC}}{3} - V_{CC} = -\dfrac{2 V_{CC}}{3} \cdot e^{-T_H / \tau}$$

$$-\dfrac{V_{CC}}{3} = -\dfrac{2 V_{CC}}{3} \cdot e^{-T_H / \tau}$$

$$e^{-T_H / \tau} = \dfrac{1}{2}$$

**Step 6: Solve for $T_H$**

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$-\dfrac{T_H}{\tau} = \ln\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right) = -\ln(2)$$

$$T_H = \tau \cdot \ln(2) = C \cdot (R_A + R_B) \cdot \ln(2)$$

Since $\ln(2) \approx 0.693$:

$$\boxed{T_H = 0.693 \cdot (R_A + R_B) \cdot C}$$

**Step 7: Derive $T_L$ by analogous reasoning**

During the LOW state, the capacitor discharges through $R_B$ only (the discharge transistor connects Pin 7 to GND, bypassing $R_A$). The capacitor decays from $2V_{CC}/3$ down to $V_{CC}/3$ with time constant $\tau' = R_B \cdot C$. By identical mathematical steps:

$$\boxed{T_L = 0.693 \cdot R_B \cdot C}$$

**Step 8: Derive the total period and frequency**

$$T = T_H + T_L = 0.693 \cdot (R_A + 2R_B) \cdot C$$

$$f = \dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B) \cdot C}$$

**Step 9: Derive the duty cycle**

$$D = \dfrac{T_H}{T} = \dfrac{(R_A + R_B)}{(R_A + 2R_B)} \times 100\%$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Monostable Timing Equation

**Step 1: Initial conditions**

In the stable state, the discharge transistor is ON, so $V_C(0) \approx 0$ V (fully discharged).

**Step 2: Quasi-stable state capacitor charging**

After the trigger pulse, the discharge transistor turns OFF. The capacitor charges from 0 V toward $V_{CC}$ through resistor R, with time constant $\tau = R \cdot C$:

$$V_C(t) = V_{CC} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t / (RC)}\right)$$

**Step 3: Apply the threshold condition**

The quasi-stable state ends when $V_C(T) = 2V_{CC}/3$:

$$\dfrac{2 V_{CC}}{3} = V_{CC} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-T / (RC)}\right)$$

$$e^{-T / (RC)} = \dfrac{1}{3}$$

$$-\dfrac{T}{RC} = \ln\left(\dfrac{1}{3}\right) = -\ln(3)$$

$$T = RC \cdot \ln(3)$$

**Step 4: Numerical evaluation**

Since $\ln(3) \approx 1.0986 \approx 1.1$:

$$\boxed{T = 1.1 \cdot R \cdot C}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Design Matrix — Numerical Verification

Let us design and verify a **1 kHz astable multivibrator with 60% duty cycle** using $C = 1\ \mu F$.

**Given:** $f = 1\ \text{kHz}$, $D = 60\%$, $C = 1\ \mu F$

**Step 1: Calculate the total period**

$$T = \dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{1000} = 1\ \text{ms}$$

**Step 2: Express duty cycle equation**

$$D = \dfrac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B} = 0.60$$

$$0.60 \cdot R_A + 1.20 \cdot R_B = R_A + R_B$$

$$0.40 \cdot R_A = 0.20 \cdot R_B$$

$$\dfrac{R_A}{R_B} = \dfrac{1}{2}$$

Let $R_A = x$, then $R_B = 2x$.

**Step 3: Substitute into the period equation**

$$T = 0.693 \cdot (R_A + 2R_B) \cdot C$$

$$10^{-3} = 0.693 \cdot (x + 2 \cdot 2x) \cdot 10^{-6}$$

$$10^{-3} = 0.693 \cdot 5x \cdot 10^{-6}$$

$$x = \dfrac{10^{-3}}{0.693 \cdot 5 \cdot 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{10^{3}}{3.465} \approx 288.6\ \Omega$$

**Step 4: Determine the standard E12 resistor values**

- $R_A \approx 270\ \Omega$ (E12 standard)
- $R_B \approx 560\ \Omega$ (E12 standard, maintains ratio close to 1:2)

**Step 5: Verification**

$$T_H = 0.693 \cdot (270 + 560) \cdot 10^{-6} = 0.693 \cdot 830 \cdot 10^{-6} \approx 0.575\ \text{ms}$$

$$T_L = 0.693 \cdot 560 \cdot 10^{-6} \approx 0.388\ \text{ms}$$

$$T = 0.575 + 0.388 = 0.963\ \text{ms}$$

$$f \approx 1038\ \text{Hz} \quad \text{(within ±5\% of design target — acceptable)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Tolerance Insight:** Because standard resistors have ±5% (E24) or ±10% (E12) tolerance, the actual output frequency will deviate from the calculated value. For precision timing applications, use **1% metal-film resistors** and **NPO/C0G ceramic capacitors** to minimize temperature drift.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Internal Block Diagram of NE555

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VCC["VCC Pin 8"] --> DIVIDER[Resistor Divider 5k 5k 5k]
    DIVIDER --> REF1["Reference 1 by 3 VCC"]
    DIVIDER --> REF2["Reference 2 by 3 VCC"]
    REF1 --> COMPA[Comparator A Trigger Pin 2]
    REF2 --> COMPB[Comparator B Threshold Pin 6]
    COMPA --> FF[SR Flip Flop]
    COMPB --> FF
    RST[Reset Pin 4] --> FF
    FF --> OUTBUF[Output Buffer]
    OUTBUF --> OUT[Output Pin 3]
    FF --> DISC[Discharge Transistor NPN]
    DISC --> DISCPIN[Discharge Pin 7]
    CV[Control Voltage Pin 5] --> REF2
    GND[GND Pin 1] --> COMPA
```

### 4.2 Functional Block Architecture — Astable Multivibrator

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SUPPLY["VCC Supply 5V to 15V"] --> IC555[NE555 Timer IC]
    RA[Resistor RA] --> NODE1[Junction Pin 7 and Pin 6]
    NODE1 --> RB[Resistor RB]
    RB --> NODE2[Junction Pin 6 Pin 2 and Capacitor Top]
    NODE2 --> IC555
    IC555 --> NODE2
    CAP[Timing Capacitor C] --> GND2[Ground]
    IC555 --> OUTPUT[Output Pin 3 Rectangular Wave]
    IC555 --> DISCHARGE[Discharge Pin 7 Internal Switch]
    DISCHARGE --> RB
```

### 4.3 Functional Block Architecture — Monostable Multivibrator

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SUPPLY["VCC Supply"] --> IC555M[NE555 Timer IC]
    TRIG[External Negative Trigger Pulse] --> PIN2[Pin 2 Trigger Input]
    PIN2 --> IC555M
    R[Timing Resistor R] --> NODE[Node Junction Pin 7 Pin 6]
    NODE --> IC555M
    CM[Timing Capacitor C] --> NODE
    CM --> GNDM[Ground]
    IC555M --> PIN3[Pin 3 Single Output Pulse]
    IC555M --> PIN7[Pin 7 Discharge Switch]
    PIN7 --> R
    RST[Reset Pin 4 Tied to VCC] --> IC555M
```

### 4.4 Waveform Timing Diagram (Astable Mode)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PHASE1[Charging Phase THIGH]
        A1[Capacitor Charges 1 by 3 VCC to 2 by 3 VCC] --> A2[Output HIGH]
    end
    subgraph PHASE2[Discharging Phase TLOW]
        B1[Capacitor Discharges 2 by 3 VCC to 1 by 3 VCC] --> B2[Output LOW]
    end
    PHASE1 --> PHASE2
    PHASE2 --> PHASE1
```

### 4.5 Operating State Transition Flow (Astable)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    START([Power ON]) --> S1[Capacitor Vc less than 1 by 3 VCC]
    S1 --> S2[Trigger Comparator Activated]
    S2 --> S3[Flip Flop SET Output HIGH]
    S3 --> S4[Discharge OFF Capacitor Charges via RA RB]
    S4 --> DECISION1{Vc greater than 2 by 3 VCC?}
    DECISION1 -- No --> S4
    DECISION1 -- Yes --> S5[Threshold Comparator Activated]
    S5 --> S6[Flip Flop RESET Output LOW]
    S6 --> S7[Discharge ON Capacitor Discharges via RB]
    S7 --> DECISION2{Vc less than 1 by 3 VCC?}
    DECISION2 -- No --> S7
    DECISION2 -- Yes --> S3
```

### 4.6 Operating State Transition Flow (Monostable)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    START([Power ON]) --> S1[Capacitor Vc approx 0 Output LOW Stable State]
    S1 --> WAIT{Trigger Pulse at Pin 2 less than 1 by 3 VCC?}
    WAIT -- No --> S1
    WAIT -- Yes --> S2[Flip Flop SET Output HIGH Discharge OFF]
    S2 --> S3[Capacitor Charges via R toward VCC]
    S3 --> CHECK{Vc reaches 2 by 3 VCC?}
    CHECK -- No --> S3
    CHECK -- Yes --> S4[Threshold Comparator Activated]
    S4 --> S5[Flip Flop RESET Output LOW]
    S5 --> S6[Discharge ON Capacitor Rapidly Discharges]
    S6 --> S1
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** Draw the internal block diagram of IC 555 and explain the function of each block.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The NE555 contains the following internal blocks:
