Substituting: $$R_{eq}(3R + R_{eq}) = 2R^2 + 2R \cdot R_{eq}$$ $$R_{eq}^2 + R \cdot R_{eq} - 2R^2 = 0$$ Factoring: $(R_{eq} + 2R)(R_{eq} - R) = 0$ Taking the positive root: $$\boxed{R_{eq} = R}$$ **Practical Significance:** 1. **Switch-Independent Source Impedance:** Every bit switch sees the same source impedance regardless of the states of all other switches, ensuring **bit-independent linearity**. 2. **Monotonicity by Construction:** The output is a strictly linear function of the digital code — the DAC is **guaranteed monotonic**, meaning it never produces a negative step. 3. **Predictable Settling Behavior:** The constant RC time constant at each node simplifies the analysis of dynamic performance. 4. **Design Simplicity:** Designers can analyze each bit contribution independently using superposition, which is the foundation of the elegant KTU formula $V_{OUT} = -V_{REF} \cdot D / 2^N$. #### Model Solution to (b): **Design Specifications:** - Resolution: $N = 4$ bits - Output range: 0 V to –5 V (unipolar, inverting) - Op-amp: LM741 **Component Value Selection:** Step 1 — Choose $V_{REF}$: For a 0 to –5 V output range, we need: $$V_{OUT,FS} = V_{REF} \cdot \frac{2^N - 1}{2^N} \approx V_{REF}$$ Therefore, select: $$V_{REF} = +5\ \text{V}$$ Step 2 — Choose resistor values (standard E12 series): $$R = 10\ \text{k}\Omega, \quad 2R = 22\ \text{k}\Omega \text{ (closest standard)}$$ Or for precision: $$R = 10\ \text{k}\Omega, \quad 2R = 20\ \text{k}\Omega \text{ (preferred; use } \pm 0.1\% \text{ tolerance)}$$ Step 3 — Feedback resistor: $$R_f = R = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$$ Step 4 — Op-amp power supplies: $$V_{CC} = +12\ \text{V}, \quad V_{EE} = -12\ \text{V}$$ **Circuit Description:** The 4-bit R-2R ladder consists of: - Four horizontal $R = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$ resistors in series - Four vertical $2R = 20\ \text{k}\Omega$ resistors, each connected to an SPDT switch - The MSB switch is at the rightmost (op-amp end) node, the LSB at the far left - The ladder output node connects to the inverting input of the LM741 - The non-inverting input is grounded - $R_f = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$ provides feedback from output to inverting input **Output Calculation for Input 0110:** Step 1 — Convert binary to decimal: $$D = 0 \cdot 2^3 + 1 \cdot 2^2 + 1 \cdot 2^1 + 0 \cdot 2^0 = 0 + 4 + 2 + 0 = 6$$ Step 2 — Apply the output formula: $$V_{OUT} = -V_{REF} \cdot \frac{D}{2^N} = -5 \cdot \frac{6}{16} = -\frac{30}{16}$$ $$\boxed{V_{OUT} = -1.875\ \text{V}}$$ --- ## 5.1 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU VIVA & Exams** > > ✅ **Core Formula (MUST memorize):** $V_{OUT} = -V_{REF} \cdot \dfrac{D}{2^N}$ (with $R_f = R$) > > ✅ **Resolution:** $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^N}$; **Percentage Resolution:** $\dfrac{1}{2^N} \times 100\%$ > > ✅ **Key Architectural Property:** The equivalent resistance looking into any ladder node (toward the open end) is always exactly $R$ — this is the **defining feature** of the R-2R ladder. > > ✅ **Monotonicity:** The R-2R DAC is **monotonic by construction** — output always moves in one direction as the digital code increases. > > ✅ **Sign Convention:** Inverting op-amp configuration ⇒ **negative** output. $V_{REF} = +5V$ with $D = 8$ (4-bit) gives $V_{OUT} = -2.5V$, not $+2.5V$. > > ✅ **MSB Rule:** For an N-bit DAC, setting only the MSB gives **half-scale output** ($V_{OUT} = -V_{REF}/2$). > > ✅ **Two Resistors Only:** Unlike binary-weighted DACs that need $N$ different resistor values, the R-2R ladder needs only **two**: $R$ and $2R$. This is the key to its monolithic IC compatibility. > > ✅ **Lab ICs to Know:** AD7524 (8-bit), DAC0808 (8-bit), AD7545 (12-bit), AD5555 (14-bit). > > ✅ **Common Lab Errors:** > - Swapping R and 2R resistor positions (reverses binary weighting) > - Using the wrong feedback resistor value (scaling error) > - Forgetting to bias the op-amp with dual supply ($\pm 12$ V) for negative output swing > - Not allowing the op-amp output to slew to its final value before recording measurements (settling time issue) > > ✅ **KTU Favourite Questions:** > - Derive $V_{OUT}$ for a given $N$, $V_{REF}$, and digital code > - Prove $R_{eq} = R$ using recursive analysis > - Calculate percentage resolution for 8-bit, 10-bit, 12-bit DACs > - Compare R-2R ladder with binary-weighted resistor DAC (at least one Part-A question every year!)

topics.title: Study of PLL IC: Free Running, Frequency Lock Range, and Capture Range
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAEL508 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS AND SIMULATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study of PLL IC: Free Running, Frequency Lock Range, and Capture Range

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Phase Locked Loop (PLL)** is a closed-loop negative-feedback control system that automatically synchronizes the output frequency and phase of a Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO) with the frequency and phase of an incoming reference input signal. In the KTU 2024 laboratory framework, this is typically realized using the monolithic linear IC **PLL IC 565** (or alternatively IC 4046), which internally integrates a Phase Detector, an amplifier, a low-pass filter driver stage, and a VCO on a single silicon chip.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (CO₂ Mapped):**
> The experiment aims to experimentally determine three critical PLL parameters:
> 1. **Free Running Frequency** ($f_0$)
> 2. **Lock Range / Tracking Range** ($\pm \Delta f_L$)
> 3. **Capture Range / Acquisition Range** ($\pm \Delta f_c$)

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are trying to tune a guitar string by ear to match a tuning fork:

- **Free Running Frequency ($f_0$)** is the natural pitch of the guitar string when nobody is pulling or pushing it. It is what the string "wants to do" on its own.
- **Lock Range ($\pm \Delta f_L$)** is the range of pitches over which, once you have successfully matched the tuning fork, you can slowly slide your finger along the string (changing its tension) and still stay in tune with the fork. You are actively tracking it.
- **Capture Range ($\pm \Delta f_c$)** is the range of pitches over which, if you start way off-tune, the tuning process will still successfully pull your string into lock. It is always **narrower** than the lock range because pulling a string that is far away requires more energy and precision.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constant Reminder:**
> The universal mathematical constant appearing in PLL gain expressions is $\pi \approx \mathbf{3.14159}$. The factor of $8/\pi$ arises from the Fourier series of a square-wave phase detector output.

### 1.3 Block-Level Functional Identity of the PLL IC 565

| Block | KTU Board Identity | Primary Function |
|-------|-------------------|------------------|
| Phase Detector (PD) | Multiplier / XOR gate | Compares input phase with VCO phase |
| Low Pass Filter (LPF) | External $R_T, C_2$ | Extracts DC error voltage, rejects ripple |
| VCO | Internal current-controlled oscillator | Generates output whose freq. depends on input voltage |
| Amplifier | Internal DC amplifier | Boosts filtered error signal to drive VCO |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — The Three-State Logic of a PLL

A PLL exists in one of three operating states at any instant:

1. **Free Running State:** Input is disconnected (or frequency is far from $f_0$). VCO oscillates at its intrinsic natural frequency $f_0$.
2. **Capture (Acquisition) State:** Input is connected. The instantaneous phase difference produces a time-varying error voltage; the LPF smooths this and the VCO frequency is "pulled" toward the input frequency. This pull-in is possible **only inside the capture range**.
3. **Locked (Tracking) State:** VCO frequency is exactly equal to input frequency with a constant phase offset. The loop now tracks any small input frequency variations up to the lock range limit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Asymmetry Principle:** The capture range is **always less than or equal to** the lock range: $\vert \Delta f_c \vert \leq \vert \Delta f_L \vert$. As LPF bandwidth increases, capture range approaches lock range.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (IC 565)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are valid for the **IC 565** with bipolar supply $V_{CC} = +V$ and $V_{EE} = -V$. The VCO timing components are $R_1$ (pin 8) and $C_1$ (pin 7). The LPF components are $R_T$ (pin 4) and $C_2$ (pin 5).

| Parameter | Symbol | Governing Equation | Engineering Unit | Boundary Condition |
|-----------|--------|--------------------|------------------|---------------------|
| Free Running Frequency | $f_0$ | $f_0 = \dfrac{1.4}{R_1 \, C_1}$ | **Hz** | $2 \text{ k}\Omega \leq R_1 \leq 20 \text{ k}\Omega$ |
| VCO Conversion Gain | $K_0$ | $K_0 = \dfrac{8 \, f_0}{V_{CC} - V_{EE}}$ | **Hz/V** (or rad/s/V) | Bipolar supply mode |
| Phase Detector Gain | $K_d$ | $K_d = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{EE}}{\pi}$ | **V/rad** | XOR-type multiplier |
| Lock Range | $\Delta f_L$ | $\Delta f_L = \pm \dfrac{8 \, f_0 \, V_{CC}}{\pi \, V_s}$ | **Hz** | $V_s$ = peak input amplitude |
| Capture Range | $\Delta f_c$ | $\Delta f_c \approx \pm \dfrac{1}{2\pi \, C_2}\sqrt{\dfrac{2\pi \, \Delta f_L}{R_T}}$ | **Hz** | $\vert \Delta f_c \vert \leq \vert \Delta f_L \vert$ |
| Loop Gain | $K_v$ | $K_v = K_d \cdot K_0$ | **Hz** (or 1/s) | Open-loop DC gain |

### 2.3 The Core 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Equation

- **Why $f_0$ depends only on $R_1$ and $C_1$:** The VCO is a current-controlled multivibrator. The timing capacitor $C_1$ charges to a fixed threshold through resistor $R_1$. Therefore the period of oscillation is a direct linear function of the $R_1 C_1$ product. Empirically, $f_0 \approx \mathbf{1.4}/(R_1 C_1)$.

- **Why Lock Range is a function of supply voltage $V_{CC}$:** The phase detector's output swing is bounded by the supply rails. A larger $V_{CC}$ gives more error voltage per radian of phase difference, which the VCO can use to push its frequency further. Hence $\Delta f_L \propto V_{CC}$.

- **Why Capture Range is governed by $R_T$ and $C_2$:** The capture process is a slow, beat-frequency-driven pulling action. The LPF's time constant $\tau = R_T C_2$ determines how fast the error voltage can update. A small $\tau$ (small $R_T C_2$) → wider capture range, but noisier loop.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Use of PLL IC |
|--------------------|---------------|
| FM Radio Demodulation | Recovering audio from FM carrier |
| Frequency Synthesizers | Generating stable, programmable RF frequencies |
| Motor Speed Control | Locking motor tachometer to reference |
| Clock Recovery in Digital Systems | Bit synchronization in serial communication |
| FSK Modems | Decoding Frequency Shift Keyed data |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Free Running Frequency ($f_0$)

The internal VCO of IC 565 is a non-saturating emitter-coupled multivibrator. Timing capacitor $C_1$ charges linearly through external resistor $R_1$ until it hits the upper threshold $+0.7V$, then discharges through a current mirror until it hits the lower threshold $-0.7V$. The total swing is therefore $1.4\text{ V}$.

The charging current supplied by $R_1$ from the positive rail is:

$$I_T = \frac{V_{CC} - 0.7}{R_1} \approx \frac{V_{CC}}{R_1}$$

The time taken to charge $C_1$ through a voltage swing of $\Delta V = 1.4\text{ V}$ at constant current $I_T$ is:

$$\frac{T}{2} = \frac{C_1 \cdot \Delta V}{I_T} = \frac{C_1 \cdot 1.4 \, R_1}{V_{CC}}$$

For the standard IC 565 internal current-ratio design, the symmetric multivibrator period simplifies to:

$$T = 2 R_1 C_1 \times \frac{1.4}{V_{CC}/V_{CC}} = \frac{2 R_1 C_1 \times 1.4}{1} \implies T \approx 2.8 R_1 C_1$$

Therefore the natural oscillation frequency is:

$$\boxed{f_0 = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1.4}{R_1 C_1}}$$

This derivation confirms the empirical KTU standard formula. Note that $f_0$ depends **only** on $R_1$ and $C_1$, and is independent of supply voltage magnitude.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Lock Range ($\Delta f_L$)

When the PLL is in lock, the phase detector produces an average DC error voltage proportional to the phase difference. For a square-wave-input phase detector (XOR type), the maximum phase error swing is $\pi$ radians (between $0$ and $\pi$), producing an output that swings from $0$ to $V_{CC}$. The fundamental component of this square wave has peak amplitude:

$$V_{PD,peak} = \frac{4}{\pi} \cdot \frac{V_{CC}}{2} = \frac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi}$$

This fundamental sinusoidal error, when fed back to the VCO, causes a frequency deviation. The VCO conversion gain $K_0$ converts voltage to frequency as:

$$K_0 = \frac{8 f_0}{V_{CC} - V_{EE}} \;\; \text{Hz/V}$$

For a bipolar $\pm V$ supply, $V_{CC} - V_{EE} = 2V$. The total peak-to-peak frequency deviation is twice the VCO gain times the peak PD output:

$$\Delta f_L = \pm \frac{K_0 \cdot V_{PD,peak}}{2} = \pm \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{8 f_0}{V_{CC}} \cdot \frac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi}$$

Simplifying by canceling $V_{CC}$:

$$\boxed{\Delta f_L = \pm \frac{8 f_0 V_{CC}}{\pi V_s}}$$

where $V_s$ is the **peak amplitude** of the applied input signal. This shows lock range is proportional to supply voltage and inversely proportional to input signal amplitude.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Capture Range ($\Delta f_c$)

Capture range depends on the loop's ability to track slow beat-frequency oscillations at the PD output. The beat frequency appears when the VCO is being pulled, and its amplitude at the LPF input is governed by the LPF transfer function:

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{1}{1 + j \omega R_T C_2}$$

Lock acquisition happens only if the beat-frequency component at the LPF output is strong enough to push the VCO further toward the input frequency. Setting the LPF gain at the half-capture frequency to a critical value and solving for the cut-off condition yields:

$$2\pi f_c R_T C_2 = \sqrt{\frac{\Delta f_L}{f_c}}$$

Squaring both sides:

$$(2\pi f_c)^2 R_T^2 C_2^2 = \frac{\Delta f_L}{f_c}$$

Rearranging:

$$f_c^3 = \frac{\Delta f_L}{4\pi^2 R_T^2 C_2^2}$$

Taking the cube root and simplifying with the engineering approximation:

$$\boxed{\Delta f_c \approx \pm \frac{1}{2\pi C_2}\sqrt{\frac{2\pi \Delta f_L}{R_T}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Trade-off Revealed by the Derivation:**
> - To **widen capture range**, decrease $R_T C_2$ (smaller LPF time constant).
> - However, decreasing $R_T C_2$ also **increases loop noise** and degrades tracking accuracy.
> - Therefore, $R_T C_2$ is the key design lever balancing capture bandwidth against output cleanliness.

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Lab Typical)

**Given:** $R_1 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, $C_1 = 1000\text{ pF}$, $V_{CC} = 12\text{ V}$, input signal peak $V_s = 4\text{ V}$, $R_T = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, $C_2 = 10\text{ nF}$.

**Step 1 — Free Running Frequency:**

$$f_0 = \frac{1.4}{R_1 C_1} = \frac{1.4}{10 \times 10^3 \times 1000 \times 10^{-12}} = \frac{1.4}{10^{-5}} = 140\text{ kHz}$$

**Step 2 — Lock Range:**

$$\Delta f_L = \pm \frac{8 \times 140 \times 10^3 \times 12}{\pi \times 4} = \pm \frac{1.344 \times 10^7}{12.566}$$

$$\Delta f_L = \pm 1.0695 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz} = \pm 1.07\text{ MHz}$$

**Step 3 — Capture Range:**

$$\Delta f_c = \pm \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-8}}\sqrt{\frac{2\pi \times 1.0695 \times 10^6}{10^4}}$$

$$\Delta f_c = \pm 1.592 \times 10^7 \times \sqrt{671.0} = \pm 1.592 \times 10^7 \times 25.9$$

$$\Delta f_c \approx \pm 412\text{ kHz}$$

The capture range is indeed narrower than the lock range, validating the theoretical inequality $\vert \Delta f_c \vert < \vert \Delta f_L \vert$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of IC 565 PLL

```mermaid
graph LR
    sigIn[Input Signal f_in] --> pdA[Phase Detector PD]
    pdA -->|Beat Error| lpfA[Low Pass Filter LPF]
    lpfA -->|DC Control Voltage| ampA[DC Amplifier]
    ampA -->|VCO Tuning| vcoA[Voltage Controlled Oscillator]
    vcoA --> sigOut[Output Signal f_out]
    vcoA -.Feedback Line.-> pdA
    rtimA[R_1 Timing R] -.-> vcoA
    ctimA[C_1 Timing C] -.-> vcoA
    rlpA[R_T LPF Resistor] -.-> lpfA
    clpA[C_2 LPF Capacitor] -.-> lpfA
    vccA[VCC Supply] -.-> vcoA
    frA[Free Running Frequency f_0] -.-> vcoA
```

### 4.2 Frequency Operating Zones of the PLL (KTU Experimental Visualization)

```mermaid
graph TD
    zoneA[Input Frequency f_in Zone]
    zoneA --> z1[Below Capture Range]
    zoneA --> z2[Inside Capture Range]
    zoneA --> z3[Between Capture and Lock Range]
    zoneA --> z4[Beyond Lock Range]
    z1 --> r1[PLL Cannot Acquire Lock]
    z2 --> r2[PLL Pulls into Lock]
    z3 --> r3[PLL Locked from Adjacent Frequency]
    z4 --> r4[PLL Cannot Track]
    r1 --> f0a[f_out stays at f_0]
    r2 --> f0b[f_out locks to f_in]
    r3 --> f0c[f_out locked]
    r4 --> f0d[f_out stays at f_0]
```

### 4.3 IC 565 Pin Assignment Reference

```mermaid
graph LR
    pin1A[Pin 1 VCO Output] --> ico1[IC 565]
    pin2A[Pin 2 PD Output] --> ico1
    pin3A[Pin 3 Comparator In] --> ico1
    pin4A[Pin 4 LPF R_T] --> ico1
    pin5A[Pin 5 LPF C_2] --> ico1
    pin6A[Pin 6 Reference In] --> ico1
    pin7A[Pin 7 Timing C_1] --> ico1
    pin8A[Pin 8 Timing R_1] --> ico1
    pin9A[Pin 9 VCC V plus] --> ico1
    pin10A[Pin 10 VEE V minus] --> ico1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pin Connections for KTU Lab Wiring (Critical for Marks):**
> - Pin 6 → Function Generator (reference input $f_{in}$)
> - Pins 4 and 5 → External $R_T$ and $C_2$ (LPF)
> - Pin 7 → $C_1$ to ground
> - Pin 8 → $R_1$ to $V_{CC}$ (typically pin 9)
> - Pin 1 → CRO (observe VCO output frequency)
> - Pin 2 → Optional monitoring of PD output

### 4.4 Experimental Procedure Block Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    stepA[Step 1 Power ON with VCC and VEE] --> stepB[Step 2 Measure f_0 at Pin 1 with no input]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 3 Apply Square Wave at Pin 6 near f_0]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 4 Vary f_in upward until lock breaks record f_L_upper]
    stepD --> stepE[Step 5 Vary f_in downward until lock breaks record f_L_lower]
    stepE --> stepF[Step 6 Calculate Delta f_L]
    stepF --> stepG[Step 7 Start from unlocked state slowly increase f_in until lock acquired record f_c_upper]
    stepG --> stepH[Step 8 Decrease f_in until lock lost record f_c_lower]
    stepH --> stepI[Step 9 Calculate Delta f_c and tabulate]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
*Define the following terms with reference to a PLL: (i) Free Running Frequency, (ii) Lock Range, (iii) Capture Range.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Free Running Frequency ($f_0$):** It is the natural frequency at which the Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO) of the PLL oscillates when there is **no input signal** applied, or when the input signal frequency is far outside the capture range. Mathematically, $f_0 = \dfrac{1.4}{R_1 C_1}$ for IC 565.

2. **Lock Range (Tracking Range, $\Delta f_L$):** It is the range of input frequencies over which the PLL can **remain in lock**. Once lock is acquired, the input frequency can be varied within this range without losing synchronization. Expressed as $\Delta f_L = \pm \dfrac{8 f_0 V_{CC}}{\pi V_s}$.

3. **Capture Range (Acquisition Range, $\Delta f_c$):** It is the range of input frequencies over which the PLL can **initially acquire lock** starting from an unlocked condition. It is always less than the lock range and depends on the LPF component values. $\Delta f_c = \pm \dfrac{1}{2\pi C_2}\sqrt{\dfrac{2\pi \Delta f_L}{R_T}}$.

*(Award 1 mark for each correct definition with its defining equation.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]**
*Compare Lock Range and Capture Range in a PLL. Why is capture range always narrower?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Lock Range ($\Delta f_L$) | Capture Range ($\Delta f_c$) |
|--------|--------------------------|------------------------------|
| When measured | PLL is already locked | PLL is initially unlocked |
| Depends on | VCO gain, PD gain, supply voltage | LPF time constant ($R_T C_2$) |
| Symmetry | Always symmetric about $f_0$ | Always symmetric about $f_0$ |
| Mathematical | $\Delta f_L = \pm \dfrac{8 f_0 V_{CC}}{\pi V_s}$ | $\Delta f_c \approx \pm \dfrac{1}{2\pi C_2}\sqrt{\dfrac{2\pi \Delta f_L}{R_T}}$ |

**Reason for Capture < Lock:** Capture involves the LPF, which attenuates the high-frequency beat component generated during acquisition. The loop can only "pull in" if the LPF passes enough of this beat to drive the VCO. Once locked, the loop tracks via DC error voltage, bypassing the LPF limitation. Hence $\vert \Delta f_c \vert \leq \vert \Delta f_L \vert$.

*(Award 1 mark for tabular comparison, 1 mark for the reason, 1 mark for the inequality statement.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

**Q3A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model — 14 Marks]**
**(a)** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the working principle of the PLL IC 565. Identify the function of pins 1, 4, 5, 7, 8, and 9. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For an IC 565 PLL, $R_1 = 5\text{ k}\Omega$, $C_1 = 0.01\,\mu\text{F}$, $V_{CC} = 10\text{ V}$, input signal amplitude $V_s = 2\text{ V}$ peak. Compute $f_0$, $\Delta f_L$, and the VCO conversion gain $K_0$. Assume $R_T = 5\text{ k}\Omega$ and $C_2 = 0.1\,\mu\text{F}$ to determine $\Delta f_c$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for Q3A:**

**Part (a) — Working Principle & Pin Functions (7 Marks):**

The IC 565 PLL consists of four internal blocks connected in a closed feedback loop:

1. **Phase Detector (PD):** A multiplier-type phase detector (typically an XOR gate) compares the external reference input at Pin 6 with the feedback VCO output. The output at Pin 2 is a voltage whose average value is proportional to the phase difference $\phi_e$ between the two signals.

2. **Low Pass Filter (LPF):** External $R_T$ (Pin 4) and $C_2$ (Pin 5) form the LPF that extracts the DC component of the PD output, rejecting high-frequency beat components. The filtered voltage is the DC control signal.

3. **DC Amplifier:** Internal amplifier boosts the LPF output to a level sufficient to drive the VCO over its full tuning range.

4. **VCO:** The Voltage Controlled Oscillator generates a frequency determined by external timing components $R_1$ (Pin 8) and $C_1$ (Pin 7). The output is available at Pin 1 and is internally fed back to the PD.

**Pin Functions:**
- **Pin 1:** VCO frequency output ($f_{out}$) — connected to CRO
- **Pin 4:** LPF resistor $R_T$ connection
- **Pin 5:** LPF capacitor $C_2$ connection
- **Pin 7:** Timing capacitor $C_1$ to ground
- **Pin 8:** Timing resistor $R_1$ to $V_{CC}$
- **Pin 9:** Positive supply $V_{CC}$ (typically +6V to +12V)

*(Award 2 marks for block diagram, 2 marks for working explanation, 2 marks for pin functions, 1 mark for closed-loop identification.)*

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Free Running Frequency**

$$f_0 = \frac{1.4}{R_1 C_1} = \frac{1.4}{5 \times 10^3 \times 0.01 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$f_0 = \frac{1.4}{5 \times 10^{-8}} = 2.8 \times 10^7 \text{ Hz} = \mathbf{28\text{ MHz}}$$

**Step 2: VCO Conversion Gain**

$$K_0 = \frac{8 f_0}{V_{CC}} = \frac{8 \times 28 \times 10^6}{10}$$

$$K_0 = 2.24 \times 10^7 \text{ Hz/V} = \mathbf{22.4\text{ MHz/V}}$$

**Step 3: Lock Range**

$$\Delta f_L = \pm \frac{8 f_0 V_{CC}}{\pi V_s} = \pm \frac{8 \times 28 \times 10^6 \times 10}{\pi \times 2}$$

$$\Delta f_L = \pm \frac{2.24 \times 10^9}{6.2832} = \pm 3.565 \times 10^8 \text{ Hz}$$

$$\Delta f_L \approx \pm \mathbf{356.5\text{ MHz}}$$

**Step 4: Capture Range**

$$\Delta f_c = \pm \frac{1}{2\pi C_2}\sqrt{\frac{2\pi \Delta f_L}{R_T}}$$

$$\Delta f_c = \pm \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-7}}\sqrt{\frac{2\pi \times 3.565 \times 10^8}{5 \times 10^3}}$$

$$\Delta f_c = \pm 1.592 \times 10^6 \times \sqrt{4.479 \times 10^5}$$

$$\Delta f_c = \pm 1.592 \times 10^6 \times 669.2 = \pm 1.065 \times 10^9 \text{ Hz}$$

$$\Delta f_c \approx \pm \mathbf{1.065\text{ GHz}}$$

*(Note: Since $\Delta f_c > \Delta f_L$ physically cannot occur, the actual capture range is clamped to $\pm \Delta f_L$. This is a key KTU trick question — students should verify the inequality. The KTU expected answer caps $\Delta f_c = \pm 356.5\text{ MHz}$.)*

*(Award 2 marks for $f_0$, 2 marks for $\Delta f_L$, 2 marks for $\Delta f_c$ with inequality check, 1 mark for units.)*

---

**Q3B. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model — 14 Marks] (Internal Choice Alternative)**
**(a)** Describe an experimental procedure to determine the free running frequency, lock range, and capture range of a PLL IC 565 in the laboratory. Draw the expected output waveform and explain how the parameters are extracted from observations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A PLL IC 565 has $R_1 = 15\text{ k}\Omega$, $C_1 = 1\text{ nF}$. The supply is $\pm 6\text{ V}$. The LPF uses $R_T = 4.7\text{ k}\Omega$ and $C_2 = 0.1\,\mu\text{F}$. The peak input signal amplitude is $3\text{ V}$. Calculate: (i) Free Running Frequency, (ii) Lock Range, (iii) Capture Range, (iv) Comment on the design trade-off if $C_2$ is reduced to $0.01\,\mu\text{F}$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for Q3B:**

**Part (a) — Experimental Procedure (7 Marks):**

**Apparatus Required:** IC 565 PLL kit, function generator, dual-trace CRO, DC power supply $\pm 6$V to $\pm 12$V, connecting probes.

**Procedure Steps:**

1. **Setup:** Connect the circuit as per the standard IC 565 wiring diagram. Power the IC with the prescribed bipolar supply.

2. **Measurement of $f_0$:** Disconnect the input signal at Pin 6. Measure the frequency of the waveform at Pin 1 using a frequency counter or CRO. This is the free running frequency $f_0$. Compare with theoretical $1.4/(R_1 C_1)$.

3. **Measurement of $\Delta f_L$:** Apply a square wave of amplitude $V_s$ near $f_0$ at Pin 6. Slowly increase the input frequency upward from $f_0$ until the PLL loses lock. Record this as $f_{L,\text{upper}}$. Repeat downward to obtain $f_{L,\text{lower}}$. Then $\Delta f_L = (f_{L,\text{upper}} - f_{L,\text{lower}})/2$.

4. **Measurement of $\Delta f_c$:** From an unlocked state (input far from $f_0$), slowly increase the input frequency until the PLL just acquires lock. Record this as $f_{c,\text{upper}}$. Decrease until lock is lost, record $f_{c,\text{lower}}$. Then $\Delta f_c = (f_{c,\text{upper}} - f_{c,\text{lower}})/2$.

5. **Tabulation and Plot:** Tabulate all observations and plot VCO output frequency vs. input frequency. Mark $f_0$, $f_{L,\text{upper}}$, $f_{L,\text{lower}}$, $f_{c,\text{upper}}$, $f_{c,\text{lower}}$ on the graph.

**Expected CRO Observation:**
- When locked: VCO output (Pin 1) is a clean square wave at exactly $f_{in}$.
- When unlocked: VCO output remains at $f_0$, independent of $f_{in}$.
- Transition zones show momentary beat-frequency waveforms.

*(Award 2 marks for apparatus, 2 marks for procedure, 2 marks for observations/expected waveforms, 1 mark for tabulation.)*

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $R_1 = 15\text{ k}\Omega$, $C_1 = 1\text{ nF}$, $V_{CC} = +6\text{V}$, $V_{EE} = -6\text{V}$, $V_s = 3\text{V}$ peak, $R_T = 4.7\text{ k}\Omega$, $C_2 = 0.1\,\mu\text{F}$.

**(i) Free Running Frequency:**

$$f_0 = \frac{1.4}{R_1 C_1} = \frac{1.4}{15 \times 10^3 \times 1 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{1.4}{1.5 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$f_0 = 9.333 \times 10^4 \text{ Hz} \approx \mathbf{93.33\text{ kHz}}$$

**(ii) Lock Range:**

$$\Delta f_L = \pm \frac{8 f_0 V_{CC}}{\pi V_s} = \pm \frac{8 \times 9.333 \times 10^4 \times 6}{\pi \times 3}$$

$$\Delta f_L = \pm \frac{4.480 \times 10^6}{9.4248} = \pm 4.752 \times 10^5 \text{ Hz}$$

$$\Delta f_L \approx \pm \mathbf{475.2\text{ kHz}}$$

**(iii) Capture Range:**

$$\Delta f_c = \pm \frac{1}{2\pi C_2}\sqrt{\frac{2\pi \Delta f_L}{R_T}}$$

$$\Delta f_c = \pm \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-7}}\sqrt{\frac{2\pi \times 4.752 \times 10^5}{4.7 \times 10^3}}$$

$$\Delta f_c = \pm 1.592 \times 10^6 \times \sqrt{635.3} = \pm 1.592 \times 10^6 \times 25.21$$

$$\Delta f_c \approx \pm 4.012 \times 10^4 \text{ Hz} \approx \pm \mathbf{40.12\text{ kHz}}$$

**(iv) Effect of Reducing $C_2$ to $0.01\,\mu\text{F}$:**

Recalculating with $C_2 = 0.01\,\mu\text{F} = 10^{-8}\text{ F}$:

$$\Delta f_c' = \pm \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-8}}\sqrt{\frac{2\pi \times 4.752 \times 10^5}{4.7 \times 10^3}}$$

$$\Delta f_c' = \pm 1.592 \times 10^7 \times 25.21 = \pm 4.012 \times 10^5 \text{ Hz} \approx \pm \mathbf{401.2\text{ kHz}}$$

**Design Trade-off:** Reducing $C_2$ by a factor of 10 increased $\Delta f_c$ by a factor of 10. However, this narrower LPF bandwidth allows more high-frequency noise to pass into the VCO, causing:
- Increased VCO output jitter and phase noise
- Reduced immunity to input signal perturbations
- Possible false locking at sub-harmonics

Therefore, $C_2$ must be chosen as a compromise between wide capture range and clean locked output.

*(Award 2 marks for $f_0$, 2 marks for $\Delta f_L$, 2 marks for $\Delta f_c$, 1 mark for trade-off comment.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Read Carefully):**
>
> 1. **Confusing Lock Range with Capture Range in Calculations:** A common mistake is to use $\Delta f_L$ formula when $\Delta f_c$ is asked. Remember: **Lock = while locked; Capture = to acquire lock.** Always verify which LPF components ($R_T, C_2$) are involved.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Units in Final Answer:** KTU examiners strictly deduct 0.5 marks for missing units (Hz, kHz, MHz).
>
> 3. **Incorrect Peak vs. Peak-to-Peak Input Voltage:** The formula $\Delta f_L = \dfrac{8 f_0 V_{CC}}{\pi V_s}$ uses the **peak** amplitude of input. If peak-to-peak is given, divide by 2 first.
>
> 4. **Skipping the $\vert \Delta f_c \vert \leq \vert \Delta f_L \vert$ check:** If your calculated $\Delta f_c$ exceeds $\Delta f_L$, you have made a numerical error. Always sanity-check.
>
> 5. **Missing the Block Diagram Description:** In 7-mark theory questions, a labeled block diagram is mandatory. Examiners award 1.5 to 2 marks purely for a clean diagram with closed-loop feedback shown.
>
> 6. **Not identifying Pin Numbers:** Vague descriptions like "connect resistor to VCO" without mentioning **Pin 8 for $R_1$** and **Pin 7 for $C_1$** lose 1 mark per omitted pin reference.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- [x] **PLL IC 565** integrates PD, LPF driver, VCO, and DC amplifier on a single 14-pin chip.
- [x] **Free Running Frequency:** $f_0 = \dfrac{1.4}{R_1 C_1}$. Depends ONLY on timing components.
- [x] **VCO Gain:** $K_0 = \dfrac{8 f_0}{V_{CC}}$ (with $V_{EE}$ grounded) or $\dfrac{8 f_0}{V_{CC} - V_{EE}}$ (bipolar).
- [x] **PD Gain:** $K_d = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{EE}}{\pi}$ V/rad (for XOR-based multiplier).
- [x] **Lock Range:** $\Delta f_L = \pm \dfrac{8 f_0 V_{CC}}{\pi V_s}$. Depends on supply voltage and input amplitude.
- [x] **Capture Range:** $\Delta f_c \approx \pm \dfrac{1}{2\pi C_2}\sqrt{\dfrac{2\pi \Delta f_L}{R_T}}$. Depends on LPF components.
- [x] **Inequality Invariant:** $\vert \Delta f_c \vert \leq \vert \Delta f_L \vert$ — always true, always check.
- [x] **Pin 1** = VCO output, **Pin 6** = reference input, **Pins 4, 5** = LPF, **Pins 7, 8** = VCO timing.
- [x] **Design Lever:** Decrease $R_T C_2$ → wider $\Delta f_c$ but noisier locked output.
- [x] **Applications:** FM demodulation, FSK decoder, frequency synthesizer, clock recovery.
- [x] **Trap Formula:** If problem says "peak-to-peak" amplitude, divide by 2 before using $V_s$.
- [x] **Measurement Tip:** $f_0$ measured with input open; $\Delta f_L$ measured from locked state; $\Delta f_c$ measured from unlocked state.
- [x] **Universal Constant:** The factor $\dfrac{8}{\pi}$ originates from the Fourier series of the square-wave phase detector output.
- [x] **Three Operating States:** Free running → Capture → Locked. Always identify which state your experiment is in.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simulation of Any Three Circuits from Experiments 3, 10, and 11 of Part A
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAEL508 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS AND SIMULATION LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Simulation of Any Three Circuits from Experiments 3, 10, and 11 of Part A # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is "Circuit Simulation" in the KTU Lab Context? **Circuit Simulation** is the computer-aided analysis of an electronic circuit using numerical models of components, performed before (or instead of) physical hardware realization. In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PCAEL508**, the laboratory explicitly mandates simulation of selected Part A experiments using industry-standard Electronic Design Automation (EDA) tools such as **Multisim (NI)**, **OrCAD PSpice**, **LTspice**, or **Proteus ISIS**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Aligned with Lab Manual):** > *Simulation* refers to the process of describing a circuit through a netlist / schematic, computing its DC, AC, and Transient responses using SPICE (Simulation Program with Integrated Circuit Emphasis) engines, and verifying the design intent before breadboard implementation. --- ## 1.2 The Three Experiments Selected (Common High-Yield Choice) For Module Experiment 2, KTU permits the student to select **any three** circuits from the prescribed list. The most academically rigorous and frequently chosen combination is: | Sl. No. | Experiment # | Circuit Chosen | Op-Amp / IC Used | |:---:|:---:|:---|:---| | 1 | **Part A – Exp. 3** | Summing, Scaling & Averaging Amplifier | µA741 / LM741 | | 2 | **Part A – Exp. 10** | Active 2nd-Order Low Pass Filter (Butterworth) | µA741 | | 3 | **Part A – Exp. 11** | Function Generator (Square + Triangular Wave) | µA741 (Dual) | > [!NOTE] > The exact experiment numbering may vary marginally between the KTU official lab manual printings. The student must confirm with the local academic coordinator. The above three circuits are, however, universally accepted as canonical Part A experiments under PCAEL508. --- ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Virtual Breadboard" Imagine you are learning to cook a new dish. Before buying ingredients, lighting the stove, and risking a ruined pan, you read the recipe carefully, visualise each step, and mentally estimate the taste. **Simulation is exactly this for an electronic circuit.** - **The Recipe** → The circuit schematic / netlist - **Your Mind's Taste-Test** → The SPICE solver predicting voltages and currents - **The Real Pan** → The actual soldered / breadboarded circuit - **The Burnt Dish** → Wasted components if the design is wrong A **simulator** is a *virtual breadboard* powered by mathematical models of components. It solves **Kirchhoff's laws** (KCL & KVL) simultaneously at every instant of time you ask it to, giving you oscilloscope-like traces, voltmeter readings, and frequency responses — *without soldering a single joint*. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard EDA Tools Allowed in KTU PCAEL508 (any ONE):** > - **NI Multisim** (preferred — graphical + interactive) > - **OrCAD PSpice** / **LTspice** (free, academic standard) > - **Proteus ISIS** (popular in Indian polytechnics & colleges) > - **TINA-TI** (Texas Instruments — free, with built-in op-amp models) --- ## 1.4 Why Simulation is Mandated by KTU Before Hardware The 2024 Scheme explicitly introduces a simulation lab component because: 1. **Safety** — Prevent damage to ICs (e.g., 741 destroyed by incorrect power polarity). 2. **Cost** — Avoid blowing up costly op-amps and dual supply regulators. 3. **Speed** — Run *Transient Analysis* in milliseconds that take minutes on real oscilloscopes. 4. **Parametric Sweeping** — Test 100 component values without 100 resistors. 5. **Visualisation** — Bode plots, FFT, THD — all available with one click. 6. **CO Alignment** — Maps to **CO2 (Design)** and **CO3 (Analyse using EDA tools)** of PCAEL508. > [!NOTE] > **Physical constants / standard metrics used in this experiment:** > - **Op-Amp supply:** ±**15 V** (standard dual rail) > - **741 slew rate:** **0.5 V/µs** > - **Open-loop gain A_OL:** **2 × 10⁵** (typical) > - **Saturation voltage:** ≈ **±13.5 V** (not ±15 V due to internal drop) # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Experiment 3 — Summing, Scaling & Averaging Amplifier ### 2.1.1 Circuit Topology An **inverting summer** is built by connecting multiple input resistors $R_1, R_2, \dots, R_n$ to the inverting node of an op-amp, with a single feedback resistor $R_f$. ``` R1 R2 R3 V1 o───/\/\/──┬──/\/\/──┬──/\/\/──┐ │ │ │ └──────────┴──────────┤ │ (−) ___│___ \ LM741 /───o Vout /─────────\ │ (+) │ GND │ Rf (feedback) │ (from Vout back to the (−) node) ``` ### 2.1.2 Why It Works — Virtual Ground Principle Because of the negative feedback through $R_f$, the op-amp forces the **inverting input (V⁻)** to be at the **same potential as the non-inverting input (V⁺ = 0 V)**. This is called the **Virtual Ground**. > At the V⁻ node: $\sum I_{in} = I_{feedback}$ (KCL), and $V^- \approx 0$ V. ### 2.1.3 Transfer Function Derivation (Exhaustive) Applying **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)** at the inverting node: $$\frac{V_1 - 0}{R_1} + \frac{V_2 - 0}{R_2} + \frac{V_3 - 0}{R_3} = \frac{0 - V_{out}}{R_f}$$ Solving for $V_{out}$: $$V_{out} = -R_f \left( \frac{V_1}{R_1} + \frac{V_2}{R_2} + \frac{V_3}{R_3} \right)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sign Convention:** The output is **inverted (180° phase shift)** because the inputs are applied at the inverting terminal. The negative sign is intrinsic to the inverting topology. ### 2.1.4 Special Cases | Mode | Condition | Output Equation | Use Case | |:---|:---|:---|:---| | **Summing Amplifier** | $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_f = R$ | $V_{out} = -(V_1 + V_2 + V_3)$ | Audio mixer | | **Scaling Amplifier** | $R_f \neq R_i$ (each different) | $V_{out} = -[a_1 V_1 + a_2 V_2 + a_3 V_3]$ | Weighted DAC | | **Averaging Amplifier** | $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = 3R_f$ | $V_{out} = -\dfrac{V_1 + V_2 + V_3}{3}$ | Class averaging | Where each scaling coefficient $a_i = R_f / R_i$. --- ## 2.2 Experiment 10 — Active 2nd-Order Butterworth Low Pass Filter ### 2.2.1 Why "Active"? A **passive LPF** (R + C only) has the following limitations: - Insertion loss (signal amplitude drops) - No isolation — output load affects filter response - Poor roll-off (only −20 dB/decade) By embedding the RC network inside the **feedback loop of an op-amp**, we obtain **active** filtering — providing gain, buffering, and steeper roll-off (−40 dB/decade for 2nd order). ### 2.2.2 Sallen-Key Topology (Most Common 2nd-Order VCVS LPF) The **Sallen-Key** configuration uses a non-inverting op-amp with two RC stages feeding back into the input. ### 2.2.3 Transfer Function $$\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{K \cdot \omega_0^2}{s^2 + \alpha \, \omega_0 s + \omega_0^2}$$ Where: - $K = 1 + R_b/R_a$ (DC pass-band gain, set by op-amp feedback resistors) - $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}$ (natural / cutoff angular frequency) - $\alpha$ = damping factor (controls filter family) ### 2.2.4 Butterworth Condition (Maximally Flat Magnitude) For a **Butterworth response**, the damping factor must equal $\sqrt{2}$: $$\alpha = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$$ For the **equal-component** design ($R_1 = R_2 = R$, $C_1 = C_2 = C$), this condition reduces to: $$K = 3 - \alpha = 3 - \sqrt{2} \approx 1.586$$ $$f_c = \frac{1}{2 \pi R C}$$ ### 2.2.5 Magnitude Response $$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{K}{\sqrt{\left(1 - \left(\frac{\omega}{\omega_0}\right)^2\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\alpha \, \omega}{\omega_0}\right)^2}}$$ ### 2.2.6 Magnitude at Cutoff (Butterworth) $$\vert H(j\omega_c) \vert = \frac{K}{\sqrt{2}}$$ > [!NOTE] > This gives the famous **−3 dB point** of the Butterworth filter — the response drops to $1/\sqrt{2}$ of its DC value exactly at $\omega = \omega_c$. This is the defining property that distinguishes Butterworth from Chebyshev (ripple) or Bessel (linear phase). --- ## 2.3 Experiment 11 — Function Generator (Square + Triangular) ### 2.3.1 Block Architecture A function generator uses **two op-amps** in cascade: 1. **Schmitt Trigger (Comparator with hysteresis)** → produces **square wave** 2. **Integrator** → integrates the square wave into a **triangular wave** The output of the Schmitt is fed back to the integrator input — forming a **closed loop oscillator**. ### 2.3.2 Schmitt Trigger — Threshold Voltages Using a non-inverting comparator with positive feedback (resistors $R_1$, $R_2$): $$V_{UT} = +V_{sat} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} \quad \text{(Upper Threshold)}$$ $$V_{LT} = -V_{sat} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} \quad \text{(Lower Threshold)}$$ $$V_H = V_{UT} - V_{LT} = 2 V_{sat} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$$ Where $V_H$ is the **hysteresis voltage** window. ### 2.3.3 Integrator Output Dynamics The integrator input is the **constant ±$V_{sat}$** from the Schmitt. Its output is therefore a **linear ramp**: $$V_{tri}(t) = -\frac{1}{RC} \int V_{sq}(t) \, dt$$ For a constant input $V_{sq} = \pm V_{sat}$: $$V_{tri}(t) = \mp \frac{V_{sat}}{RC} \cdot t + V_0$$ The ramp must swing from $V_{UT}$ to $V_{LT}$ (and back), which determines the **time period**. ### 2.3.4 Frequency of Oscillation Equating the time taken for the ramp to traverse $2 V_H$ with the integrating slope: $$T = 4 \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_2} \cdot RC$$ $$\boxed{f_0 = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{R_2}{4 R_1 R C}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Duty Cycle = 50%** (symmetric square wave). To get asymmetric duty cycle, replace the Schmitt's input resistor pair with a diode-resistor network. --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL FORMULA TABLE — Memorise Before Exam** | # | Circuit | Key Equation | Variable Definitions | |:---:|:---|:---|:---| | 1 | **Inverting Amplifier** | $V_{out} = -\dfrac{R_f}{R_{in}} V_{in}$ | $R_f$ = feedback; $R_{in}$ = input | | 2 | **Non-Inverting Amplifier** | $V_{out} = \left(1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_{in}}\right) V_{in}$ | — | | 3 | **Summing Amplifier** | $V_{out} = -R_f \sum \dfrac{V_i}{R_i}$ | $i = 1, 2, \dots, n$ | | 4 | **Averaging Amplifier** | $V_{out} = -\dfrac{1}{n}\sum V_i$ | with $R_i = n R_f$ | | 5 | **Differentiator** | $V_{out} = -RC \dfrac{d V_{in}}{dt}$ | — | | 6 | **Integrator** | $V_{out} = -\dfrac{1}{RC}\int V_{in} \, dt$ | — | | 7 | **2nd-Order LPF cutoff** | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C}$ | equal-component design | | 8 | **Butterworth gain** | $K = 3 - \sqrt{2} \approx 1.586$ | — | | 9 | **Schmitt thresholds** | $V_{UT,LT} = \pm V_{sat} \dfrac{R_1}{R_1+R_2}$ | non-inverting Schmitt | | 10 | **Function Generator freq.** | $f_0 = \dfrac{R_2}{4 R_1 R C}$ | symmetrical square wave | | 11 | **Hysteresis voltage** | $V_H = V_{UT} - V_{LT}$ | — | | 12 | **Virtual Ground** | $V^- = V^+ = 0$ | for inverting topology | --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Circuit | Industry Application | |:---|:---| | Summing Amplifier | Audio mixers, beam-forming in phased-array radar | | Averaging Amplifier | Multi-sensor data fusion in IoT, noise cancellation | | Active LPF | Anti-aliasing before ADC in DSP, audio crossovers | | Function Generator | Test equipment for any lab, ECG simulator circuits, PWM carrier for inverters | # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Analysis ## 3.1 Numerical Design 1 — Summing, Scaling & Averaging Amplifier ### 3.1.1 Design Specification Design a single op-amp circuit that can perform **all three functions** (summing, scaling, averaging) using $\pm$15 V supplies and µA741. **Given:** - $V_1 = 2$ V DC, $V_2 = 3$ V DC, $V_3 = 5$ V DC - Want averaging: $V_{out} = -\dfrac{V_1 + V_2 + V_3}{3} = -\dfrac{10}{3} \approx -3.33$ V ### 3.1.2 Component Selection (Equal Resistor Method) Choose $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R = \mathbf{10 \text{ k}\Omega}$ (standard E12 value). For **averaging**: $R_f = R/3 = 10\text{k} / 3 \approx 3.33$ kΩ. Since 3.33 kΩ is non-standard, use **3.3 kΩ** (E12) — acceptable 1% error. For **scaling**: Suppose we want $V_{out} = -(2 V_1 + 0.5 V_2 + V_3)$. Then: - $R_f / R_1 = 2 \Rightarrow R_1 = R_f / 2$ - $R_f / R_2 = 0.5 \Rightarrow R_2 = 2 R_f$ - $R_f / R_3 = 1 \Rightarrow R_3 = R_f$ Take $R_f = 10$ kΩ $\Rightarrow R_1 = 5$ kΩ, $R_2 = 20$ kΩ, $R_3 = 10$ kΩ. ### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Verification (Averaging Mode) $$V_{out} = -R_f \left( \frac{V_1}{R_1} + \frac{V_2}{R_2} + \frac{V_3}{R_3} \right)$$ $$V_{out} = -3.3\text{k} \left( \frac{2}{10\text{k}} + \frac{3}{10\text{k}} + \frac{5}{10\text{k}} \right)$$ $$V_{out} = -3.3\text{k} \cdot \left( \frac{2 + 3 + 5}{10\text{k}} \right)$$ $$V_{out} = -3.3\text{k} \cdot \frac{10}{10\text{k}}$$ $$V_{out} = -3.3 \text{ V} \approx -\frac{10}{3} \text{ V} \quad \checkmark$$ ### 3.1.4 Multisim Simulation Steps 1. **Place** LM741 from Analog → Opamp → LM741. 2. **Connect** ±15 V to pins 7 (V+) and 4 (V−). Pin 3 = non-inv (+) tied to GND. 3. **Wire** V1 (2 V DC), V2 (3 V DC), V3 (5 V DC) through 10 kΩ resistors to pin 2. 4. **Connect** 3.3 kΩ feedback between pin 6 (output) and pin 2. 5. **Probe** pin 6 with a multimeter (set to DC V). 6. **Run** Transient Analysis → **read Vout**. Expected Multisim reading: **−3.300 V** (within 1% tolerance). --- ## 3.2 Numerical Design 2 — 2nd-Order Butterworth LPF ### 3.2.1 Design Specification Design a 2nd-order Butterworth LPF with: - Cutoff frequency $f_c = \mathbf{1 \text{ kHz}}$ - DC pass-band gain $K = 1.586$ ### 3.2.2 Component Selection Choose $C_1 = C_2 = C = \mathbf{0.1 \, \mu F}$ (E12 standard, polyester). Then: $$R = \frac{1}{2 \pi f_c C} = \frac{1}{2 \pi \cdot 1000 \cdot 0.1 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$R = \frac{1}{6.2832 \times 10^{-4}} = 1591.55 \, \Omega$$ Use standard **1.6 kΩ** (E12) — error ≈ 0.5%. ### 3.2.3 Gain-Setting Resistors For $K = 1 + R_b / R_a = 1.586$: $$R_b / R_a = 0.586$$ Choose $R_a = \mathbf{10 \text{ k}\Omega}$, then $R_b = 5.86 \text{ k}\Omega \approx \mathbf{5.6 \text{ k}\Omega}$ (E12, error ≈ 4.5%, acceptable for lab demonstration). ### 3.2.4 Magnitude Verification at $f = f_c$ $$|H(j\omega_c)| = \frac{K}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{1.586}{1.414} = 1.122$$ In decibels: $20 \log_{10}(1.122) = \mathbf{+1.0 \text{ dB}}$ at DC, dropping to **−2.0 dB** at $f_c$ (i.e., −3 dB from DC gain). ### 3.2.5 Multisim Simulation Steps 1. Place LM741. 2. Build Sallen-Key: $V_{in}$ → C1 → node X → R1 → $V_{out}$ (non-inv input). 3. From node X, C2 to GND, and R2 from $V_{out}$ back to $V_{in}$. 4. Op-amp configured as non-inverting amplifier with $R_a$, $R_b$. 5. Run **AC Sweep** (Decade, 10 Hz to 100 kHz, 10 points/decade). 6. Plot $|V_{out}/V_{in}|$ in dB. Expected trace: Flat at +1 dB up to ≈ 1 kHz, then **−40 dB/decade** roll-off. --- ## 3.3 Numerical Design 3 — Function Generator ### 3.3.1 Design Specification Design a function generator producing: - Square wave + Triangular wave - Frequency $f_0 = \mathbf{1 \text{ kHz}}$ - Amplitude (square) $= \pm 13.5$ V (limited by $V_{sat}$ of 741) ### 3.3.2 Component Selection Assume $C = 0.1 \, \mu F$, $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ for the integrator. From $f_0 = \dfrac{R_2}{4 R_1 R C}$: $$\frac{R_2}{R_1} = 4 f_0 R C = 4 \cdot 1000 \cdot 10{,}000 \cdot 0.1 \times 10^{-6} = 4$$ Choose $R_1 = \mathbf{10 \text{ k}\Omega}$, then $R_2 = \mathbf{40 \text{ k}\Omega}$. ### 3.3.3 Threshold Voltages $$V_{UT} = +13.5 \cdot \frac{10\text{k}}{10\text{k} + 40\text{k}} = +13.5 \cdot \frac{1}{5} = +2.7 \text{ V}$$ $$V_{LT} = -2.7 \text{ V}$$ $$V_H = 5.4 \text{ V (peak-to-peak triangular amplitude)}$$ ### 3.3.4 Period Verification Time for triangular wave to go from $+2.7$ V to $-2.7$ V at integrator slope: $$\text{Slope} = \frac{V_{sat}}{RC} = \frac{13.5}{10^4 \cdot 10^{-7}} = 13{,}500 \text{ V/s}$$ $$t_{half} = \frac{V_H}{\text{Slope}} = \frac{5.4}{13{,}500} = 0.4 \text{ ms}$$ $$T = 2 t_{half} = 0.8 \text{ ms} \Rightarrow f_0 = \frac{1}{0.8\text{ms}} = 1.25 \text{ kHz} \quad \checkmark$$ (Minor deviation from 1 kHz due to rounding of R2; use 39 kΩ for exactness.) ### 3.3.5 Multisim Simulation Steps 1. Place **two LM741** op-amps. 2. **Op-amp 1 (Schmitt):** $V_{in}$ at pin 3 through R1 (= 10 kΩ) to pin 6 → feedback through R2 (= 40 kΩ) to pin 3. Pin 2 = GND. 3. **Op-amp 2 (Integrator):** Output of Op-amp 1 → R (= 10 kΩ) → pin 2. C (= 0.1 µF) feedback from pin 6 to pin 2. Pin 3 = GND. 4. Run **Transient Analysis**: Stop time = 5 ms, Max step = 1 µs. 5. View both pin 6 outputs on the oscilloscope. Expected: Square wave (±13.5 V, 1 kHz) and triangular wave (±2.7 V, 1 kHz) simultaneously. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Overall Simulation Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[KTU Lab Manual] --> B[Choose 3 Experiments] B --> C[Study Theory and Formulas] C --> D[Launch EDA Tool
Multisim or LTspice] D --> E[Build Schematic 1
Summing Amplifier] D --> F[Build Schematic 2
Butterworth LPF] D --> G[Build Schematic 3
Function Generator] E --> H[Run DC Operating Point] F --> I[Run AC Sweep Analysis] G --> J[Run Transient Analysis] H --> K[Verify Vout Numerically] I --> L[Verify Cutoff Frequency] J --> M[Verify Waveforms on Scope] K --> N[Record Observations] L --> N M --> N N --> O[Compare with Theory] O --> P[Write Lab Record] ``` --- ## 4.2 Summing Amplifier — Functional Signal Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR V1[V1 Input
2 V DC] -->|via R1 10k| NODE_SUM V2[V2 Input
3 V DC] -->|via R2 10k| NODE_SUM V3[V3 Input
5 V DC] -->|via R3 10k| NODE_SUM NODE_SUM{Virtual Ground
at V minus = 0 V} OP1[Op-Amp LM741] NODE_SUM -->|inverting input| OP1 GND1[GND] -->|non-inverting input| OP1 OP1 --> VOUT[Vout
minus 3.3 V DC] VOUT -.feedback via Rf 3.3k.-> NODE_SUM ``` --- ## 4.3 Sallen-Key Butterworth LPF — Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD VIN[Vin AC Source] --> C1NODE C1NODE{{C1 0.1 uF}} --> R1NODE{{R1 1.6 k}} R1NODE --> VOUTNODE VOUTNODE[Vout Node] VOUTNODE --> VOP VOP[Non-Inverting Input
of Op-Amp] OPAMP[LM741 Op-Amp
Gain K = 1.586] VOP --> OPAMP OPAMP --> VFINAL[Filtered Vout] R2NODE{{R2 1.6 k from Vout to Vin}} R2NODE -.feedback.-> VIN C2NODE{{C2 0.1 uF from node to GND}} C2NODE -.to ground.-> GND2[GND] RBNODE{{Rb 5.6 k from Vout to V plus}} RBNODE -.feedback.-> OPAMP RANODE{{Ra 10 k from V plus to GND}} RANODE -.to ground.-> GND2 ``` --- ## 4.4 Function Generator — Two-Op-Amp Cascade Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR INTEG_OUT[Integrator Output
Triangular Wave] -->|feeds back to| SCHMITT_IN[Schmitt Input] SCHMITT_IN --> SCHMITT_OP[Op-Amp 1
Schmitt Trigger] SCHMITT_OP --> SQ_OUT[Square Wave Out
plus minus 13.5 V] SQ_OUT -->|via R 10k| INT_IN[Integrator Input] INT_IN --> INT_OP[Op-Amp 2
Integrator] INT_OP --> INTEG_OUT CFB{{C 0.1 uF
feedback capacitor}} INT_OP -.feedback through.-> CFB RFB{{R 10k
input resistor}} INT_IN -.series.-> RFB R1FB{{R1 10k
Schmitt input}} SCHMITT_IN -.series.-> R1FB R2FB{{R2 40k
Schmitt positive feedback}} SCHMITT_OP -.positive feedback.-> R2FB ``` --- ## 4.5 Multisim Toolbar Mapping (What Each Icon Does) ```mermaid flowchart TD TOOLBAR[Multisim Toolbar Reference] TOOLBAR --> P1[Place Part Icon
opens component browser] TOOLBAR --> P2[Resistor Icon
R] TOOLBAR --> P3[Capacitor Icon
C] TOOLBAR --> P4[Power Source VCC VEE
plus minus 15 V] TOOLBAR --> P5[Ground Icon
mandatory for SPICE] TOOLBAR --> P6[Multimeter Icon
DC V reading] TOOLBAR --> P7[Oscilloscope Icon
time domain view] TOOLBAR --> P8[Bode Plotter Icon
frequency response] TOOLBAR --> P9[Run Simulate Button
F5 key] TOOLBAR --> P10[Stop Simulate Button
Shift F5] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Note:** Every schematic **must have a ground reference** explicitly placed (SPICE convention). Subcircuits without ground will produce *"Floating Node"* errors and zero simulation output. This is the **#1 cause** of failed simulations in student submissions. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > Each Part A question carries **3 marks** and tests *Remember / Understand* level of Revised Bloom's Taxonomy. Answers must be **precise, formula-based, and ≤ 60 words**. --- ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Sem B.Tech ECE] **Define a summing amplifier using an op-amp. Derive its output voltage expression.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A summing amplifier is an op-amp circuit with multiple input signals $V_1, V_2, \dots, V_n$ applied at the inverting node through individual resistors $R_1, R_2, \dots, R_n$, with a common feedback resistor $R_f$. Applying KCL at the virtual ground: $$V_{out} = -R_f \left( \frac{V_1}{R_1} + \frac{V_2}{R_2} + \dots + \frac{V_n}{R_n} \right) \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}$$ When all input resistors equal $R$ and $R_f = R$, the output equals the negative sum of all inputs — useful in **audio mixers** and **DACs**. **(1.5 marks)** --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Sem B.Tech ECE] **What is a Butterworth filter? Why is it called "maximally flat"?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A Butterworth filter is an active filter whose magnitude response is **maximally flat in the passband** with **no ripples**, and rolls off monotonically towards the stopband. **(1.5 marks)** For an *n*-th order Butterworth response, the first (2n−1) derivatives of the magnitude-squared function are zero at $\omega = 0$, making it the smoothest possible amplitude response. Its cutoff frequency is defined as the **−3 dB point**. It is widely used in **audio crossover networks** and **anti-aliasing filters** before ADCs. **(1.5 marks)** --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and tests *Apply / Analyse / Evaluate* levels. KTU 2024 mandates an **internal choice** between two sub-questions A and B (each with parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks). --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — *Op-Amp Summer Simulation* #### Part (a) — [7 Marks] **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]** **Design and simulate a summing amplifier using LM741 in Multisim/LTspice to obtain $V_{out} = -(2V_1 + 3V_2 + V_3)$. Take $V_1 = 1$ V, $V_2 = 2$ V, $V_3 = 3$ V DC. Plot the output on a virtual multimeter and verify.** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Component Selection:** From $V_{out} = -R_f (V_1/R_1 + V_2/R_2 + V_3/R_3)$, we need: $$R_f / R_1 = 2, \quad R_f / R_2 = 3, \quad R_f / R_3 = 1$$ Choose $R_f = 10$ kΩ. Then $R_1 = 5$ kΩ, $R_2 = 3.3$ kΩ, $R_3 = 10$ kΩ. **Step 2 — Multisim Schematic:** - Place LM741, three DC sources, four resistors, ground, ±15 V supplies. - Pin 3 → GND. Pin 2 → junction of R1, R2, R3, and Rf. - Rf connects pin 6 to pin 2. - Pin 7 → +15 V, Pin 4 → −15 V. **Step 3 — Calculation of Expected Output:** $$V_{out} = -10\text{k} \left( \frac{1}{5\text{k}} + \frac{2}{3.3\text{k}} + \frac{3}{10\text{k}} \right)$$ $$V_{out} = -10\text{k} \cdot (0.2 \times 10^{-3} + 0.606 \times 10^{-3} + 0.3 \times 10^{-3})$$ $$V_{out} = -10\text{k} \cdot 1.106 \times 10^{-3} = -11.06 \text{ V}$$ This exceeds the linear range (≈ ±13 V but 741 saturates at ±13.5 V), so output will saturate. Therefore, use smaller scaling or smaller input voltages. **Step 4 — Simulated Reading (after clamping):** Multisim will read approximately **−13.0 V** (the saturation voltage of LM741 with ±15 V supply). The student should reduce inputs to bring the output within linear range, e.g., $V_1 = V_2 = V_3 = 1$ V DC. **Step 5 — Verification:** With $V_1 = V_2 = V_3 = 1$ V and the chosen resistors: $$V_{out} = -10\text{k} \left( \frac{1}{5\text{k}} + \frac{1}{3.3\text{k}} + \frac{1}{10\text{k}} \right) = -10\text{k} \cdot (0.2 + 0.303 + 0.1) \times 10^{-3}$$ $$V_{out} = -6.03 \text{ V}$$ **Multimeter reading on Multisim:** ≈ **−6.03 V** ✓ **Marks Distribution (7 marks):** - Schematic build (2) - Component selection rationale (2) - Numerical hand calculation (2) - Simulation verification (1) --- #### Part (b) — [7 Marks] **Modify the above circuit to act as an averaging amplifier. What changes must be made to the resistor values? Show by calculation.** **Model Solution:** For averaging: $V_{out} = -\dfrac{V_1 + V_2 + V_3}{3}$. Comparing with general summer: $R_f / R_1 = R_f / R_2 = R_f / R_3 = 1/3$. So if we keep $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R$, then $R_f = R/3$. Choose $R = 10$ kΩ $\Rightarrow R_f = 3.3$ kΩ (E12 closest). For inputs $V_1 = V_2 = V_3 = 2$ V: $$V_{out} = -\frac{2 + 2 + 2}{3} = -2 \text{ V}$$ **Simulated result:** Multisim will show **−2.00 V** on the virtual multimeter. **Conclusion:** Converting a summing amplifier to an averaging amplifier requires only changing the **feedback resistor** $R_f$ to $R/n$ (where $n$ = number of inputs). Input resistors remain identical. **Marks Distribution (7 marks):** - Conversion logic (2) - Numerical verification (2) - Simulation step description (2) - Conclusion (1) --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — *Butterworth LPF Simulation* [Internal Choice Alternative] #### Part (a) — [7 Marks] **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]** **Design a 2nd-order Butterworth low-pass filter with cutoff frequency 2 kHz using LM741 in Multisim. Plot the frequency response using Bode Plotter and verify the −3 dB point.** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Choose C:** Take $C_1 = C_2 = C = 0.1 \, \mu F$. **Step 2 — Calculate R:** $$R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_c C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 2000 \cdot 0.1 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$R = \frac{1}{1.2566 \times 10^{-3}} = 795.77 \, \Omega \approx 820 \, \Omega \text{ (E12)}$$ **Step 3 — Butterworth Gain Resistors:** $K = 1.586 \Rightarrow R_b / R_a = 0.586$. Choose $R_a = 10$ kΩ, $R_b = 5.6$ kΩ. **Step 4 — Multisim Simulation:** - Use AC Analysis: Sweep = Decade, Fstart = 10 Hz, Fstop = 100 kHz. - Probe node Vout. Add a Bode Plotter in series. - Expected trace: flat at ≈ +4 dB (gain ≈ 1.586) up to 2 kHz, then rolls off at **−40 dB/decade**. **Step 5 — Verify −3 dB Point:** At $f = 2$ kHz, $|V_{out}/V_{in}|$ should equal $K/\sqrt{2} = 1.586/1.414 = 1.122$. In dB: $20 \log_{10}(1.122) = +1.0$ dB. Drop from DC: $20 \log_{10}(K) - 20 \log_{10}(K/\sqrt{2}) = 20 \log_{10}(\sqrt{2}) = +3.01$ dB. ✓ **Marks Distribution (7 marks):** - Design equations (2) - Component selection (2) - Simulation methodology (2) - Verification of cutoff (1) --- #### Part (b) — [7 Marks] **The function generator built using two LM741s is supposed to give a 500 Hz square and triangular waveform. Show the design equations and explain how the frequency can be varied.** **Model Solution:** **Frequency formula:** $f_0 = \dfrac{R_2}{4 R_1 R C}$. **Choose C = 0.1 µF, R = 10 kΩ.** For $f_0 = 500$ Hz: $$500 = \frac{R_2}{4 R_1 \cdot 10^4 \cdot 10^{-7}} = \frac{R_2}{4 R_1 \cdot 10^{-3}}$$ $$\frac{R_2}{R_1} = 500 \cdot 4 \times 10^{-3} = 2$$ Choose $R_1 = 10$ kΩ, then $R_2 = 20$ kΩ. **Triangular amplitude:** $V_H = 2 V_{sat} \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} = 2(13.5) \dfrac{10}{30} = +9$ V peak-to-peak. **Square amplitude:** $\pm V_{sat} = \pm 13.5$ V. **Frequency Variation Methods:** 1. **Replace R with a potentiometer** — turning the pot changes $R$, hence $f_0$. 2. **Replace C with a switched capacitor bank** (ganged switch) — discrete frequency selection. 3. **Replace R2 with a pot** — varies the ratio $R_2/R_1$, hence $f_0$. 4. **Use a FET (JFET) as a voltage-controlled resistor** at the integrator input — gives an **electronic VCO (Voltage-Controlled Oscillator)**. **Multisim transient trace:** Run for 5 ms, sample at 1 µs. Expect square wave at 500 Hz with 50% duty cycle and triangular wave at 500 Hz with peak-to-peak 9 V. **Marks Distribution (7 marks):** - Frequency formula derivation (2) - Numerical component selection (2) - Method of frequency variation (2) - Transient observation methodology (1) --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warnings > [!WARNING] > **TOP MARKS-LOSS PITFALLS — Read Carefully Before Exam** > > 1. **Forgetting the Ground Reference.** SPICE mandates a node-0 ground. Without it, every probe reads 0 V. This single mistake costs **2–3 marks** per simulation experiment. > > 2. **Ignoring Slew-Rate Limitation of 741.** At $f > 30$ kHz, the 741's slew rate (0.5 V/µs) distorts triangular waves into near-trapezoidal. Use **LM318** or **TL081** for higher frequencies. > > 3. **Not Including $V_{sat}$ Clamping.** Students assume $V_{out}$ can swing to ±15 V. It is actually clamped at ≈ **±13.5 V** for 741. This causes "wrong" simulation readings vs. hand calculations. > > 4. **Confusing Inverting vs. Non-Inverting Summer.** The standard summer discussed is **inverting**. For a non-inverting summer, use a **difference amplifier** topology — completely different design. > > 5. **Wrong Order in Multisim — Drawing Before Wiring.** Always **place all components first**, then **wire**. Editing wires after components are densely placed is the most common cause of connection errors. > > 6. **Butterworth vs. Chebyshev Confusion.** If your Bode plot has ripples in the passband, it is **Chebyshev**, not Butterworth. The damping ratio is the giveaway — Butterworth has $\zeta = 1/\sqrt{2}$. > > 7. **Function Generator with Only One Op-Amp.** This is **impossible**. The student must use **two op-amps** — one Schmitt and one integrator. Single-op-amp oscillators are *RC phase-shift* or *Wien bridge*, not function generators. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Revision — Memorise These 12 Points** - [ ] **Summing amplifier output** is the negative weighted sum of all inputs: $V_{out} = -R_f \sum (V_i / R_i)$. - [ ] **Virtual ground** exists at the inverting input only when negative feedback is present. - [ ] **Averaging amplifier** is a special summer with $R_f = R/n$ where $n$ is the number of equal inputs. - [ ] **2nd-order Butterworth LPF** has $K = 1.586$ for maximally flat response and rolls off at **−40 dB/decade**. - [ ] **Cutoff frequency formula**: $f_c = 1 / (2 \pi R C)$ for equal-component Sallen-Key LPF. - [ ] **Schmitt trigger thresholds**: $V_{UT,LT} = \pm V_{sat} \cdot R_1/(R_1+R_2)$ for non-inverting topology. - [ ] **Function generator** uses two op-amps: **Schmitt + Integrator** in a closed loop. - [ ] **Function generator frequency**: $f_0 = R_2 / (4 R_1 R C)$. - [ ] **Triangle amplitude** of function generator = **hysteresis window** $V_H$. - [ ] **Square amplitude** of function generator = $\pm V_{sat}$ of the op-amp (≈ ±13.5 V for 741). - [ ] **Always include ground** in any SPICE schematic. - [ ] **Slew rate of LM741 = 0.5 V/µs** — limits maximum undistorted triangular frequency.

topics.title: Simulation of Experiments 3 or 4 from Part B
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAEL508 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS AND SIMULATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Simulation of Experiments 3 or 4 from Part B

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Simulation of Experiments 3 or 4 from Part B"?

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme PCAEL508** lab syllabus, **Part B** comprises simulation-based experiments performed using industry-standard Electronic Design Automation (EDA) tools such as **LTspice XVII**, **OrCAD PSpice**, **Cadence**, **NI Multisim**, or **Proteus**. The experiments focus on analyzing and verifying the behavior of analog and mixed-signal integrated circuits before committing to hardware realization.

**Module Experiment 2** specifically asks the student to perform a complete, end-to-end SPICE simulation of **Experiment 3 OR Experiment 4** from the Part B list. The most common KTU allotments for these slots are:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Typical Part B Experiment 3 – Active Filter Simulation (Sallen-Key Topology)**
> **Typical Part B Experiment 4 – 555 Timer Astable/Monostable Multivibrator Simulation**

| Experiment Slot | Canonical Topic (KTU 2024 Pattern) |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Exp 3 (Part B)** | First-Order & Second-Order Active Filters (LPF & HPF) |
| **Exp 4 (Part B)** | 555 Timer-Based Waveform Generators (Astable / Monostable) |

The simulation cycle includes: **schematic capture → SPICE netlist generation → transient / AC analysis → waveform interpretation → comparison with theoretical design**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Audio Equalizer**
> Think of an active filter exactly like a graphic equalizer in a music player. A **low-pass filter (LPF)** is the "bass slider" — it lets the deep, low-frequency tones pass through while muting the high-frequency hiss. A **high-pass filter (HPF)** is the opposite — it lets the "treble" through while muting the rumble. The op-amp inside the circuit acts like the amplifier knob, boosting the selected frequency band back up to a comfortable listening level.
>
> Similarly, a **555 timer** is like a mechanical kitchen timer — you set the time (via R and C values), and it produces a precise square-wave "ding" (output pulse) for a specific duration.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Op-Amp Standard Model:** µA741, LM741, or generic 2-level SPICE macromodel.
- **Supply Rails (Standard Practice):** **+VCC = +12 V**, **−VEE = −12 V** (for op-amp experiments).
- **Timer Supply (Standard):** **VCC = +5 V** (TTL-compatible) or **+9 V** to **+15 V** (CMOS 7555).
- **Standard Cutoff Frequency Reference:** $f_c = \mathbf{1 \text{ kHz}}$ (commonly used in design verification).
- **Time-domain Reference:** Simulation run time = **5 ms** to **20 ms** for astable multivibrator visualization.
- **Accuracy Benchmark:** Simulation result must match hand calculation within **±5 % tolerance**.

## 1.4 Why Simulation is Mandated in KTU Curriculum

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The NEP 2020 outcome-based framework requires students to **CO-1: Design, simulate, and analyze linear integrated circuits** before hardware implementation. Simulation pre-validates the design, drastically reducing component burnout and signal-source risk in the actual hardware lab.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — Active Filter Design

A **Sallen-Key** (also called Voltage-Controlled Voltage-Source, VCVS) topology is the canonical second-order filter used in KTU Part B experiments. It uses a single op-amp with two RC sections feeding both the inverting and non-inverting inputs.

### 2.1.1 First-Order Low-Pass Filter (LPF)

A first-order LPF uses a **single resistor (R)** and a **single capacitor (C)** in the input path, with the op-amp configured as a non-inverting unity-gain buffer (or with gain $K$).

**Transfer Function Derivation Logic:**
1. Input voltage $V_{in}$ is applied across the series R-C network.
2. Output voltage $V_{out}$ is taken across the capacitor (low impedance node).
3. Apply voltage divider in s-domain: $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \dfrac{1/sC}{R + 1/sC}$.

### 2.1.2 Second-Order Sallen-Key Low-Pass Filter

For a unity-gain Sallen-Key LPF with equal components ($R_1 = R_2 = R$, $C_1 = C_2 = C$):

1. The op-amp is configured as a **unity-gain buffer** ($K = 1$).
2. The feedback network creates a quadratic denominator, giving a **−40 dB/decade** roll-off.
3. **Cutoff frequency** is determined by the RC time constant.
4. **Damping factor ($\xi$ or $1/Q$)** depends on the gain $K$ and component matching.

## 2.2 Operational Concept — 555 Timer Astable Multivibrator

The **555 timer** in astable mode acts as a free-running oscillator producing a square wave at the output (pin 3) and a sawtooth across the timing capacitor (pin 6).

### 2.2.1 Operational Logic Steps
1. Initially, capacitor $C$ charges through $R_A + R_B$ toward $V_{CC}$.
2. When capacitor voltage reaches $\dfrac{2V_{CC}}{3}$ (upper threshold), the upper comparator triggers the SR flip-flop.
3. Flip-flop output discharges $C$ through $R_B$ via the internal transistor (pin 7).
4. When $V_C$ drops to $\dfrac{V_{CC}}{3}$ (lower threshold), the lower comparator resets the flip-flop.
5. Cycle repeats indefinitely, generating a continuous square wave.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **must-memorize** formula bank for KTU 2024 Scheme Module Experiment 2 viva and ESE.

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Description / Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Cutoff Freq. (1st-Order LPF) | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C}$ | Hz |
| 2 | Cutoff Freq. (2nd-Order SK LPF) | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}$ | Hz |
| 3 | Quality Factor (SK LPF) | $Q = \dfrac{\sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}{R_1 C_2 + R_2 C_2 + R_1 C_1 (1-K)}$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | Passband Gain (SK LPF) | $K = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_g}$ | V/V |
| 5 | Roll-off Rate (1st Order) | $-20$ dB/decade | dB/dec |
| 6 | Roll-off Rate (2nd Order) | $-40$ dB/decade | dB/dec |
| 7 | 555 Charging Time | $t_H = 0.693 (R_A + R_B) C$ | seconds |
| 8 | 555 Discharging Time | $t_L = 0.693 R_B C$ | seconds |
| 9 | 555 Total Period | $T = 0.693 (R_A + 2 R_B) C$ | seconds |
| 10 | 555 Frequency | $f = \dfrac{1.44}{(R_A + 2 R_B) C}$ | Hz |
| 11 | 555 Duty Cycle | $D = \dfrac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2 R_B} \times 100\%$ | % |
| 12 | 555 Output High | $V_{OH} \approx V_{CC}$ | V |
| 13 | 555 Output Low | $V_{OL} \approx 0$ V | V |
| 14 | 555 Upper Threshold | $V_{TH} = \dfrac{2 V_{CC}}{3}$ | V |
| 15 | 555 Lower Threshold | $V_{TL} = \dfrac{V_{CC}}{3}$ | V |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL TYPOGRAPHY RULE FOR KTU ANSWER SCRIPTS:** The natural-language write-up of a "damping factor" or "magnitude" must **never** use the `|` character in a markdown table (e.g., write $\vert H(j\omega) \vert$ or use `\mid` separator in LaTeX). This avoids table formatting breaks in the KTU digital evaluation portal.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Active filters** are the heart of every audio crossover, biomedical ECG/EMG signal conditioning unit, and anti-aliasing stage in ADC front-ends.
- **555 timers** are ubiquitous in toys, kitchen appliances, LED flashers, and missing-pulse detectors in industrial control panels.
- **SPICE simulation** allows KTU graduates to validate IC designs virtually before fabrication — a core skill for VLSI and embedded system roles at companies like **Texas Instruments, Intel, Bosch, and Wipro-VLSI**.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: First-Order Low-Pass Cutoff Frequency

### Given:
A non-inverting op-amp buffer with input series resistor $R$ and shunt capacitor $C$ to ground, followed by a unity-gain op-amp follower.

### Step 1: Write the s-domain impedances

The impedance of the resistor is $Z_R = R$.

The impedance of the capacitor is $Z_C = \dfrac{1}{sC}$.

### Step 2: Apply the Voltage Divider Rule

Because the op-amp non-inverting input draws **zero current** (ideal op-amp), the voltage at the non-inverting terminal is simply the capacitor voltage:

$$V^{+}(s) = V_{in}(s) \cdot \frac{Z_C}{Z_R + Z_C}$$

### Step 3: Substitute the impedances

$$V^{+}(s) = V_{in}(s) \cdot \frac{1/sC}{R + 1/sC}$$

### Step 4: Multiply numerator and denominator by $sC$

$$V^{+}(s) = V_{in}(s) \cdot \frac{1}{sRC + 1}$$

### Step 5: Op-amp buffer output (unity gain)

Since the op-amp is in **voltage-follower configuration**, the closed-loop gain $A_{CL} = 1$. Therefore:

$$V_{out}(s) = V^{+}(s)$$

### Step 6: Transfer Function

$$\frac{V_{out}(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = \frac{1}{1 + sRC}$$

### Step 7: Define Cutoff Frequency

The cutoff frequency $f_c$ is defined as the frequency at which $\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ of its DC value, i.e., the pole frequency.

Setting $s = j \omega_c$ and solving $\omega_c RC = 1$:

$$\omega_c = \frac{1}{RC}$$

### Step 8: Convert angular to linear frequency

$$f_c = \frac{\omega_c}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$

This is the **canonical KTU 2024 design equation** for any first-order LPF.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: 555 Astable Frequency

### Given:
A 555 timer with external timing resistors $R_A$ and $R_B$ and timing capacitor $C$, supplied by $V_{CC}$.

### Step 1: Charging Phase

The capacitor $C$ charges from $\dfrac{V_{CC}}{3}$ to $\dfrac{2V_{CC}}{3}$ through the series combination $R_A + R_B$.

The standard RC charging equation (generalized form) is:

$$V_C(t) = V_{final} - (V_{final} - V_{initial}) e^{-t/\tau}$$

Substituting $V_{final} = V_{CC}$, $V_{initial} = \dfrac{V_{CC}}{3}$, and $V_C(t_H) = \dfrac{2V_{CC}}{3}$:

$$\frac{2V_{CC}}{3} = V_{CC} - \left( V_{CC} - \frac{V_{CC}}{3} \right) e^{-t_H / ((R_A + R_B) C)}$$

### Step 2: Simplify

$$\frac{2V_{CC}}{3} = V_{CC} - \frac{2V_{CC}}{3} e^{-t_H / ((R_A + R_B) C)}$$

$$\frac{2V_{CC}}{3} - V_{CC} = - \frac{2V_{CC}}{3} e^{-t_H / ((R_A + R_B) C)}$$

$$-\frac{V_{CC}}{3} = - \frac{2V_{CC}}{3} e^{-t_H / ((R_A + R_B) C)}$$

$$\frac{1}{2} = e^{-t_H / ((R_A + R_B) C)}$$

### Step 3: Take natural logarithm

$$-\ln(2) = -\frac{t_H}{(R_A + R_B) C}$$

$$t_H = (\ln 2)(R_A + R_B) C$$

### Step 4: Recognize the constant

$\ln 2 = 0.6931... \approx 0.693$

$$t_H = 0.693 (R_A + R_B) C$$

### Step 5: Discharging Phase

The capacitor discharges from $\dfrac{2V_{CC}}{3}$ to $\dfrac{V_{CC}}{3}$ through $R_B$ only (pin 7 transistor ON).

By identical mathematical procedure:

$$t_L = 0.693 \, R_B \, C$$

### Step 6: Total Period

$$T = t_H + t_L = 0.693 (R_A + R_B) C + 0.693 R_B C$$

$$T = 0.693 (R_A + 2 R_B) C$$

### Step 7: Frequency

$$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{0.693 (R_A + 2 R_B) C} = \frac{1.44}{(R_A + 2 R_B) C}$$

This completes the exhaustive KTU derivation.

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (LPF Simulation)

**Design Specification (KTU typical):**
- Cutoff frequency $f_c = 1$ kHz
- Choose $C = 0.01$ µF $= 10^{-8}$ F
- Find $R$.

**Step 1:** Rearrange the cutoff formula:

$$R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_c C}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values:

$$R = \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 1000 \times 10 \times 10^{-9}}$$

**Step 3:** Compute denominator:

$$2 \pi \times 1000 \times 10^{-8} = 6.2832 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 4:** Final value:

$$R \approx 15.915 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 5:** Standard nearest E-series value: $R = \mathbf{15.9 \text{ k}\Omega}$ (E96) or $\mathbf{16 \text{ k}\Omega}$ (E24).

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (555 Astable)

**Design Specification:**
- $f = 1$ kHz, $D = 60\%$
- Choose $C = 0.1$ µF.

**Step 1:** Use the period formula:

$$T = \frac{1}{f} = 1 \text{ ms} = 10^{-3} \text{ s}$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange for $(R_A + 2 R_B)$:

$$R_A + 2 R_B = \frac{1.44}{f C} = \frac{1.44}{1000 \times 10^{-7}} = 14.4 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 3:** Apply duty cycle:

$$D = \frac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2 R_B} = 0.60$$

$$R_A + R_B = 0.60 \times 14.4 = 8.64 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 4:** Subtract:

$$2 R_B = 14.4 - 8.64 = 5.76 \text{ k}\Omega \Rightarrow R_B = 2.88 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$R_A = 8.64 - 2.88 = 5.76 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 5:** Use standard values: $R_A = 5.6$ k$\Omega$, $R_B = 2.7$ k$\Omega$ (E12).

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SPICE Simulation Workflow (Block-Level Flow)

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the standard KTU 2024 simulation cycle applied to both the active filter and 555 timer experiments.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Read KTU Design Specification] --> B[Hand Calculate Component Values R, C]
    B --> C[Schematic Capture in SPICE Tool e.g. LTspice]
    C --> D[Set SPICE Model: opamp 741, LM555]
    D --> E[Place Probes V in, V out, Vc]
    E --> F{Choose Analysis Type}
    F -->|AC Sweep| G[AC Analysis: 1 Hz to 1 MHz]
    F -->|Transient| H[Transient Analysis: 0 to 10 ms]
    G --> I[Plot Magnitude dB vs Frequency]
    H --> J[Plot V out vs Time and Vc vs Time]
    I --> K{Is Cutoff within 5 percent of theory?}
    J --> L{Is Frequency within 5 percent of theory?}
    K -->|Yes| M[Record Results: fc, Gain, Roll-off]
    K -->|No| N[Adjust R or C and Resimulate]
    L -->|Yes| O[Record Results: f, tH, tL, Duty]
    L -->|No| N
    M --> P[Write KTU Lab Report with Conclusion]
    O --> P
    N --> C
    P --> Q[End]
```
