# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the cutoff frequency of an active low-pass filter. State its formula in terms of R and C.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The cutoff frequency $f_c$ of an active low-pass filter is the frequency at which the magnitude of the transfer function drops to $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ (or $-3$ dB) of its maximum (DC) value. It is also called the **$-3$ dB frequency** or **half-power frequency**. For a first-order Sallen-Key LPF, the formula is:

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi R C}$$

It is measured in **Hz**, and represents the boundary between the passband and stopband of the filter.

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**State any two advantages of simulating a circuit in SPICE before hardware implementation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Zero component damage:** Simulation allows validation of op-amp and 555 timer behavior without risk of burning costly ICs due to design errors such as incorrect biasing or wrong R-C values.
2. **Parametric sweep facility:** A SPICE tool like LTspice can sweep frequency, temperature, and component tolerances in a single run, enabling rapid characterization that would take hours on a hardware breadboard.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**Design and simulate a second-order Sallen-Key low-pass filter with cutoff frequency $f_c = 2$ kHz using the µA741 op-amp in LTspice. (a) Derive the transfer function and design equations. (b) Draw the SPICE schematic, perform an AC analysis from 10 Hz to 1 MHz, and obtain the magnitude response in dB. Verify the simulated $f_c$ with theoretical calculation.**

#### (a) Derivation & Design (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Sallen-Key Transfer Function (2 marks):**
For a unity-gain Sallen-Key LPF with $R_1 = R_2 = R$ and $C_1 = C_2 = C$:

$$\frac{V_{out}(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = \frac{1}{s^2 R^2 C^2 + 2 s R C + 1}$$

**Step 2 — Cutoff Frequency Identification (2 marks):**
The denominator is a standard 2nd-order characteristic polynomial $s^2 + 2\xi \omega_n s + \omega_n^2$. Equating coefficients:

$$\omega_n = \frac{1}{RC}, \quad \xi = 1, \quad Q = \frac{1}{2\xi} = 0.707$$

Hence $f_c = f_n = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi R C}$.

**Step 3 — Component Calculation (3 marks):**
Given $f_c = 2$ kHz, choose $C = 0.01$ µF.

$$R = \frac{1}{2 \pi f_c C} = \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 2000 \times 10^{-8}} \approx 7.96 \text{ k}\Omega$$

Select nearest E12 value: **$R = 8.2$ k$\Omega$**. Predicted $f_c$ recalculated:

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 8200 \times 10^{-8}} \approx 1.94 \text{ kHz}$$

#### (b) Simulation Procedure (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Schematic Capture (2 marks):**
Open LTspice → New Schematic. Place the **UniversalOpamp2** symbol (or µA741 model). Place two resistors $R_1$, $R_2$ each of $8.2$ k$\Omega$, two capacitors $C_1$, $C_2$ each of $0.01$ µF, and a sine source $V_{in}$ (1 V amplitude, 0 phase, AC = 1).

**Step 2 — Netlist Description (2 marks):**

```
V1 in 0 SINE(0 1 1k) AC 1
R1 in mid 8.2k
R2 mid out 8.2k
C1 mid 0 0.01uF
C2 out 0 0.01uF
XU1 mid mid out out UA741
```

**Step 3 — Run AC Analysis (2 marks):**
Simulate → Edit Simulation Cmd → **.ac dec 100 10 1Meg**. Run. Plot $V(out)$ as dB magnitude.

**Step 4 — Result Interpretation (1 mark):**
The plot shows a flat 0 dB passband up to $\sim 1.94$ kHz and a $-40$ dB/decade roll-off above. The simulated $-3$ dB point matches the theoretical $f_c = 1.94$ kHz within **±2 % tolerance**. Report the gain-bandwidth product limitation if observed.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks – Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**Design a 555 timer astable multivibrator to generate a 1 kHz square wave with a duty cycle of 50 %. (a) Derive the design equations for $R_A$, $R_B$, and $C$. (b) Simulate the circuit in Multisim/Proteus, plot the output waveform, and verify $f$ and duty cycle.**

#### (a) Design Equations (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Duty Cycle Constraint (2 marks):**
For $D = 50\%$, $t_H = t_L$. From the 555 equations:

$$t_H = 0.693 (R_A + R_B) C, \quad t_L = 0.693 R_B C$$

Setting $t_H = t_L$ gives $R_A = 0$. To achieve exactly 50 % with a practical circuit, a **diode bypass across $R_B$** is added, yielding the modified design:

$$f = \frac{1.44}{(R_A + 2 R_B) C}, \quad D = 50\%$$

**Step 2 — Choose C (1 mark):**
Pick $C = 0.1$ µF.

**Step 3 — Solve for R values (2 marks):**
$T = 1/f = 1$ ms. $R_A + 2 R_B = \dfrac{1.44}{f C} = \dfrac{1.44}{1000 \times 10^{-7}} = 14.4$ k$\Omega$.

For $D = 50\%$: $R_A = R_B$. Therefore $3 R_B = 14.4$ k$\Omega$, so $R_B = 4.8$ k$\Omega$ and $R_A = 4.8$ k$\Omega$.

**Step 4 — Component Selection (2 marks):**
Use standard E12: **$R_A = R_B = 4.7$ k$\Omega$** with $C = 0.1$ µF. Recompute:

$$f = \frac{1.44}{(4.7\text{k} + 9.4\text{k}) \times 10^{-7}} \approx 1.02 \text{ kHz (within tolerance)}$$

#### (b) Simulation Procedure (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Schematic (2 marks):**
Place **LM555** in Multisim. Connect $V_{CC} = +5$ V at pin 8, GND at pin 1, Reset (pin 4) tied to $V_{CC}$, Control Voltage (pin 5) bypassed with **0.01 µF** to GND, Discharge (pin 7) connected to $R_A = 4.7$ k$\Omega$ → node → $R_B = 4.7$ k$\Omega$ → pin 6/2.

**Step 2 — Capacitor (1 mark):**
Connect timing capacitor $C = 0.1$ µF from pin 6/2 to GND.

**Step 3 — Probes (2 marks):**
Place oscilloscope probe at pin 3 (output) and at pin 6/2 (capacitor waveform). Set run time to **5 ms** with max time step 1 µs.

**Step 4 — Result Verification (2 marks):**
Observe square wave at pin 3 with $V_{OH} \approx 5$ V and $V_{OL} \approx 0$ V. Measure period $T = 0.98$ ms, giving $f = 1.02$ kHz. The capacitor pin 6 waveform is a sawtooth ramping between $1.67$ V ($V_{CC}/3$) and $3.33$ V ($2V_{CC}/3$). Match within **±5 %** of theoretical values.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to add AC = 1 in the sine source** in LTspice — the AC analysis will yield 0 dB everywhere. Marks lost: 2.
> 2. **Confusing $R_A$ and $R_B$ in the 555 astable circuit** — Discharge pin (pin 7) is the one *between* the two resistors, not the one at $V_{CC}$. Marks lost: 1–2.
> 3. **Not adding the 0.01 µF bypass capacitor on pin 5** of the 555 — causes noisy Control Voltage and unstable frequency. Marks lost: 1.
> 4. **Writing duty cycle as a decimal instead of a percentage** in the result table — KTU strict marking deducts ½ mark.
> 5. **Failing to mention the op-amp macromodel used** (e.g., "UniversalOpamp2" vs. "µA741") — KTU expects the **exact SPICE model name** in the report. Marks lost: 1.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU PCAEL508 Module Experiment 2**

- **Definition to memorize:** Cutoff frequency is the $-3$ dB point where $\vert H(j\omega_c) \vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ of DC gain.
- **Single most important formula:** $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ for first-order LPF/HPF.
- **Second-order canonical form:** Sallen-Key unity-gain LPF gives Butterworth response with $Q = 0.707$, $\xi = 1/\sqrt{2}$.
- **Roll-off rule:** First-order = $-20$ dB/dec, second-order = $-40$ dB/dec, beyond $f_c$.
- **555 Timer Golden Equations:** $f = \dfrac{1.44}{(R_A + 2 R_B) C}$; $D = \dfrac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2 R_B} \times 100\%$.
- **555 Internal Thresholds:** $V_{TH} = 2V_{CC}/3$, $V_{TL} = V_{CC}/3$ — must be quoted in the KTU answer.
- **SPICE analysis type rule:** AC sweep (`.ac dec`) for filters; Transient (`.tran`) for 555 oscillators.
- **KTU Tolerance Window:** All simulation vs. theory comparisons must be within **±5 %**.
- **Mandatory report sections:** Title, Aim, Apparatus (tool + version), Theory, Design Calculations, Schematic, Netlist, Waveform/Simulation Plot, Result Table, Inference.
- **Examiner's mantra:** Always quote the **SPICE tool name and version** (e.g., "LTspice XVII" or "OrCAD PSpice 17.4") in the apparatus section.
